Ransomware encrypts all EMR files | 1. Offsite backups, incident response plan | | **Availability** | 2. DDoS attack crashes the hospital portal | 2. CDN, rate limiting, redundant servers | | **Availability** | 3. Power outage in the data center | 3. UPS, diesel generators, multi-region cloud | > **Examiner's Insight:** Notice how a single threat (e.g., ransomware) can breach multiple CIA components. KTU examiners appreciate answers that demonstrate such cross-impact analysis. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating Authentication and Authorization** — These are sequential, not synonymous. AuthN = identity check, AuthZ = permission grant. > 2. **Forgetting Accountability** — Many answers cover only CIA. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes AAA, so missing it costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **No real-world examples** — Examiners reward answers with concrete examples (banking, healthcare, e-commerce). Pure theory without examples scores lower. > 4. **Ignoring the order of operations** — Always present the chain as: Identification → Authentication → Authorization → Action → Accountability. > 5. **Skipping diagrams** — A neat block diagram in 14-mark answers is mandatory. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for clear, labeled diagrams. > 6. **No mention of "Why"** — Don't just define concepts; explain *why* each matters. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes outcome-based learning (CO mapping). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1** - ✅ **CIA Triad** = **Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability** — the three pillars of information security. - ✅ **AAA Support** = **Authentication + Authorization + Accountability** — enable the CIA Triad in real systems. - ✅ **Confidentiality** is achieved through **Encryption, ACLs, and Data Masking**. - ✅ **Integrity** is achieved through **Hashing (SHA-256), MACs, and Digital Signatures**. - ✅ **Availability** is achieved through **Redundancy, Backups, and DDoS Mitigation**. - ✅ **Authentication** uses **factors**: Knowledge (password), Possession (OTP), Inherence (biometric). - ✅ **Authorization** follows the **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** and uses models like **RBAC, ABAC, MAC, DAC**. - ✅ **Accountability** is achieved through **tamper-proof audit logs** and provides **Non-Repudiation**. - ✅ **Sequence to remember:** *Identification → Authentication → Authorization → Action → Accountability*. - ✅ **Common KTU exam keywords:** Disclosure, Modification, Disruption, Identity Proof, Access Rights, Traceability. - ✅ **Risk Formula:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$. - ✅ **Real-world examples to quote:** Banking (fund transfer), Healthcare (EMR), E-commerce (online shopping), University (KTU portal). - ✅ **Trade-off principle:** Strengthening one CIA pillar may weaken another (e.g., heavy encryption ↔ slower access). > **Final KTU Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, structure your response as **Definition → Diagram → Examples → Threats → Controls → Conclusion**. This aligns with KTU's outcome-based evaluation rubric and maximizes your score.

topics.title: Common Threats and Vulnerabilities: Malware (Viruses, Worms, Trojans), Phishing, Social Engineering, Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Common Threats and Vulnerabilities: Malware (Viruses, Worms, Trojans), Phishing, Social Engineering, Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat:** Any circumstance or event with the potential to adversely impact an information system through unauthorized access, destruction, disclosure, modification of data, or denial of service. (NIST SP 800-30 aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability:** A weakness in an information system, internal controls, or implementation that could be exploited or triggered by a threat source. Vulnerabilities are the *gaps*; threats are the *forces* that exploit them.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad (Core Security Model):** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — every threat discussed in this module maps to a violation of at least one pillar of the CIA triad.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **bank vault**.

- A **vulnerability** is an unlocked back door, a thin wall, or a guard who falls asleep.
- A **threat** is a *group of thieves* planning to break in. They have the *capability* and *intent*.
- The **risk** is what happens if the thieves find the unlocked door — money disappears, customer trust shatters, and the bank cannot serve its customers.

In computing, your operating system is the vault, your software is the walls, and the internet is the road the thieves travel on. The threats and vulnerabilities discussed below are the most common "crowbars" used by attackers against this vault.

| Pillar of CIA Triad | What Attackers Want | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Steal your secrets | A thief photocopying your vault's contents |
| **Integrity** | Tamper with your data | A forger altering your bank ledger |
| **Availability** | Shut you down | A burglar blocking the bank's front door |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Which CIA property is violated?", map DoS → Availability, Phishing/Social Engineering → Confidentiality + Integrity, Malware → all three.

---

### 1.3 Quick Taxonomy of the Threats Covered

| Threat | Primary CIA Violation | Human/Technical | Spread Vector |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Integrity, Availability | Technical | Host file (needs human action) |
| **Worm** | Availability, Integrity | Technical | Network (self-propagating) |
| **Trojan** | Confidentiality, Integrity | Technical | Disguised software |
| **Phishing** | Confidentiality | Human + Technical | Email / Web |
| **Social Engineering** | Confidentiality | Primarily Human | Phone, in-person, email |
| **DoS / DDoS** | Availability | Technical | Network flood |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2024 KTU Syllabus Note:** Module 1 requires you to be able to *classify, differentiate, and explain the propagation mechanism* of each threat. Memorize the spread vector column above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Malware (Malicious Software) — Deep Analysis

Malware is an umbrella term for any code intentionally designed to cause damage, gain unauthorized access, or disrupt a system. KTU Module 1 focuses on three classical categories.

#### 2.1.1 Virus

- **Definition:** A malicious program that **attaches itself to a legitimate host file** (executable, document macro, boot sector) and spreads only when the infected host is executed or opened.
- **Operational Logic (Why it works):**
  1. Virus code is dormant until a *trigger event* (date, file open, user action).
  2. Upon trigger, the virus **replicates** by inserting its code into other host files.
  3. The **payload** (destructive part) executes — could be data deletion, corruption, or backdoor installation.
- **Lifecycle Stages:** *Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution (Payload) → Damage.*
- **Real-world utility (for defenders):** Signature-based antivirus uses a hash/fingerprint of the virus code. This is why *polymorphic* viruses mutate their code to evade detection.

#### 2.1.2 Worm

- **Definition:** A **self-replicating, self-propagating** malicious program that spreads across networks **without any human interaction** and without needing a host file.
- **Operational Logic (Why it works):**
  1. Exploits a **network vulnerability** (e.g., unpatched SMB, RPC, buffer overflow).
  2. Scans the network for other vulnerable hosts.
  3. Copies itself to the new victim and repeats.
- **Key difference from Virus:** Worms do *not* need a host file or user action. They are *network-borne*.
- **Famous Example:** *ILOVEYOU* (2000, VBS worm via email), *WannaCry* (2017, SMB exploit).

#### 2.1.3 Trojan (Trojan Horse)

- **Definition:** A malicious program **disguised as legitimate, desirable software**. Unlike viruses/worms, Trojans **do not self-replicate** — they rely on the user being *tricked into installing* them.
- **Operational Logic (Why it works):**
  1. Masquerades as a game, utility, or "cracked" software.
  2. User runs it voluntarily (bypasses suspicion).
  3. Hidden payload opens a **backdoor**, installs a **keylogger**, or **downloads other malware**.
- **Types:** Backdoor Trojans, Banking Trojans (e.g., *Zeus*), RATs (Remote Access Trojans).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's High-Yield Distinction:** The single most asked KTU question in this module is *"Differentiate Virus, Worm, and Trojan."* Use these three axes: **(i) Needs host file?, (ii) Self-replicates?, (iii) Needs human action to spread?**

#### 2.1.4 Malware Comparison Matrix

| Property | Virus | Worm | Trojan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Host file required** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No (but pretends to be legitimate) |
| **Self-replication** | ✅ (via host) | ✅ (autonomous) | ❌ No |
| **Human action to spread** | ✅ Required | ❌ Not required | ✅ Required (user installs) |
| **Network propagation** | ❌ Optional | ✅ Primary mode | ❌ Optional |
| **Primary payload** | Damage, corruption | Bandwidth exhaustion, backdoor | Backdoor, data theft |
| **Example** | *Creeper* (theoretical) | *Code Red*, *WannaCry* | *Zeus*, *Emotet* |

---

### 2.2 Phishing — Deep Analysis

- **Definition:** A **social-engineering attack** delivered via digital communication (email, SMS — *smishing*, voice — *vishing*) where the attacker impersonates a **trusted entity** to trick the victim into revealing sensitive information (credentials, OTPs, credit card numbers).
- **Operational Logic (Why it works):**
  1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker gathers public info (LinkedIn, company website) to craft a believable lure.
  2. **Lure delivery:** Spoofed email appearing to come from a bank, IT admin, or CEO.
  3. **Bait:** Urgency ("Your account will be locked in 24 hours!"), fear, or reward ("You've won a prize!").
  4. **Hook:** Victim clicks a link to a **clone website** with a slightly misspelled URL (`paypa1.com` instead of `paypal.com`).
  5. **Capture:** Credentials entered on the fake site are harvested by the attacker.
- **Variants:**
  - *Spear Phishing:* Targeted at a specific individual or organization.
  - *Whaling:* Targeted at senior executives (the "big fish").
  - *Pharming:* DNS cache poisoning that redirects legitimate URLs to malicious sites.

---

### 2.3 Social Engineering — Deep Analysis

- **Definition:** The **psychological manipulation of people** into performing actions or divulging confidential information. It targets the *human* element, which is statistically the weakest link in cybersecurity.
- **Why it works:** Humans trust authority, fear consequences, seek rewards, and want to be helpful. Attackers exploit these cognitive biases.
- **Common Techniques (Framework: *The 6 Attack Vectors*):**

| Vector | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pretexting** | Inventing a fake scenario to steal info | "Hi, I'm from IT, I need your password to fix a bug." |
| **Baiting** | Leaving infected USB drives or links in tempting places | USB labeled "Salary 2024" left in a parking lot |
| **Tailgating / Piggybacking** | Physically following an authorized person into a restricted area | Holding the door while pretending to have forgotten a badge |
| **Quid Pro Quo** | Offering a service in exchange for information | Fake "free tech support" call asking for credentials |
| **Impersonation** | Pretending to be someone trusted | Calling as a bank representative |
| **Reverse Social Engineering** | Victim is tricked into *initiating* contact with the attacker | Posting a fake IT help number on a forum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defense Principle (from the textbook):** Security is only as strong as the weakest link. Technical controls (firewalls, antivirus) are bypassed if a human willingly hands over credentials. Hence *security awareness training* is a **non-technical control** but is mandated by KTU syllabus.

---

### 2.4 Denial of Service (DoS) & Distributed DoS (DDoS) — Deep Analysis

- **Definition:** An attack that **overwhelms a system, server, or network with traffic or resource requests**, rendering it **unavailable to legitimate users**.
- **CIA Violation:** Primarily **Availability** (the "A" of CIA).
- **DoS vs DDoS:**
  - **DoS:** Single attacker/source.
  - **DDoS:** Multiple compromised systems (a *botnet* of *zombies* / *bots*) controlled by a *bot-herder* via a *Command-and-Control (C&C)* channel.

#### 2.4.1 Classification of DoS / DDoS Attacks

The most widely used classification is the **three-layer OSI-based taxonomy**:

| Layer | Attack Type | Mechanism | Target Resource |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L3 — Network** | ICMP Flood (Ping of Death), Smurf Attack | Sends massive ICMP echo requests | Bandwidth |
| **L4 — Transport** | SYN Flood, UDP Flood | Exhausts connection state tables | Memory / Half-open connections |
| **L7 — Application** | HTTP GET/POST Flood, Slowloris | Sends legitimate-looking but slow requests | Server CPU, DB connections, threads |

#### 2.4.2 SYN Flood — Step-by-Step Mechanism

1. Attacker sends a flood of **TCP SYN** packets with **spoofed source IP addresses**.
2. The victim server responds with **SYN-ACK** to the spoofed IPs (which never reply).
3. The server's **connection table** fills up with *half-open* connections waiting for the final ACK.
4. Legitimate users cannot establish a connection because the table is full.
5. The server's **memory is exhausted**, and the service becomes **unavailable**.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metrics Table

> [!NOTE]
> Although Module 1 is conceptual, the following formulas appear regularly in numerical/analytical KTU questions on DoS/DDoS.

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Attack Bandwidth (B_attack)** | $B_{attack} = N_{bots} \times B_{per\_bot}$ | **bps / Gbps** | Measuring DDoS traffic strength |
| **Botnet Size (N_bots)** | $N_{bots} = \dfrac{B_{attack}}{B_{per\_bot}}$ | Count (bots) | Estimating botnet magnitude |
| **Half-Open Connection Exhaustion** | $T_{conn} = \dfrac{S_{backlog}}{R_{SYN}}$ | Seconds | Time to fill TCP backlog |
| **Packet Rate (P_rate)** | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\Delta t}$ | **pps** (packets per second) | Flood intensity |
| **Bit Rate / Throughput** | $R = \dfrac{P_{size} \times P_{rate}}{8}$ | **bps** | Total bandwidth used |
| **Service Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | **% Uptime** | SLA calculations |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Hours | Reliability metric |
| **Mean Time To Recover (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{repairs}}$ | Hours | Recovery metric |
| **Detection Latency (L)** | $L = T_{detect} - T_{attack\_start}$ | Seconds | Time-to-detect KPI |
| **Attack Severity Score (CVSS-style)** | $S = f(Base, Temporal, Environmental)$ | Score 0–10 | Risk scoring (qualitative) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Markdown Integrity Note:** All vertical bars in math expressions above (e.g., absolute value, conditional probability) are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe structure.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Analysts (Security Operations Centers):** Use these metrics to triage DDoS alerts in SIEM tools (Splunk, QRadar).
- **Network Engineers:** Configure rate-limiting and BGP blackholing based on bandwidth thresholds.
- **Insurance Underwriters:** Use MTBF/MTTR and CVSS scores to price **cyber-insurance premiums**.
- **Compliance:** ISO 27001 and NIST CSF mandate availability metrics reporting (SLA ≥ 99.9%).

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> This module is primarily conceptual, so the "derivations" below are **analytical walkthroughs** of attack propagation logic, throughput calculations, and risk-scoring logic — the kind of structured step-by-step reasoning KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1 — Virus Lifecycle (5-Stage State Machine)

**Premise:** A KTU question may ask: *"Explain the lifecycle of a virus with a neat diagram."* Here is the rigorous state transition:

$$
S_{virus} : D \xrightarrow{\;trigger\;} P \xrightarrow{\;attach\;} T \xrightarrow{\;condition\;} E \xrightarrow{\;payload\;} X
$$

| State | Symbol | Action | Transition Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dormant** | $D$ | Virus is loaded but inactive | Awaiting trigger |
| **Propagation** | $P$ | Inserts copies into host files | File open / write event |
| **Triggering** | $T$ | Looks for activation condition | Date, count, user action |
| **Execution** | $E$ | Payload code runs | Condition met |
| **Damage** | $X$ | Corrupts data, opens backdoor, etc. | End state |

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Dormant ($D$):** After initial infection (e.g., opening an infected email attachment), the virus lies dormant. It may have an internal counter.
2. **Propagation ($P$):** The virus copies itself into other .exe, .doc, or .com files. Each new file becomes a new infection vector.
3. **Triggering ($T$):** A specific logical condition is checked. Examples: `if (date == "April 1")` or `if (infected_count == 5)`.
4. **Execution ($E$):** The payload (destructive code) is delivered. Could be a logic bomb, data wipe, or backdoor installer.
5. **Damage ($X$):** Final state — system compromise achieved.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2 — DDoS Bandwidth Derivation

**Question pattern (KTU 2024-style):** *"A botnet of 5,000 bots each generates 4 Mbps of attack traffic. The target server's link has 10 Gbps capacity. Will the server be overwhelmed?"*

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$$
N_{bots} = 5{,}000 \quad ; \quad B_{per\_bot} = 4 \text{ Mbps} \quad ; \quad B_{link} = 10 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total attack bandwidth.**

$$
B_{attack} = N_{bots} \times B_{per\_bot}
$$

$$
B_{attack} = 5{,}000 \times 4 \text{ Mbps} = 20{,}000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize units (convert Gbps to Mbps).**

$$
B_{link} = 10 \text{ Gbps} \times 1000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare.**

$$
B_{attack} = 20{,}000 \text{ Mbps} \;\gt\; B_{link} = 10{,}000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

$$
\text{Since } B_{attack} > B_{link}, \text{ the link is saturated by a factor of } \dfrac{20{,}000}{10{,}000} = 2\times
$$

**Result:** The server **is overwhelmed** with **2× oversubscription** of its inbound link. The attack is successful.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3 — SYN Flood Backlog Exhaustion Time

**Question pattern:** *"A server has a TCP backlog of 128 half-open connections. An attacker sends 64 SYN packets per second. How long until the backlog is full?"*

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
T_{conn} = \dfrac{S_{backlog}}{R_{SYN}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
T_{conn} = \dfrac{128 \text{ connections}}{64 \text{ SYN/sec}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
T_{conn} = 2 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

In just **2 seconds**, the backlog is full. Legitimate SYN packets will be dropped, and the server appears "hung" to real users. This demonstrates why SYN flood attacks are devastatingly efficient even with modest packet rates.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough 4 — Availability (SLA) Calculation

**Question pattern:** *"A cloud provider guarantees 99.9% uptime. How many minutes of downtime are allowed per month (30 days)?"*

**Step 1 — Total minutes in a 30-day month.**

$$
T_{month} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43{,}200 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Allowed downtime.**

$$
T_{down} = T_{month} \times (1 - A)
$$

$$
T_{down} = 43{,}200 \times (1 - 0.999) = 43{,}200 \times 0.001
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
T_{down} = 43.2 \text{ minutes per month}
$$

**Step 4 — Tier Comparison (Bonus Insight).**

| SLA Tier | Allowed Downtime / Month | Allowed Downtime / Year |
|---|---|---|
| **99%** (Two 9s) | 432.0 min (7.2 hrs) | 87.6 hrs (3.65 days) |
| **99.9%** (Three 9s) | **43.2 min** | 8.76 hrs |
| **99.99%** (Four 9s) | 4.32 min | 52.6 min |
| **99.999%** (Five 9s) | 25.9 sec | 5.26 min |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Linking DoS to SLA / availability metrics shows the examiner you understand the *business impact* of an attack, not just the *technical mechanism*. This often earns the extra 1–2 marks beyond the standard model answer.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough 5 — Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact

**Question pattern:** *"Compute the qualitative risk score for two scenarios and recommend which to mitigate first."*

$$
Risk = Likelihood \times Impact
$$

**Scenario A:** Phishing email to employees (Likelihood: High = 0.9, Impact: Medium = 0.5)

$$
Risk_A = 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45
$$

**Scenario B:** Zero-day worm in server OS (Likelihood: Low = 0.2, Impact: High = 0.9)

$$
Risk_B = 0.2 \times 0.9 = 0.18
$$

**Conclusion:** Mitigate **Scenario A first** because $Risk_A > Risk_B$. This illustrates the classic *risk-management principle*: high-frequency, moderate-impact events are often costlier in aggregate than rare, high-impact ones.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Six Threats

| Threat | Attack Surface | Propagation Method | Primary Defense | Detection Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Local files | User opens infected file | Antivirus, sandboxing | Low–Medium |
| **Worm** | Network services | Self-scan & exploit | Patch management, IDS/IPS | Medium |
| **Trojan** | User trust | User installs disguised app | Application whitelisting, EDR | Medium–High |
| **Phishing** | Human + email | Spoofed email + fake site | Email filtering, DMARC, training | High (evolves) |
| **Social Eng.** | Human psychology | Phone, in-person, email | Security awareness, MFA | Very High |
| **DoS / DDoS** | Network / app layer | Traffic flood | Rate limiting, scrubbing, CDN | Low–Medium (volume-based) |

### 4.2 SYN Flood Attack — Sequence / Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Atk as Attacker
    participant Vct as Victim Server
    participant Spf as Spoofed IP Host
    participant Leg as Legitimate User

    Note over Atk,Spf: TCP Three-Way Handshake Exploitation

    Atk->>Vct: 1. SYN (spoofed source IP)
    Vct->>Spf: 2. SYN-ACK (goes to spoofed IP, never replies)
    Vct-->>Vct: 3. Half-open connection stored in backlog
    Atk->>Vct: 4. Repeat thousands of times
    Vct-->>Vct: 5. Backlog FULL
    Leg->>Vct: 6. Legitimate SYN
    Vct--xLeg: 7. DROPPED (no space in backlog)
    Note over Vct,Leg: Service Unavailable to Real Users
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Virus and Worm:** Students often write "worms need a host file" — *false*. Worms are standalone and self-propagate. This is the #1 mark-loser.
> 2. **Writing only definitions, not propagation logic:** A 7-mark question on "Explain Phishing" needs the *5-step lure→hook→capture* flow, not just "Phishing means fake emails."
> 3. **Forgetting CIA mapping:** When asked "classify the threat," explicitly state which CIA property is violated.
> 4. **DoS vs DDoS confusion:** DoS = single source; DDoS = multiple sources (botnet). Examiners deduct 2 marks if you use them interchangeably.
> 5. **No real-world example:** KTU values application-level answers. Always cite one example (*WannaCry, Zeus, ILOVEYOU*).

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm. State one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| Host file required | Yes — attaches to a host program | No — runs as an independent process |
| Self-replication | Yes, but requires host execution | Yes, autonomously across the network |
| Human action to spread | Required (open file, run program) | Not required (network-borne) |

- **Virus example:** *Creeper* (theoretical, 1971) or *Melissa* (1999).
- **Worm example:** *WannaCry* (2017) or *Code Red* (2001).

> *Distribution: 1 mark for host file distinction, 1 mark for replication distinction, 1 mark for examples.*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is Phishing? List any three social-engineering techniques used in phishing attacks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Phishing** is a social-engineering attack in which an attacker impersonates a trusted entity (bank, employer, IT admin) via email or web to trick victims into revealing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, or OTPs.

**Three techniques:**
1. **Urgency / fear-based lures** — "Your account will be deactivated in 24 hours."
2. **Spoofed sender identity** — Email appears to come from `support@bank.com` but is actually from a lookalike domain.
3. **Clone websites** — Victim clicks a link to a near-identical fake login page.

> *Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for techniques (1 each for any two, full 2 for three).*

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module carries a 14-mark question. You must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Both options are designed to test the same CO at the same Bloom's level (Apply / Analyze).

---

#### QUESTION (Module 1 — 14 Marks) [Internal Choice]

**Option A:**
**(a)** Explain the lifecycle of a computer virus with a neat state-transition diagram. Differentiate between a boot-sector virus, a file-infecting virus, and a macro virus. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** What is a Denial-of-Service attack? Explain the SYN Flood attack in detail with a sequence diagram. Differentiate between DoS and DDoS. **(7 marks)**

---

**Option B:**
**(a)** Define Malware. Compare and contrast Virus, Worm, and Trojan Horse on the basis of (i) host file requirement, (ii) replication mechanism, (iii) propagation vector, and (iv) trigger mechanism. Give one real-world example of each. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Define Social Engineering. Explain any **five** social-engineering attack vectors with suitable examples. Discuss why humans are considered the weakest link in cybersecurity. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.1 Model Solution — Option A

#### Part (a) — Virus Lifecycle & Virus Types [7 marks]

**Lifecycle (5 marks):**

1. **Dormant Phase [1 mark]:** The virus is loaded into memory but is inactive. It is awaiting a trigger event (e.g., a specific date, system clock event, or a count of infected files). The virus may sit dormant for an extended period to evade detection.
2. **Propagation Phase [1 mark]:** The virus locates uninfected host files (executables, documents, boot sectors) and inserts a copy of itself into them. Each newly infected file becomes a new vector for spreading the virus.
3. **Triggering Phase [1 mark]:** The virus checks its activation condition. Common triggers include:
   - A specific date/time (e.g., *Friday the 13th* virus).
   - A count of infected files exceeding a threshold.
   - A user action like opening a specific file.
4. **Execution Phase [1 mark]:** The payload (destructive code) is delivered. This may be a logic bomb, data deletion routine, encryption routine (ransomware precursor), or backdoor installer.
5. **Damage Phase [1 mark]:** The intended impact is realized — corrupted files, stolen credentials, system crash, or persistent backdoor access.

**Types of Viruses (2 marks):**

| Type | Target | Spread Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot-sector virus** | Master Boot Record (MBR) | Infects the system boot sector; activates on system startup from infected disk |
| **File-infecting virus** | .exe, .com, .sys files | Attaches to executable files; runs when file is executed |
| **Macro virus** | Documents with macros (Word, Excel) | Spreads via infected documents using VBA macro language |

---

#### Part (b) — DoS Attack & SYN Flood [7 marks]

**DoS Definition (1 mark):** A Denial-of-Service attack is an attempt to make a machine, network resource, or service unavailable to its intended users by temporarily or permanently disrupting services.

**DoS vs DDoS (2 marks):**

| Property | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Single attacker | Multiple attackers / botnet |
| Volume | Limited by single source bandwidth | Massive (sum of all bots) |
| Mitigation | Easier to block source IP | Harder; distributed sources |
| Tools | Single machine, LOIC | Botnet, Mirai |

**SYN Flood Attack (3 marks) — Step-by-step:**

1. Attacker sends a flood of TCP **SYN** packets to the target server with **spoofed source IP addresses**.
2. Server replies with **SYN-ACK** and allocates a slot in its TCP connection backlog for the half-open connection.
3. Because the source IP is spoofed, the final **ACK** never arrives, and the half-open connection persists.
4. As the attacker repeats this thousands of times per second, the backlog fills up.
5. When the backlog is full, **legitimate SYN packets are dropped** and the server is unable to accept new connections.
6. **Result:** Service becomes unavailable to genuine users.

**Sequence Diagram (1 mark — drawn or described):**
*(See SECTION 4.2 for the full mermaid sequence diagram; in the answer sheet, students should draw the three-way handshake with the spoofed IP and the final dropped legitimate SYN.)*

---

### 5.2 Model Solution — Option B

#### Part (a) — Malware Comparison Table [7 marks]

**Malware Definition (1 mark):**
Malware (Malicious Software) is any program or code intentionally designed to cause damage, gain unauthorized access to, or disrupt a computer system. The three classical categories — virus, worm, and trojan — differ in how they spread and activate.

**Comparison (4 marks — 1 mark per criterion):**

| Criterion | Virus | Worm | Trojan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Host file requirement** | Required — attaches to a legitimate host | Not required — runs standalone | Not required — pretends to be legitimate software |
| **(ii) Replication mechanism** | Inserts itself into host files; spreads when host is run | Self-replicates across the network autonomously | Does not self-replicate |
| **(iii) Propagation vector** | File sharing, email attachments, USB | Network scanning, exploiting vulnerabilities | User downloading disguised software |
| **(iv) Trigger mechanism** | Date, event, user action (often required) | Automatic once vulnerability is found | User must voluntarily install/execute |

**Real-world examples (2 marks — partial credit for any two):**
- **Virus:** *Melissa* (1999), *ILOVEYOU* (2000 — note: this was technically a worm too, can be debated).
- **Worm:** *WannaCry* (2017, exploited SMB vulnerability), *Code Red* (2001, exploited IIS).
- **Trojan:** *Zeus* (banking trojan), *Emotet* (loader trojan), *DarkComet* (RAT).

---

#### Part (b) — Social Engineering [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
Social Engineering is the art of manipulating people psychologically into performing actions or divulging confidential information. It exploits human cognitive biases rather than technical vulnerabilities.

**Five Attack Vectors (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Pretexting** — The attacker fabricates a believable scenario (pretext) to obtain information. *Example:* An attacker calls an employee pretending to be from the IT department, claiming they need the employee's password to "reset" the system.

2. **Baiting** — The attacker leaves malware-infected physical media (USB drives) in locations where victims will find them. *Example:* A USB labeled "Confidential Salary 2024" is left in an office parking lot. An employee plugs it in out of curiosity, and the system is infected.

3. **Tailgating (Piggybacking)** — The attacker physically follows an authorized person into a restricted area. *Example:* An attacker in a suit holds the door behind an employee who has badge access, saying "I forgot my badge, thanks!"

4. **Quid Pro Quo** — The attacker offers a service or favor in exchange for information. *Example:* An attacker calls random office extensions claiming to be from "Tech Support" offering free software installation in exchange for login credentials.

5. **Impersonation** — The attacker pretends to be a trusted authority figure. *Example:* Calling an employee as a "bank representative" and convincing them to share their OTP under the pretext of "verifying a transaction."

**Why Humans are the Weakest Link (1 mark):**
Technical controls like firewalls, antivirus, and encryption can be bypassed if a human willingly hands over credentials, plugs in an unknown USB, or clicks a malicious link. Humans are subject to cognitive biases (trust in authority, urgency, helpfulness) that attackers exploit. Even with all technical defenses in place, **one mistake by one employee** can compromise the entire organization. Therefore, **security awareness training** is a critical non-technical control mandated by frameworks like ISO 27001 and NIST CSF.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision List — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Threat vs Vulnerability:** Threat = the *actor/force*; Vulnerability = the *weakness*. Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact.
- ☐ **CIA Triad:** All six threats in this module violate at least one pillar. Always map your answer.
- ☐ **Virus:** Needs **host file** + human action to spread. Lifecycle: Dormant → Propagation → Trigger → Execute → Damage.
- ☐ **Worm:** **No host file**, **no human action**, self-propagates via network. Example: *WannaCry*.
- ☐ **Trojan:** **Does not self-replicate**, but **user-installed** under disguise. Example: *Zeus*.
- ☐ **Phishing:** Lure → Hook (clone site) → Capture. Variants: Spear, Whaling, Smishing, Vishing, Pharming.
- ☐ **Social Engineering:** Targets **humans**, not machines. Six vectors: Pretexting, Baiting, Tailgating, Quid-Pro-Quo, Impersonation, Reverse Social Engineering.
- ☐ **DoS:** Single-source attack on **availability**. **DDoS:** Multi-source (botnet). Examples: SYN Flood (L4), HTTP Flood (L7), Smurf (L3).
- ☐ **SYN Flood Formula:** $T_{conn} = S_{backlog} / R_{SYN}$. Spoofed IPs exhaust the TCP backlog.
- ☐ **DDoS Bandwidth:** $B_{attack} = N_{bots} \times B_{per\_bot}$. Compare with link capacity to determine success.
- ☐ **SLA Tiers:** 99% = 7.2 hr/month, 99.9% = 43.2 min/month, 99.99% = 4.32 min/month.
- ☐ **Defense Layers:** Patch management + Antivirus + Email filtering + MFA + Security awareness training = layered defense (defense-in-depth).
- ☐ **Exam Tip:** Always cite a **real-world example** and explicitly state the **CIA property violated** in every answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Policies and Risk Management: Developing and Implementing Security Policies, Risk Assessment and Mitigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Policies and Risk Management: Developing and Implementing Security Policies, Risk Assessment and Mitigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Security Policy — Formal Definition

A **Security Policy** is a formal, documented set of rules, principles, and practices that dictate how an organization manages, protects, and distributes its sensitive information and computing assets. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a security policy is the *authoritative foundation* of an organization's security posture — it defines *what* must be protected, *who* can access it, and *how* protection must be enforced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A security policy is not a technical document — it is a *managerial/governance* document. It tells engineers and users what the rules are. The technical mechanisms (firewalls, ACLs, encryption) are the *implementation* of the policy, not the policy itself.

**Three Hierarchical Layers of Security Policy (in order of authority):**

1. **Organizational/Program-level Policy** — The master governance document, set by senior management.
2. **System/Issue-specific Policies** — Address specific areas (e.g., Email Policy, Remote Access Policy, BYOD Policy).
3. **System-specific Rules/Procedures** — The lowest layer, defining exact technical steps (e.g., firewall rule sets).

### 1.2 Risk Management — Formal Definition

**Risk Management** is the continuous, cyclical process of identifying, assessing, prioritizing, mitigating, and monitoring risks to an organization's information assets. The objective is to reduce risk to an *acceptable level* (called *residual risk*) in alignment with the organization's risk appetite and business objectives.

The classical formulation is:

$$ \text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact}) $$

Or more explicitly, in *Annualized Loss Expectancy* terms:

$$ \text{Risk} = \text{Probability of Threat Exploitation} \times \text{Value of Asset} \times \text{Exposure Factor} $$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Vault"**
>
> Imagine you are the owner of a jewellery shop:
>
> - **Security Policy** is the *rulebook* you give to your employees: "No one enters the vault alone", "Customers cannot photograph items above ₹1 lakh", "Cash must be deposited before 6 PM". The rulebook is useless if there are no locks, no guards, and no cameras.
> - **Risk Management** is your *strategic thought process*: you realize the threat is theft, the vulnerability is the back door, and the impact is loss of inventory worth ₹50 lakhs. You then decide: install a steel door (mitigation), buy insurance (transfer), or close the back door entirely (avoidance).
>
> The policy *documents* the rules; the risk management process *decides* the rules. They are two sides of the same coin.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU Examinations

| Term | Standard Meaning | Unit / Quantifier |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset** | Any resource of value (data, hardware, software, people) | Counted in inventory |
| **Threat** | Any potential cause of an unwanted incident | Counted per vector |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited | Counted per asset |
| **Likelihood** | Probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability | Scale 1–5 (Qualitative) |
| **Impact** | Consequence if exploitation succeeds | Scale 1–5 (Qualitative) |
| **Risk** | Function of Threat × Vulnerability × Impact | Categorical: Low / Medium / High |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after controls are applied | Always ≥ 0 |
| **Risk Appetite** | Amount of risk the organization is willing to accept | Stakeholder-defined |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Security Policy Development Lifecycle (SPDL)

A security policy is never written in a single shot. It follows a **5-phase lifecycle**:

#### Phase 1: Policy Planning & Scoping
- Identify the *purpose* of the policy (compliance, protection, governance).
- Identify the *scope* (entire organization, one department, one system).
- Identify *stakeholders* (legal, HR, IT, executive board).
- Identify the *governing standards* (ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, GDPR, PCI-DSS).

#### Phase 2: Policy Drafting
- Use a standardized template (purpose, scope, policy statement, responsibilities, enforcement, review).
- Ensure each policy has **at least 4 mandatory sections**:
  1. **Purpose** — *Why* does this policy exist?
  2. **Scope** — *Who* and *what* does it apply to?
  3. **Policy Statement** — *What* are the rules?
  4. **Enforcement & Compliance** — *What happens* if violated?

#### Phase 3: Policy Review & Approval
- Stakeholder review.
- Legal review (especially for HR-related policies).
- Executive sign-off (CISO + CEO or equivalent).

#### Phase 4: Policy Implementation & Communication
- Distribute to all employees.
- Conduct awareness training.
- Integrate technical controls (firewall, IAM, DLP) to enforce the policy.

#### Phase 5: Policy Maintenance & Review
- Review annually (or after major incident).
- Update for new threats, new regulations, or organizational changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A policy is a *living document*. If a policy has not been reviewed in over 12 months, it is considered *stale* and is a common audit finding.

### 2.2 Types of Security Policies (Mandatory Classification for KTU)

| Policy Type | Purpose | Audience |
|---|---|---|
| **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** | Defines acceptable use of organizational IT assets | All employees |
| **Access Control Policy** | Defines who can access what, when, and how | IT administrators, IAM teams |
| **Information Classification Policy** | Defines data sensitivity levels (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) | All employees handling data |
| **Incident Response Policy** | Defines steps to take when a security incident occurs | SOC, IR team, CISO |
| **Business Continuity / Disaster Recovery (BC/DR) Policy** | Defines continuity of operations after disaster | Executive management, IT ops |
| **Email & Internet Use Policy** | Defines acceptable online behavior | All employees |
| **Remote Access / BYOD Policy** | Defines security for off-site access | Remote workers, IT |
| **Data Retention & Disposal Policy** | Defines lifecycle of data (creation to destruction) | Legal, Records, IT |

### 2.3 The Risk Management Process (NIST SP 800-30 Aligned)

The KTU 2024 syllabus follows the **NIST Risk Management Framework** structure:

```
Step 1: Frame the Risk  →  Define assumptions, constraints, risk tolerance
Step 2: Assess the Risk  →  Identify, analyze, evaluate
Step 3: Respond to Risk  →  Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept
Step 4: Monitor Risk     →  Continuous tracking and review
```

### 2.4 Risk Assessment Methodologies

#### A. Qualitative Risk Assessment
- Uses descriptive scales (Low, Medium, High).
- Easier, faster, no monetary values required.
- **Best for:** KTU exam questions, small to medium organizations.

$$ \text{Risk Level} = \text{Likelihood Score} \times \text{Impact Score} $$

Where both scores are typically on a 1–5 scale, giving a matrix range of 1–25.

#### B. Quantitative Risk Assessment
- Uses **monetary values** (₹, $, €).
- Computes **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** and **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

$$ \text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)} $$

$$ \text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)} $$

$$ \text{Value of Safeguard} = \text{ALE (before)} - \text{ALE (after)} - \text{Annual Cost of Safeguard} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Quantitative risk analysis is used by insurance companies, banks, and critical infrastructure (power grids, hospitals) to justify security investments in board meetings. The ALE/ARO framework allows a CFO to compare security spend against projected loss in rupees.

### 2.5 The Four Risk Response Strategies (Memorize This)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "ATMA" Mnemonic (Kerala-friendly!)** — Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.

| Strategy | Action | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Avoid** | Eliminate the risk entirely by removing the cause | Stop using a vulnerable service |
| **Transfer** | Shift risk to a third party | Buy cyber insurance, outsource to MSSP |
| **Mitigate** | Reduce likelihood or impact by applying controls | Install firewall, enable MFA, train staff |
| **Accept** | Acknowledge the risk and continue (when cost of mitigation > impact) | Accept low-risk vulnerability in a non-critical system |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | When to Use | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ \text{Risk} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact} $ | Qualitative matrix | Scores typically 1–5 each |
| 2 | $ \text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} $ | Loss per single event | In ₹ / $ / € |
| 3 | $ \text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} $ | Annual expected loss | Per year |
| 4 | $ \text{RoI} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control} $ | Justify a control | Positive = worth it |
| 5 | $ \text{Residual Risk} = \text{Inherent Risk} - \text{Control Effectiveness} $ | Post-mitigation assessment | Categorical |
| 6 | $ \text{Risk Appetite Boundary} = \text{Maximum Acceptable Impact} \times \text{Threshold Likelihood} $ | Setting governance limits | Defined by board |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Table-Break Rule Applied:** All vertical bars inside formulas have been replaced with `\\vert` or `\\times` style notation to preserve markdown table rendering integrity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Qualitative Risk Matrix (Common KTU 2-Mark Question)

**Problem Statement:** An organization identifies the following risk scenario:
- **Threat:** Phishing attack
- **Vulnerability:** Employees not trained on email safety
- **Likelihood:** 4 (out of 5) — *Likely*
- **Impact:** 5 (out of 5) — *Severe*

**Step 1 — Identify the risk level using the matrix:**

$$ \text{Risk Score} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values into the equation:**

$$ \text{Risk Score} = 4 \times 5 = 20 $$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result against a 5×5 matrix threshold table (KTU standard):**

| Risk Score Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | Low |
| 5 – 12 | Medium |
| 13 – 25 | High |

**Step 4 — Conclude the risk classification:**

$$ 20 \in [13, 25] \Rightarrow \text{Risk Classification} = \text{HIGH} $$

**Step 5 — Recommend a response strategy:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended Response:** *Mitigate* (highest priority). Implement mandatory security awareness training, deploy anti-phishing email gateway, and enable MFA on all corporate accounts.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Quantitative ALE Computation (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

**Problem Statement:** A KTU-affiliated startup hosts a customer database worth ₹50,00,000 (50 lakhs). Industry data shows the database is breached approximately **once every 4 years** due to SQL injection. Each breach exposes **60%** of the records.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Asset Value (AV)} &= ₹\,50{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Exposure Factor (EF)} &= 0.60 \\
\text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)} &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF} \\
\text{SLE} &= ₹\,50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
\text{SLE} &= ₹\,30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE} &= ₹\,30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 \\
\text{ALE} &= ₹\,7{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Analysis of a Proposed Control:**

The startup is considering a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that:
- Costs ₹2,00,000 per year (subscription, training, maintenance)
- Reduces breach probability to once every **20 years** (ARO = 0.05)
- Reduces the exposure factor to **30%** (EF = 0.30)

**Step 5 — Compute the new SLE and ALE after the control:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= ₹\,50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = ₹\,15{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= ₹\,15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = ₹\,75{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Return on Security Investment (ROSI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control} \\
\text{ROSI} &= ₹\,7{,}50{,}000 - ₹\,75{,}000 - ₹\,2{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ROSI} &= ₹\,4{,}75{,}000 \text{ per year (Positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Engineering Recommendation:**

$$ \text{ROSI} = +₹\,4{,}75{,}000 \Rightarrow \text{The WAF is FINANCIALLY JUSTIFIED} $$

The organization should **mitigate** the risk by purchasing the WAF because the positive ROSI indicates the control saves ₹4,75,000 annually net of its own cost.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Risk Register Construction (Full 14-Mark Walkthrough)

**Problem Statement:** A university IT department is preparing an annual risk register. They have identified 4 assets and need to score each.

| # | Asset | Threat | Likelihood (L) | Impact (I) | Score = L×I |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Student records DB | Ransomware | 3 | 5 | ? |
| A2 | Wi-Fi network | Rogue AP | 4 | 2 | ? |
| A3 | Faculty laptops | Theft | 2 | 4 | ? |
| A4 | Backup tapes | Data leakage | 1 | 3 | ? |

**Step 1 — Compute the missing risk score for A1 (Student records DB):**

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{\text{A1}} = 3 \times 5 = 15 \Rightarrow \text{HIGH}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the missing risk score for A2 (Wi-Fi network):**

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{\text{A2}} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \Rightarrow \text{MEDIUM}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the missing risk score for A3 (Faculty laptops):**

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{\text{A3}} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \Rightarrow \text{MEDIUM}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the missing risk score for A4 (Backup tapes):**

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{\text{A4}} = 1 \times 3 = 3 \Rightarrow \text{LOW}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranked Risk Register:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Priority 1 (HIGH)} &: \text{A1 — Student records DB} \\
\text{Priority 2 (MEDIUM, tied)} &: \text{A2 — Wi-Fi network} \\
\text{Priority 2 (MEDIUM, tied)} &: \text{A3 — Faculty laptops} \\
\text{Priority 4 (LOW)} &: \text{A4 — Backup tapes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the ATMA response strategy:**

- **A1:** Mitigate — deploy immutable backups + EDR + phishing training.
- **A2:** Mitigate — enable 802.1X + WPA3 enterprise authentication.
- **A3:** Transfer + Mitigate — buy laptop insurance + deploy full-disk encryption (BitLocker/FileVault).
- **A4:** Accept — current controls (locked cabinet, transport protocols) are adequate; document decision in register.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Security Policy Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1 Plan and Scope"] --> B["Phase 2 Draft Policy"]
    B --> C["Phase 3 Review and Approve"]
    C --> D["Phase 4 Implement and Train"]
    D --> E["Phase 5 Monitor and Review"]
    E --> A
    E --> F["Policy Retirement if Obsolete"]
    F --> A
    A --> A1["Identify purpose scope and stakeholders"]
    A --> A2["Map to standards ISO27001 NIST"]
    B --> B1["Purpose Scope Statement Enforcement"]
    B --> B2["Use standardized templates"]
    C --> C1["Legal HR and CISO review"]
    C --> C2["Executive sign off"]
    D --> D1["Communicate to all employees"]
    D --> D2["Deploy technical controls"]
    E --> E1["Annual review cycle"]
    E --> E2["Update for new threats"]
```

### 4.2 NIST Risk Management Process (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["STEP 1 Frame the Risk"] --> step2["STEP 2 Assess the Risk"]
    step2 --> step3["STEP 3 Respond to Risk"]
    step3 --> step4["STEP 4 Monitor the Risk"]
    step4 --> step1
    step2 --> sub1["Identify threats and vulnerabilities"]
    step2 --> sub2["Determine likelihood and impact"]
    step2 --> sub3["Compute inherent risk score"]
    step3 --> sub4["AVOID Eliminate cause"]
    step3 --> sub5["TRANSFER Shift to third party"]
    step3 --> sub6["MITIGATE Apply controls"]
    step3 --> sub7["ACCEPT Document and proceed"]
    step4 --> sub8["Continuous monitoring SIEM"]
    step4 --> sub9["Periodic audits and reviews"]
```

### 4.3 Risk Treatment Decision Matrix (5x5 Heat Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Likelihood 1 Rare"] --- I1["Impact 1 Negligible"]
    I1 --- I2["Impact 2 Minor"]
    I2 --- I3["Impact 3 Moderate"]
    I3 --- I4["Impact 4 Major"]
    I4 --- I5["Impact 5 Severe"]
    L2["Likelihood 2 Unlikely"] --- Z1["LOW"]
    L3["Likelihood 3 Possible"] --- Z2["MEDIUM"]
    L4["Likelihood 4 Likely"] --- Z3["HIGH"]
    L5["Likelihood 5 Almost Certain"] --- Z4["CRITICAL"]
    Z1 --- act1["Accept or Monitor"]
    Z2 --- act2["Mitigate with standard controls"]
    Z3 --- act3["Mitigate urgently with priority controls"]
    Z4 --- act4["Avoid Transfer or Escalate to Board"]
```

### 4.4 Policy Hierarchy (Tree Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top["Master Information Security Policy"] --> p1["Acceptable Use Policy"]
    top --> p2["Access Control Policy"]
    top --> p3["Data Classification Policy"]
    top --> p4["Incident Response Policy"]
    top --> p5["BC DR Policy"]
    top --> p6["Network Security Policy"]
    p2 --> p2a["Password Policy"]
    p2 --> p2b["Privileged Access Policy"]
    p2 --> p2c["Remote Access Policy"]
    p3 --> p3a["Data Retention Policy"]
    p3 --> p3b["Data Disposal Policy"]
    p4 --> p4a["Escalation Procedure"]
    p4 --> p4b["Forensics Procedure"]
    p4 --> p4c["Communication Plan"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> Define a Security Policy. List any **four** essential components of a well-written security policy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Security Policy** is a formal, documented set of rules and procedures that govern the protection of an organization's information assets.

**Four Essential Components:**
1. **Purpose** — The reason the policy exists (e.g., to protect customer PII).
2. **Scope** — The people, systems, and data the policy applies to.
3. **Policy Statement** — The specific, enforceable rules and expected behavior.
4. **Enforcement & Compliance** — The consequences for non-compliance and the audit mechanism.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
> Differentiate between **qualitative** and **quantitative** risk assessment. Give one merit of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Descriptive (Low/Medium/High) | Numerical (₹, $) |
| **Merit** | Quick, low-cost, no precise data needed (2 marks) | Provides financial justification for management (2 marks) |
| **Limitation** | Subjective | Requires accurate historical data |

(Any 1 valid distinction + 1 merit = 3 marks as per KTU key pattern.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 1, Set A]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Security Policy Development Lifecycle (SPDL)** in detail with all its phases. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the **four risk response strategies** (Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept) with one real-world example for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Security Policy Development Lifecycle has **5 phases**:

1. **Phase 1 — Policy Planning & Scoping (1.5 marks):**
   - Identify the *purpose* and *scope* of the policy.
   - Identify *stakeholders* (CISO, legal, HR, IT).
   - Map the policy to *governance standards* (ISO 27001, NIST, GDPR).

2. **Phase 2 — Policy Drafting (1.5 marks):**
   - Use a standardized template.
   - Include the four mandatory sections: Purpose, Scope, Policy Statement, Enforcement.
   - Ensure language is clear, unambiguous, and enforceable.

3. **Phase 3 — Review & Approval (1 mark):**
   - Stakeholder review, legal review, executive sign-off.

4. **Phase 4 — Implementation & Communication (1.5 marks):**
   - Distribute the policy to all employees.
   - Conduct awareness training.
   - Deploy technical controls (firewall, IAM, DLP) to enforce the policy.

5. **Phase 5 — Maintenance & Review (1.5 marks):**
   - Annual review.
   - Update for new threats, new regulations, or business changes.
   - Retire the policy if it becomes obsolete.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

| # | Strategy | Explanation | Real-World Example | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Avoid** | Eliminate the risk by removing the cause | A bank stops offering online wire transfers to retail customers after a wave of BEC fraud | 1.75 |
| 2 | **Transfer** | Shift the financial impact to a third party | An e-commerce firm purchases cyber-liability insurance to cover breach costs | 1.75 |
| 3 | **Mitigate** | Reduce likelihood or impact using controls | Enabling MFA on all admin accounts to reduce account takeover risk | 1.75 |
| 4 | **Accept** | Document the risk and proceed when mitigation cost > impact | Accepting the risk of a legacy printer in a non-networked pantry | 1.75 |

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 1, Set B]**
> **(a)** A company hosts an e-commerce database worth **₹80,00,000**. The estimated probability of breach is **once every 5 years**, and each breach exposes **40%** of the records. Compute the **SLE** and **ALE**. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** If a control reduces the breach probability to **once every 25 years** and the exposure factor to **10%**, and the control costs **₹1,50,000 per year**, determine the **ROSI** and recommend whether the control should be adopted. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AV} &= ₹\,80{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{EF} &= 0.40 \\
\text{ARO} &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SLE (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF} \\
&= ₹\,80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 \\
&= ₹\,32{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
&= ₹\,32{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 \\
&= ₹\,6{,}40{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify new values (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EF}_{\text{after}} &= 0.10 \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} &= \frac{1}{25} = 0.04 \\
\text{Cost of Control} &= ₹\,1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute new SLE and ALE (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= ₹\,80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = ₹\,8{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= ₹\,8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 = ₹\,32{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ROSI (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control} \\
&= ₹\,6{,}40{,}000 - ₹\,32{,}000 - ₹\,1{,}50{,}000 \\
&= ₹\,4{,}58{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation (1 mark):**

$$ \text{ROSI} = +₹\,4{,}58{,}000 \Rightarrow \text{Control is JUSTIFIED. Adopt it.} $$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Risk" with "Threat" or "Vulnerability":** Risk is the *combination* of threat × vulnerability × impact. A threat alone is NOT a risk. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in ALE:** Always write "₹ … per year" or "per annum". ALE is an *annual* figure. Marks are deducted if the unit is missing.
> 3. **Reversing SLE and ALE formula:** SLE is computed *first*, then ALE. Many students write ALE = AV × EF × ARO directly, which is mathematically correct but shows no working. KTU examiners award **step marks** only when SLE is computed first.
> 4. **Policy without Scope:** Writing a policy without explicitly stating "Scope" is considered incomplete. **1 mark is deducted** in policy-based questions.
> 5. **Mitigate vs. Transfer confusion:** Insurance is *Transfer*, not *Mitigate*. Installing a firewall is *Mitigate*. This is a very common KTU trap — **2 marks lost** if swapped.
> 6. **Not stating Residual Risk:** After applying a control, mention the *residual risk* explicitly. Examiners reward this as a marker of conceptual depth.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Cheat Sheet**

- A **Security Policy** is a *documented governance tool*, not a technical configuration.
- A policy is built using a **5-phase lifecycle**: Plan → Draft → Approve → Implement → Review.
- **8 mandatory policy types** you must remember: AUP, Access Control, Data Classification, Incident Response, BC/DR, Email/Internet, Remote Access, Data Retention.
- **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** (qualitative form).
- **SLE = AV × EF** (single-event loss).
- **ALE = SLE × ARO** (annual loss).
- **ROSI = ALE(before) − ALE(after) − Cost of Control**. Positive ROSI = control justified.
- **4 Risk Response Strategies (ATMA):** Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
- **Qualitative Risk** = subjective, categorical (Low/Med/High) — fast & cheap.
- **Quantitative Risk** = objective, monetary (₹, $) — accurate but data-hungry.
- A **Risk Register** lists assets, threats, likelihood, impact, score, and chosen response.
- **Residual Risk** is the risk that *remains* after applying controls — never zero in practice.
- **Risk Appetite** is the board-level decision of *how much* risk is acceptable.
- A **policy must be reviewed at least annually** to remain valid.
- **NIST Risk Management Framework:** Frame → Assess → Respond → Monitor (loops continuously).
- The **5×5 risk matrix** is the standard KTU exam grid (score 1–25; thresholds 1–4 / 5–12 / 13–25).
- **Insider threats are typically Likelihood-High × Impact-High** — they require both technical and HR controls.
- Always conclude a risk-based question with a **clear engineering recommendation** and the **strategy name** (avoid/transfer/mitigate/accept) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts and Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts and Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Basic Concepts and Terminology" in Security?

In KTU's **PECDT412 — Foundations of Security in Computing**, Module 2 lays the *linguistic and conceptual foundation* of the entire subject. Before a student can study cryptographic algorithms, access control models, or network firewalls, they must master the **universe of discourse** of information security — the precise meaning of words like *threat*, *vulnerability*, *risk*, *attack*, *asset*, and *control*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (NIST SP 800-12 / Stallings-Cryptography aligned):**
> **Computer Security** is the protection afforded to an automated information system to preserve the **availability**, **integrity**, and **confidentiality** of information system resources (hardware, software, firmware, information/data, and telecommunications).

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — The Bank Vault

Think of information security like protecting a **bank vault** that holds gold bars (your *data*):

| Bank Vault Element | Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The gold bars inside | **Asset** (data, hardware, software) |
| The locked door, the cameras, the guard | **Controls / Countermeasures** |
| A burglar who plans to rob the bank | **Threat agent / Attacker** |
| The unlocked back window | **Vulnerability / Weakness** |
| The actual act of breaking in | **Attack** |
| The chance the bank loses gold this year | **Risk** |
| The bank's promise not to deny your deposit | **Non-repudiation** |
| The CCTV footage proving who entered | **Accountability** |

If you confuse "threat" with "attack" or "vulnerability" with "risk", you cannot design proper defenses — just as a bank that fixes windows (vulnerability) but ignores burglars (threat) is still unsafe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module-2 syllabus for *Foundations of Security in Computing* explicitly requires understanding the **CIA Triad**, **X.800 Security Architecture**, and the **taxonomy of security attacks and services**. Master these terms — they reappear in every later module.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars — The CIA Triad

The cornerstone of every security textbook and every KTU question paper is the **CIA Triad** (also called the *Information Security Triad* or *AIC Triad*).

1. **Confidentiality** — *Preserving authorized restrictions on access and disclosure.*
   - *Plain English:* Only the right people are allowed to read the data. Strangers must not.
   - *Bank analogy:* Only the account holder can see their balance.

2. **Integrity** — *Guarding against improper modification or destruction of information.*
   - *Plain English:* Data must not be silently changed, tampered with, or corrupted. Any change must be authorized and detectable.
   - *Bank analogy:* The ledger cannot be altered without an authorized transaction record.

3. **Availability** — *Ensuring timely and reliable access to and use of information.*
   - *Plain English:* Authorized users must get the data when they need it, without unreasonable delay.
   - *Bank analogy:* The ATM must dispense cash when a customer swipes their card on a working day.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beyond CIA — Two Extra Pillars the KTU Module Emphasises:**
> - **Authenticity** — The property of being genuine and verifiable (proof of identity of sender and source).
> - **Accountability** — The property that actions of an entity can be traced uniquely to that entity (audit trails, logs).
>
> These two extend the CIA Triad into what some texts call the **Five Pillars of Information Security** or the *Parkerian Hexad* (which adds *Possession*, *Utility*, and *Authenticity* to CIA).

### 1.4 The Seven Foundational Terms (Definitions You Must Memorise)

| # | Term | KTU Board Definition (memorise verbatim) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Asset** | A resource of value that an organisation wishes to protect (data, hardware, software, people, reputation). |
| 2 | **Threat** | Any potential occurrence, malicious or otherwise, that could damage an asset. A threat is a *capability, intent, or event* — not yet realised. |
| 3 | **Vulnerability** | A weakness in an information system that could be exploited or triggered by a threat source. |
| 4 | **Attack** | An *actual* attempt to exploit a vulnerability to cause harm. Threats *theorise*; attacks *materialise*. |
| 5 | **Risk** | The *likelihood* that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the *impact* of such exploitation. |
| 6 | **Control (Countermeasure / Safeguard)** | A safeguard prescribed to protect an asset, address a threat, or close a vulnerability, and thereby reduce risk. |
| 7 | **Security Policy** | A set of rules and practices that specify how an organisation manages and protects its information assets. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the CIA Triad Mechanically

The three pillars are not independent. A failure in one often cascades into the others.

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality — the Mechanism

Confidentiality is achieved by **preventing unauthorised disclosure** of sensitive information. The operational mechanisms that enforce it are:

- **Data classification & labelling** — assigning sensitivity levels (e.g., Public → Internal → Confidential → Restricted).
- **Access control** — only authorised principals read the resource.
- **Encryption** — ciphertext is unintelligible to the interceptor.
- **Steganography** — hiding the very *existence* of the message.
- **Physical security** — locked server rooms, CCTV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two commonly-confused violations of confidentiality:**
> - *Disclosure* — someone reads data they should not.
> - *Exposure* — data is released to the public sphere without authorisation.
> Both violate confidentiality; *exposure* is a superset of *disclosure*.

### 2.1.2 Integrity — the Mechanism

Integrity protects two sub-properties:
1. **Data integrity** — the content of the data has not been altered in an unauthorised way.
2. **System integrity** — the system performs its intended functions without unauthorised manipulation.

Mechanisms:
- **Hashing** (e.g., SHA-256) — produces a fixed-length digest; any bit-change in the input produces a totally different digest.
- **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** — keyed hash that proves both integrity and authenticity.
- **Digital signatures** — asymmetric-key equivalent of MAC.
- **Version control & audit logs** — record every modification.
- **Checksums / CRCs** — detect bit-errors in transit.

### 2.1.3 Availability — the Mechanism

Availability is the guarantee that the system *responds* to legitimate requests within an acceptable timeframe. Common threats to availability:

- **Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack** — floods the system with junk requests.
- **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** — same as DoS but from many bot machines.
- **Ransomware** — encrypts data and locks the user out.
- **Hardware failure / natural disaster.**

Mechanisms to defend availability:
- **Redundancy** — duplicate systems (RAID, hot standbys, multi-site replication).
- **Failover clustering**.
- **Backups** (the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite).
- **Load balancers & Content Delivery Networks (CDN)**.
- **Disaster Recovery (DR) & Business Continuity Plans (BCP).**

## 2.2 OSI Security Architecture (ITU-T X.800) — The KTU High-Yield Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the **X.800 standard** which defines three key categories:

### A. Security Attacks
- **Passive Attacks** — eavesdropping, monitoring, traffic analysis. *Goal: learn the information, not modify it. Hard to detect, easy to prevent.*
- **Active Attacks** — masquerading, replay, modification of messages, denial of service. *Goal: alter resources / affect operation. Easy to detect, hard to prevent.*

### B. Security Services (X.800 defines 5 + 4 specifics)
A *security service* is a *processing or communication service* provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection.

| Service (X.800) | Protects which pillar? | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Confidentiality** | Confidentiality | Hides content from eavesdroppers |
| **Data Integrity** | Integrity | Detects tampering |
| **Authentication** | Authenticity | Verifies identity of peer |
| **Non-repudiation** | Accountability | Sender cannot deny sending |
| **Access Control** | All three | Mediates who can do what |
| (plus) Availability, Data-Origin Authentication, etc. | | |

### C. Security Mechanisms
A *security mechanism* is the *means* (cryptographic, physical, procedural) used to implement one or more services. Examples: encipherment, digital signature, access control, data integrity, authentication exchange, traffic padding, routing control, notarisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU exam loves asking:** *"Distinguish between a Security Service and a Security Mechanism."*
> **Stock answer:** A service specifies *what* protection is required (the goal). A mechanism specifies *how* that protection is achieved (the tool).

## 2.3 Passive vs Active Attacks — The High-Yield Table

| Property | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Learn / observe information | Alter / disrupt / fabricate |
| **Detection** | Difficult (no modification) | Easy (system behaves anomalously) |
| **Prevention** | Easy (encrypt) | Difficult (must watch all traffic) |
| **Examples** | Eavesdropping, Traffic Analysis | Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS |
| **Effect on CIA** | Breaches Confidentiality | Breaches Integrity & Availability |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Risk and Cost-Benefit Mathematics

These three equations are tested repeatedly in KTU 14-mark problems. Memorise them.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Net Benefit} = (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{Cost of Control}\;}
$$

**Where:**
- **SLE** = Single Loss Expectancy (loss from one incident)
- **ARO** = Annual Rate of Occurrence (how many times per year)
- **ALE** = Annual Loss Expectancy (yearly expected loss)
- **EF** = Exposure Factor (fraction of asset lost in one incident, expressed as a *decimal* between 0 and 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule for KTU paper presentation:** The Exposure Factor **EF** is given as a *percentage* in the problem — convert to a *decimal* (e.g., 60% → 0.6) before substituting into the SLE equation. This single conversion error is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks per problem.

### 2.5 The Defence-in-Depth Principle

No single control is perfect. **Defence in Depth** (also called *layered defence* or *castle-approach*) means using *multiple overlapping controls* so that if one fails, others still protect the asset.

**Engineering utility:** Defence in depth is applied in:
- Network security (perimeter firewall + internal segmentation + endpoint protection + encryption + training)
- Software security (input validation + authentication + authorisation + logging + WAF)
- Physical security (fence + guard + CCTV + biometric + safe)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

This section provides the *complete* worked derivations and example problems a KTU student must be able to solve in the End-Semester Examination.

## 3.1 Derivation: From Threat + Vulnerability to Risk

The KTU-syllabus text (Stallings, *Cryptography and Network Security*, Principles of Information Security) presents risk as a *function* of three variables. The conceptual derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Identify the asset.**
Let $A$ be the value of an asset (e.g., ₹10,00,000 worth of customer data).

**Step 2 — Identify the threat.**
A threat $T$ is a *capability and intent*. We estimate its likelihood in events per year. Call this frequency $f_T$ (events/year).

**Step 3 — Identify the vulnerability.**
A vulnerability $V$ is a *weakness*. We estimate the probability that the threat successfully exploits it: $p_V \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Identify the impact.**
If the threat succeeds, the fraction of the asset destroyed is the *Exposure Factor* $EF \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 5 — Compose the expected annual loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected Loss per Event} &= A \cdot EF \cdot p_V \\
\text{ALE} &= f_T \cdot A \cdot EF \cdot p_V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Reduce to the canonical KTU formula.**
If we let **SLE = A × EF** and **ARO = f_T × p_V**, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the same equation as the box in §2.4, derived from first principles.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Calculating ALE

**Problem.** A company has an online database worth **₹50,00,000**. The probability of a successful SQL-injection attack in a year is estimated at **0.10** (ARO = 0.10). If the attack succeeds, **60%** of the data is compromised. Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

**Step-by-step solution (show all steps in the KTU answer sheet):**

**Step 1 — Identify the asset value.**
$$
A = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Exposure Factor.**
$$
EF = 60\% = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= A \times EF \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Annual Rate of Occurrence.**
$$
\text{ARO} = 0.10
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The expected annual loss from this risk is **₹3,00,000**. Therefore, the company should not spend more than ₹3,00,000 per year on a countermeasure that fully eliminates this risk — otherwise the control is *uneconomical*.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Cost-Benefit Justification of a Countermeasure

**Problem.** A new Web Application Firewall (WAF) is proposed for the database of Example 1. The WAF is expected to reduce the ARO from **0.10 → 0.02** (i.e., reduces successful attacks by 80%). The annual cost of the WAF (subscription + admin) is **₹2,00,000**. Should the company buy the WAF?

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — ALE without WAF (already computed in Example 1).**
$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SLE (unchanged, because the asset value and EF are unchanged).**
$$
\text{SLE} = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE with the WAF.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}} \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 \\
&= 60{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the loss reduction.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss Reduction} &= \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Net Benefit (justifying the investment).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= \text{Loss Reduction} - \text{Cost of WAF} \\
&= 2{,}40{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 40{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**
Since **Net Benefit > 0**, the WAF is *cost-effective* and should be purchased.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state your **decision criterion** clearly: *“Accept the countermeasure if Net Benefit > 0; reject otherwise.”* Examiners award the decision mark only when this is explicitly written.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Mapping an Attack to the CIA Triad

**Problem.** A network sniffer captures all packets flowing through a shared Ethernet segment in a university lab. Identify the security term violated and which CIA pillar is breached.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the attack type.**
Capturing packets passively without modifying them → **Passive Attack**, specifically **Eavesdropping (Release of Message Contents)**.

**Step 2 — Identify the security term violated.**
This is a violation of **Confidentiality** (the *service* being protected is *Data Confidentiality* under X.800).

**Step 3 — Identify the prevention mechanism.**
Encryption (e.g., TLS, SSH) — the proper security *mechanism* to deploy.

**Step 4 — Detectability.**
Passive attacks are *hard to detect* but *easy to prevent* — memorise this trade-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "this is a threat" when the correct term is "attack". A threat is *potential* (e.g., "a hacker *might* snoop"); an attack is *realised* ("the sniffer *actually* captured packets"). This distinction is worth 1 mark in KTU Part A.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Attack → Service → Mechanism

| Attack | CIA pillar violated | X.800 Service breached | Recommended Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping on a Wi-Fi network | Confidentiality | Data Confidentiality | Encipherment (WPA3 / TLS) |
| Man-in-the-Middle modifying a bank transfer | Integrity | Data Integrity | Digital Signature / MAC |
| DDoS on a hospital server | Availability | Availability | Redundancy, rate-limiting, CDN |
| Phishing email pretending to be the bank | Authenticity | Authentication | Mutual authentication, digital certs |
