> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). List any four of its components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** PKI is the set of hardware, software, policies, procedures, and cryptographic mechanisms required to create, manage, distribute, and revoke digital certificates that bind public keys to user identities in a trusted manner.
>
> **Any four components (4 × ½ = 2 marks):**
> 1. **Certification Authority (CA)** — issues and signs certificates.
> 2. **Registration Authority (RA)** — verifies applicant identity.
> 3. **Digital Certificate (X.509)** — binds identity to public key.
> 4. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL)** — lists revoked certificates.
> 5. **Certificate Repository** — public directory for distribution.
> 6. **PKCS standards** — interoperability rules (PKCS#1, #7, #10, #12).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is a Certificate Revocation List (CRL)? Why is OCSP preferred over CRL in modern applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **CRL Definition (1 mark):** A CRL is a digitally signed, time-stamped list issued by a CA containing the serial numbers of all certificates that have been revoked before their scheduled expiry date. The list is published in a public repository (typically LDAP or HTTP).
>
> **Why OCSP is preferred (2 marks):**
> 1. **Real-time status:** OCSP provides *per-certificate* status on demand, whereas CRLs are periodically refreshed (often every 24 hours), leading to revocation latency.
> 2. **Bandwidth efficiency:** CRLs grow linearly with the user base; OCSP responses are small and constant-size.
> 3. **Privacy & performance:** With **OCSP stapling**, the server itself delivers a fresh, signed OCSP response during the TLS handshake, eliminating client-side CA lookups and preserving user privacy.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a) OR Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECDT412]**

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Option (a)*

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the hierarchical PKI trust model with a neat diagram. Compare it briefly with the Web of Trust model.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the X.509 v3 certificate format. Explain how a digital certificate is verified by a relying party, including the role of CRL and OCSP.

##### Model Solution — (a) Hierarchical Trust vs Web of Trust [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical PKI (5 marks):**
> The hierarchical model is a tree structure with a single **Root CA** at the top, intermediate (subordinate) CAs below, and end-entity certificates at the leaves. Trust flows *downward* through the chain: each CA signs the certificate of the CA below it. The root is self-signed and serves as the **trust anchor** embedded in OS/browser trust stores. Examples: **VeriSign/Symantec root, India's CCA-licensed Certifying Authorities, US Federal PKI.**
>
> **Web of Trust (2 marks):**
> The Web of Trust (used in **PGP/GPG**) is a decentralized model where users sign each other's public keys. There is no central authority; trust emerges from a network of mutual signatures. While it is free and censorship-resistant, it does not scale to billions of users and lacks the policy enforcement of PKI. Hence, commercial deployments (HTTPS, e-commerce, banking) universally use the hierarchical model.

| Property | Hierarchical PKI | Web of Trust |
|---|---|---|
| Authority | Centralized CA | Decentralized, peer-signed |
| Scalability | Very high | Low |
| Revocation | CRL / OCSP | Weak; no formal CRL |
| Use case | HTTPS, S/MIME, Banking | Personal email, Open source |
| Standards | X.509, PKCS, RFC 5280 | OpenPGP, RFC 4880 |

##### Model Solution — (b) X.509 v3 Format & Verification [7 marks]

**X.509 v3 Certificate Format (3 marks):**
As detailed in Section 2.2 above, an X.509 v3 certificate contains:
- **Version** (v3 = 2)
- **Serial Number** (unique per CA)
- **Signature Algorithm Identifier** (e.g., sha256WithRSAEncryption)
- **Issuer Distinguished Name (DN)**
- **Validity Period** (notBefore, notAfter)
- **Subject Distinguished Name (DN)**
- **Subject Public Key Info** (algorithm + public key)
- **Extensions v3** (keyUsage, extKeyUsage, subjectAltName, basicConstraints, CRL Distribution Points, Authority Information Access for OCSP)
- **Signature Value** — CA's signature over DER-encoded TBSCertificate

**Verification Process (4 marks):**
1. **Parse** the certificate and decode ASN.1/DER.
2. **Check validity window**: $\text{notBefore} \le t \le \text{notAfter}$.
3. **Check revocation status**:
   - Fetch the **CRL** from the CRL Distribution Point extension and verify CA's signature; ensure serial is not listed.
   - **OR** send an **OCSP request** to the URL in Authority Information Access; validate signed response.
4. **Verify CA's signature**: Compute SHA-256 of TBSCertificate, recover hash from $S^{e_{CA}} \bmod n_{CA}$, and compare.
5. **Validate the chain** recursively up to a trusted root in the local trust store, and check policy/key-usage extensions.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Option (b)*

> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of Registration Authority (RA) and Certification Authority (CA) in PKI. What are the threats to a CA and how are they mitigated?
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the SSL/TLS handshake that uses PKI for server authentication. Mention the cryptographic steps involved.

##### Model Solution — (a) RA, CA, Threats & Mitigations [7 marks]

**Role of RA (2 marks):**
The RA acts as the *identity verifier* on behalf of the CA. It performs **out-of-band** KYC, domain control validation (DCV), and policy compliance checks. The RA does **not** sign certificates itself — it only approves or rejects CSRs forwarded to the CA. This separation of duties is a critical **security control**.

**Role of CA (2 marks):**
The CA is the **trusted signer** of certificates. It binds the verified identity to the public key by computing a digital signature:
$$ S_{CA} = H(\text{TBSCertificate})^{d_{CA}} \bmod n_{CA} $$
The CA also publishes CRLs and/or OCSP responder information, and operates a public repository.

**Threats to CA & Mitigations (3 marks):**

| Threat | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **CA Private Key Compromise** | Attacker can mint fraudulent certs | **HSM (Hardware Security Module)** with FIPS 140-2 Level 3+; offline root; strict dual-control |
| **Mis-issuance** | Wrong entity gets cert | Multi-person approval, automated linting, **Certificate Transparency (CT) logs** |
| **CA Compromise via Web Bug** | Nation-state actor infiltrates CA | Network segmentation, continuous audit, **WebTrust / ETSI audits** |
| **Algorithm weakness** | Forgery possible | Migrate to RSA ≥ 2048 / ECDSA P-256 / Ed25519 |
| **Rogue CA** | Untrusted CA issues certs | Browser/OS root programs enforce strict inclusion policies |

##### Model Solution — (b) TLS Handshake with PKI [7 marks]

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant S as Web Server
    C ->> S: ClientHello (TLS version, cipher suites, random nonce)
    S ->> C: ServerHello (chosen cipher, server random nonce)
    S ->> C: Certificate (X.509 v3 containing server public key)
    S ->> C: ServerKeyExchange if needed (e.g., DHE params)
    S ->> C: ServerHelloDone
    C ->> C: Validate certificate chain to trusted root
    C ->> C: Check OCSP/CRL for revocation
    C ->> S: ClientKeyExchange (pre-master secret encrypted with server PK)
    C ->> S: ChangeCipherSpec
    C ->> S: Finished (MAC over handshake)
    S ->> C: ChangeCipherSpec
    S ->> C: Finished (MAC over handshake)
    C ->> S: Application Data (encrypted)
    S ->> C: Application Data (encrypted)
```

**Step-by-step cryptographic walkthrough (KTU examiner mapping):**
1. **ClientHello / ServerHello** — negotiate TLS 1.3 or 1.2, cipher suite (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` with `X25519`).
2. **Server Certificate** — server sends its X.509 v3 certificate chain.
3. **Client Verification** — client:
   - Validates the chain to a trusted root in its trust store.
   - Verifies the CA's RSA/ECDSA signature using the embedded public key.
   - Checks $\text{notBefore} \le t \le \text{notAfter}$.
   - Confirms `extKeyUsage = serverAuth`.
   - Queries OCSP responder (or relies on **OCSP stapling** from the server's `CertificateStatus` message).
4. **Key Exchange** — client encrypts the pre-master secret with the server's public key (RSA) or performs ECDHE for forward secrecy.
5. **Both sides derive** session keys via HKDF.
6. **Finished messages** — first application data is protected with the new symmetric keys; handshake authentication is complete.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating RA with CA** — RA verifies identity; CA signs. Markers deduct 1 mark per confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting CRL/OCSP** in the verification flow — a TLS handshake question that omits revocation checking is incomplete.
> 3. **Writing "the server sends its public key"** — the server sends its **certificate containing** the public key, signed by a CA. Marks are deducted for loose wording.
> 4. **Mixing up RSA and ECDSA** in the signature field — read the `AlgorithmIdentifier` carefully.
> 5. **No mention of forward secrecy (ECDHE)** in TLS 1.2/1.3 answers — examiners expect ECDHE for full marks.
> 6. **Skipping trust anchor verification** — PKI verification is *recursive up to a trusted root*, not a single-step check.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — PKI for KTU 2024**

- **PKI** = framework for issuing, managing, and revoking digital certificates.
- **Core components**: CA, RA, Certificate, CSR, CRL, OCSP, Repository, PKCS standards.
- **X.509 v3** is the universal certificate format (ASN.1/DER).
- **Certificate chain validation** is *recursive* — end-entity → intermediate → root.
- **CA signature** = $\text{RSA-Sign}(SK_{CA}, H(\text{TBS}))$; verification uses $PK_{CA}$.
- **Validity window** is enforced alongside revocation checks.
- **Revocation mechanisms**: CRL (offline, periodic) vs OCSP (online, real-time) vs OCSP stapling (privacy + performance).
- **Trust models**: Hierarchical (X.509 PKI) vs Web of Trust (PGP). Hierarchical dominates commercial use.
- **CA security** depends on HSM, offline root, and audit compliance (WebTrust/ETSI).
- **TLS handshake** uses PKI for server authentication; modern TLS adds ECDHE for **forward secrecy**.
- **Current minimums**: RSA 2048-bit, SHA-256 hash, certificate validity ≤ 398 days.
- **Quantum risk**: Shor's algorithm breaks RSA/ECDSA; PKI is migrating to **PQC** (ML-DSA, ML-KEM per FIPS 203/204/205).
- **India-specific**: Certifying Authorities are licensed by **CCA (Controller of Certifying Authorities)** under the IT Act, 2000; examples include eMudhra, NIC CA, nCode.
- **Certificate Transparency (CT)** logs (RFC 6962) provide public, append-only audit trails to detect mis-issuance.
- **Key insight for KTU**: PKI is the *trust-enabling* layer; asymmetric cryptography alone is insufficient.
- **Examiner's mantra**: Always state *who trusts whom* and *which key signs what* — vagueness loses marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Cryptography: Secure Communications (SSL/TLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Cryptography: Secure Communications (SSL/TLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of SSL/TLS

**Secure Sockets Layer (SSL)** and its successor **Transport Layer Security (TLS)** are cryptographic protocols designed to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** for communications over an insecure network such as the public Internet. They operate as a **session-layer security shim** sandwiched between the Transport Layer (TCP) and the Application Layer (HTTP, SMTP, FTP, etc.) in the TCP/IP stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology)**
> "SSL/TLS is an application-independent protocol that creates a secure channel between two communicating parties using a combination of public-key cryptography (for authentication and key exchange), symmetric-key cryptography (for bulk data encryption), and Message Authentication Codes (for integrity)."

TLS 1.2 (RFC 5246) and TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) are the modern deployed versions. SSL 3.0 is officially deprecated (POODLE attack, 2014) and must not be used.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diplomatic Courier Box"

Imagine two ambassadors, **Alice (Client)** and **Bob (Server)**, who have never met, sitting in a crowded train station full of eavesdroppers:

| Real-World Analogy | Cryptographic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Bob shows his **government-issued ID** | Server **X.509 Digital Certificate** verified by a trusted CA |
| They agree on a **shared codebook language** (e.g., Pig Latin) | Negotiation of a **Cipher Suite** |
| Alice opens a **sealed combination lock** using Bob's public padlock | **Key Exchange** (RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECDHE) |
| They speak in the agreed codebook | **Symmetric Bulk Encryption** (AES-128/256-GCM) |
| Each message has a **wax seal with imprint** | **MAC / HMAC** for message integrity |
| Both sign a **notarized transcript** at the end | **Finished messages** with PRF-verified handshake |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SSL/TLS matters for KTU exams:** Almost every secure web transaction (online banking, e-commerce, email) sits on TLS. It is the **single most deployed cryptographic protocol in the world**, and KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists it as a Module 2 application of cryptography.

### 1.3 The Four Cryptographic Goals Achieved

1. **Confidentiality** → Symmetric encryption (AES, ChaCha20) using a session key known only to the two parties.
2. **Integrity** → HMAC-SHA256 or AEAD modes (AES-GCM) detect any bit tampering.
3. **Authentication** → Server (and optionally client) presents an X.509 certificate chained to a trusted Root CA.
4. **Forward Secrecy** → Use of (EC)DHE key exchange so that compromise of long-term private keys does not retroactively decrypt past sessions (mandatory in TLS 1.3).

### 1.4 Position in the Network Stack

$$
\text{Application Layer (HTTP)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{TLS Record Layer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{TCP} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{IP} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Link}
$$

The standard port for HTTPS is **TCP 443**, while plain HTTP uses **TCP 80**.

### 1.5 Evolution Timeline (KTU High-Yield Memorization)

| Version | Year | Status | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| SSL 1.0 | 1994 | Never released | Internal Netscape design |
| SSL 2.0 | 1995 | **Deprecated (RFC 6176, 2011)** | First public release |
| SSL 3.0 | 1996 | **Deprecated (RFC 7568, 2015)** | Redesign after attacks |
| TLS 1.0 | 1999 | Legacy / weak | Minor upgrade over SSL 3.0 |
| TLS 1.1 | 2006 | Legacy | Explicit IV, CBC attack fixes |
| **TLS 1.2** | 2008 | **Currently dominant** | SHA-256, AEAD, PRF flexibility |
| **TLS 1.3** | 2018 | **Modern / recommended** | 1-RTT handshake, mandatory PFS, 0-RTT |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The TLS Protocol Stack

TLS is **not a single protocol** but a **layered family of four sub-protocols**, each operating on its own logical channel:

1. **Handshake Protocol** — negotiates parameters, authenticates peers, establishes shared secrets.
2. **ChangeCipherSpec Protocol** — single-byte signal indicating that subsequent records will use the newly agreed keys.
3. **Alert Protocol** — conveys errors, warnings, and connection closure reasons.
4. **Record Protocol** — the actual carrier that fragments, compresses (optional), encrypts, and MACs application data.

### 2.2 The TLS 1.2 Handshake — Bullet-by-Bullet Logic

**Phase 1 — Capability Negotiation (Client → Server):**
- Client sends a **ClientHello** containing:
  - `client_version` (highest supported, e.g., TLS 1.2)
  - `client_random` → **32 bytes of fresh entropy**
  - `session_id` (for resumption)
  - `cipher_suites` (ordered list of acceptable algorithms)
  - `compression_methods`
  - `extensions` (SNI, ALPN, supported_groups, signature_algorithms, …)

**Phase 2 — Server Response & Authentication (Server → Client):**
- **ServerHello** echoes chosen `server_version`, `server_random` (32 bytes), `session_id`, `cipher_suite`, and extensions.
- **Certificate** message: server's X.509 chain.
- **ServerKeyExchange** (only for DHE/ECDHE suites): carries the server's ephemeral DH/ECDH public parameters and a **digital signature** over them.
- **ServerHelloDone** (empty marker in TLS 1.2).

**Phase 3 — Client Key Delivery & Verification (Client → Server):**
- **ClientKeyExchange**: client sends its ephemeral DH public value (or encrypts a pre-master secret with server's RSA public key in non-PFS suites).
- **CertificateVerify** (only if client is authenticated): signs a hash of all handshake messages.
- **ChangeCipherSpec (CCS)**: announces switch to negotiated keys.
- **Finished**: `PRF(master_secret, "client finished", Hash(handshake_messages))` — first message protected by new keys. This is the **key confirmation** step.

**Phase 4 — Server Acknowledgment (Server → Client):**
- **ChangeCipherSpec (CCS)**
- **Finished**: analogous server-side verification hash.

After this **2-RTT** exchange (1-RTT in TLS 1.3), the **Record Protocol** takes over for application data.

### 2.3 The TLS 1.3 Handshake (Modern, 1-RTT)

TLS 1.3 **removes static RSA key exchange** entirely. Only (EC)DHE is permitted, guaranteeing forward secrecy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** *"Explain how TLS 1.3 differs from TLS 1.2 in key exchange."* — Answer: TLS 1.3 eliminates cipher suites without (EC)DHE, mandates forward secrecy, reduces handshake to 1-RTT, and encrypts more of the handshake (server certificate is now encrypted under handshake traffic keys).

In TLS 1.3, the server may send its **Certificate, CertificateVerify, and Finished** in the same flight as ServerHello (assuming the client included a `key_share` for the server's chosen group in its first flight). This is called **0.5-RTT server response**.

### 2.4 Key Derivation — The Mathematical Heart

TLS uses the **HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand Key Derivation Function (HKDF)** to derive every key from a small amount of shared entropy.

**Step A — Pre-Master Secret (TLS 1.2):**

For RSA suites:
$$
\text{PMS} = \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{RSA-encrypt}} \text{Server}
$$
where PMS is **48 bytes** chosen by the client.

For (EC)DHE suites:
$$
\text{PMS} = \text{DH\_shared\_secret} \quad (\text{an agreed group element in } \mathbb{Z}_p^* \text{ or } E(\mathbb{F}_p))
$$

**Step B — Master Secret (TLS 1.2, PRF = HMAC-SHA256):**
$$
\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\; \text{"master secret"},\; \text{ClientRandom} \Vert \text{ServerRandom}) \quad \text{(48 bytes)}
$$

**Step C — Key Block Expansion:**
$$
\text{KeyBlock} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; \text{ServerRandom} \Vert \text{ClientRandom})
$$

The KeyBlock is sliced, in order, into:
- `client_write_MAC_key`
- `server_write_MAC_key`
- `client_write_encryption_key`
- `server_write_encryption_key`
- `client_write_IV`
- `server_write_IV`

**Step D — TLS 1.3 HKDF (cleaner):**

$$
\text{Early\_Secret} = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt}=\text{""},\; \text{IKM} = \text{PSK})
$$

$$
\text{Handshake\_Secret} = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt}=\text{Derive-Secret(Early\_Secret, "c e traffic", ClientHello…ServerHello)},\; \text{IKM} = \text{DH\_shared\_secret})
$$

$$
\text{Master\_Secret} = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt}=\text{Derive-Secret(Handshake\_Secret, "c hs traffic", ClientHello…ServerFinished)},\; \text{IKM} = \text{""})
$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **RSA Encryption (TLS key transport)** | $C \equiv M^{e} \bmod n$ | Server cert public-key encryption of PMS |
| **RSA Decryption** | $M \equiv C^{d} \bmod n$ | Server recovers PMS |
| **Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret** | $K \equiv g^{ab} \bmod p \equiv (g^a)^b \equiv (g^b)^a \bmod p$ | (EC)DHE key exchange |
| **DH Public Value (client)** | $A = g^a \bmod p$ | Sent in ClientKeyExchange |
| **DH Public Value (server)** | $B = g^b \bmod p$ | Sent in ServerKeyExchange |
| **HMAC** | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | TLS 1.2 MAC computation |
| **TLS 1.2 PRF** | $\text{PRF}(\text{secret}, \text{label}, \text{seed}) = \text{P\_SHA256}(\text{secret}, \text{label} + \text{seed})$ | Deriving MS and KeyBlock |
| **HKDF-Extract** | $\text{PRK} = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{salt},\; \text{IKM})$ | TLS 1.3 secret extraction |
| **HKDF-Expand** | $\text{OKM} = T(1) \Vert T(2) \Vert \cdots$, where $T(i) = \text{HMAC}(\text{PRK}, T(i-1) \Vert \text{info} \Vert i)$ | TLS 1.3 key expansion |
| **AEAD Encryption (AES-GCM)** | $C,\; \text{Tag} = \text{AEAD-Encrypt}(K, IV, \text{Plaintext}, AAD)$ | TLS 1.2/1.3 record protection |
| **X.509 Signature Verification** | Accept iff $\text{Verify}_{\text{CA\_public}}(H(\text{TBSCertificate})) = \text{valid}$ | Server certificate validation |
| **Forward Secrecy Property** | Compromise of long-term key $\not\Rightarrow$ recovery of session key | Mandatory in (EC)DHE |
| **Recommended Cipher Suite (TLS 1.3)** | `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` | Modern best practice |
| **Recommended Cipher Suite (TLS 1.2)** | `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` | Legacy-compatible |
| **Record Fragment Max Size** | $2^{14} \text{ bytes} = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes}$ | TLS 1.2/1.3 fragmentation limit |
| **Sequence Number Size** | **64-bit** unsigned (TLS 1.3) | Replay protection |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars (`|x|`) appear in the table above** — KTU board exams sometimes deduct marks for markdown rendering errors. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in your answer scripts to be safe.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HTTPS Everywhere** — Every browser since 2018 marks HTTP as "Not Secure".
- **IoT & Embedded TLS** — MQTT-over-TLS (port 8883), CoAP-over-DTLS for constrained devices.
- **Email** — SMTP STARTTLS, IMAP/POP3 TLS.
- **VPNs** — OpenVPN and WireGuard use TLS for control channel.
- **API Security** — REST/gRPC services use TLS as the floor; OAuth/JWT add authorization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Pre-Master Secret to Application Traffic Keys (TLS 1.2)

We will exhaustively derive the **6-key block** that encrypts and MACs both directions of a TLS 1.2 session. This is the most calculation-heavy, mark-rich question KTU examiners ask.

**Step 1 — Establish the Pre-Master Secret (PMS).**
Assume the negotiated suite is `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`. The server's ECDH public key is the point $B = b \cdot G$ on the NIST P-256 curve, and the client generates private scalar $a$, computes $A = a \cdot G$, and sends $A$ in ClientKeyExchange. Both sides compute:

$$
K = a \cdot B = b \cdot A \in E(\mathbb{F}_p)
$$

Then they extract the x-coordinate and run it through the KDF:

$$
\text{PMS} = \text{KDF}(x_K, 48 \text{ bytes})
$$

**Step 2 — Master Secret via PRF.**

The TLS 1.2 PRF for SHA-256 is defined as:

$$
\text{P\_SHA256}(\text{secret},\; \text{seed}) = A_1 \Vert A_2 \Vert \cdots
$$

where

$$
A(0) = \text{seed},\qquad A(i) = \text{HMAC}(\text{secret},\; A(i-1)) \text{ for } i \ge 1
$$

and the output is

$$
\text{P\_SHA256} = \text{HMAC}(\text{secret},\; A(1) \Vert \text{seed}) \;\Vert\; \text{HMAC}(\text{secret},\; A(2) \Vert \text{seed}) \;\Vert\; \cdots
$$

Now compute the Master Secret:

$$
\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\; \text{"master secret"},\; C \Vert S)
$$

where $C$ = ClientRandom (32 bytes) and $S$ = ServerRandom (32 bytes). The output is truncated to **48 bytes**.

**Step 3 — Key Block via PRF.**

$$
\text{KeyBlock} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; S \Vert C)
$$

The required length depends on the negotiated cipher. For `AES_256_GCM_SHA384`:

| Key Material | Bytes Needed |
|---|---|
| `client_write_MAC_key` (HMAC-SHA384) | 48 |
| `server_write_MAC_key` (HMAC-SHA384) | 48 |
| `client_write_encryption_key` (AES-256) | 32 |
| `server_write_encryption_key` (AES-256) | 32 |
| `client_write_IV` (GCM explicit nonce) | 8 |
| `server_write_IV` (GCM explicit nonce) | 8 |
| **Total** | **176 bytes** |

**Step 4 — IV Construction (AES-GCM).**

TLS 1.2 constructs the **96-bit IV** for each record as:

$$
\text{IV} = \text{client\_write\_IV} \;\Vert\; \text{explicit\_nonce}
$$

where `client_write_IV` is the 4-byte salt (from the KeyBlock) and `explicit_nonce` is a **8-byte** random nonce placed in the record header by the sender and incremented per record.

**Step 5 — Finished Message Computation.**

Client Finished (sent immediately after CCS):

$$
\text{Verify\_Data}_{\text{client}} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"client finished"},\; \text{SHA256}(\text{all handshake messages}))
$$

This is **12 bytes** for SHA-256. The server independently computes the same value, and any mismatch aborts the handshake.

**Step 6 — Application Data Encryption.**

For each application record, the sender computes:

$$
(C,\; \text{Tag}) = \text{AES-256-GCM-Encrypt}(K, \text{IV}, \text{Plaintext}, \text{AAD})
$$

where AAD includes the TLS record header (type, version, length, sequence number). The 16-byte Tag provides both **integrity** and (partial) **authentication**.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Diffie-Hellman Computation (Classical Example for Board Exams)

Suppose the negotiated group has $p = 23$, $g = 5$.

- Alice (client) picks private $a = 6$, computes $A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$.

$$
5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^6 \equiv 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}
$$

So $A = 8$.

- Bob (server) picks private $b = 15$, computes $B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$.

First reduce using Fermat: $5^{22} \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$, so $5^{15} = 5^{22-7} \equiv 5^{-7} \equiv (5^7)^{-1} \pmod{23}$.

Compute $5^7$:

$$
5^1 = 5,\quad 5^2 = 2,\quad 5^3 = 10,\quad 5^4 = 4,\quad 5^5 = 20,\quad 5^6 = 8,\quad 5^7 = 40 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}
$$

So $5^{-7} \equiv 17^{-1} \pmod{23}$. Solve $17x \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$:

$$
17 \cdot 19 = 323 = 14 \cdot 23 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{23}
$$

Therefore $B = 19$.

- **Shared secret** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23$.

Reduce $8^{15}$:

$$
8^2 = 64 \equiv 18 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^4 \equiv 18^2 = 324 = 14 \cdot 23 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^8 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \pmod{23}
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
4 \cdot 2 = 8
$$

$$
8 \cdot 18 = 144 = 6 \cdot 23 + 6 \equiv 6 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
6 \cdot 8 = 48 = 2 \cdot 23 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

So $K = 2$.

Verify: $K = B^a = 19^6 \bmod 23$.

$$
19^2 = 361 = 15 \cdot 23 + 16 \equiv 16 \equiv -7 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
19^4 \equiv (-7)^2 = 49 = 2 \cdot 23 + 3 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
19^6 = 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \equiv 3 \cdot (-7) = -21 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \;\checkmark
$$

Both sides agree on $K = 2$, demonstrating that the handshake produces an identical shared secret without ever transmitting it.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Key Exchange Methods

| Property | Plain RSA (TLS ≤ 1.2) | Static DH | DHE | ECDHE (TLS 1.3 standard) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Secrecy** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Performance** | Fastest | Fast | Slowest | Fast (small keys, e.g., 256-bit ≈ 3072-bit RSA) |
| **Server CPU cost** | One RSA decrypt | One DH exp | One exp per connection | One EC scalar mult per connection |
| **Quantum resistance** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (post-quantum variant PQC-KEM emerging) |
| **Use in TLS 1.3** | **Removed** | **Removed** | Allowed | **Required** |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — RSA Key Transport in TLS 1.2 RSA Suite

Let $p = 61$, $q = 53$, $e = 17$. The server's RSA public key is $(n, e) = (3233, 17)$.

Client wants to send PMS = 42 (a 48-byte integer truncated for this example).

**Encrypt:**
$$
C = 42^{17} \bmod 3233
$$

Compute stepwise using repeated squaring:

$$
42^1 = 42
$$

$$
42^2 = 1764 \bmod 3233 = 1764
$$

$$
42^4 = 1764^2 = 3{,}111{,}696 \bmod 3233
$$

Divide: $3{,}111{,}696 / 3233 = 962$ remainder $r$. $962 \times 3233 = 3{,}110{,}146$. So $r = 3{,}111{,}696 - 3{,}110{,}146 = 1550$. Thus $42^4 \equiv 1550 \pmod{3233}$.

$$
42^8 \equiv 1550^2 = 2{,}402{,}500 \bmod 3233
$$

$2{,}402{,}500 / 3233 = 743$ r $r = 2{,}402{,}500 - 743 \cdot 3233 = 2{,}402{,}500 - 2{,}402{,}119 = 381$. So $42^8 \equiv 381 \pmod{3233}$.

$$
42^{16} \equiv 381^2 = 145{,}161 \bmod 3233
$$

$145{,}161 / 3233 = 44$ r $r = 145{,}161 - 44 \cdot 3233 = 145{,}161 - 142{,}252 = 2909$. So $42^{16} \equiv 2909 \pmod{3233}$.

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:

$$
42^{17} = 42^{16} \cdot 42^1 \equiv 2909 \cdot 42 = 122{,}178 \bmod 3233
$$

$122{,}178 / 3233 = 37$ r $r = 122{,}178 - 37 \cdot 3233 = 122{,}178 - 119{,}621 = 2557$.

Thus $C = 2557$. The server recovers PMS using $d$, the private exponent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** This exact kind of modular exponentiation is what KTU examiners love to assign as a 7-mark sub-question. Show every squaring step explicitly. The 5-step "square-and-multiply" pattern above is the expected solution.

### 3.5 Record Protocol Byte Layout (For Diagram-of-Bytes Type Questions)

$$
\underbrace{\text{Type}}_{1 \text{ B}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{Version}}_{2 \text{ B}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{Length}}_{2 \text{ B}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{Fragment}}_{\le 16{,}384 \text{ B}}
$$

- **Type:** `22` (Handshake), `20` (ChangeCipherSpec), `21` (Alert), `23` (Application Data).
- **Version:** `0x0303` for TLS 1.2, `0x0301` for compatibility (TLS 1.3).
- **Length:** 16-bit big-endian.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TLS 1.2 Handshake (Full 2-RTT)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    C->>S: ClientHello (version, random_C, cipher_suites, extensions)
    S->>C: ServerHello (version, random_S, chosen_suite)
    S->>C: Certificate (X.509 chain)
    S->>C: ServerKeyExchange (ECDHE params + signed)
    S->>C: ServerHelloDone

    Note over C: Verify cert chain
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange (ECDHE public A)
    C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec
    C->>S: Finished (PRF verify_data)

    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec
    S->>C: Finished (PRF verify_data)

    Note over C,S: Application data encrypted with AES-GCM
    C->>S: Application Data (AES-256-GCM)
    S->>C: Application Data (AES-256-GCM)
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — TLS 1.3 Handshake (1-RTT)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    C->>S: ClientHello + key_share (ECDH A)
    Note over S: Server derives handshake_secret
    S->>C: ServerHello + key_share (ECDH B)
    S->>C: EncryptedExtensions
    S->>C: Certificate
    S->>C: CertificateVerify
    S->>C: Finished

    Note over C: Client derives handshake_secret, master_secret
    C->>S: Finished (encrypted)

    Note over C,S: Application data (AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305)
    C->>S: App Data
    S->>C: App Data
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — TLS Sub-Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP[Application Layer HTTP SMTP IMAP]
    subgraph TLS_STACK
        HP[Handshake Protocol]
        CCS[Change Cipher Spec Protocol]
        AP[Alert Protocol]
        RP[Record Protocol]
    end
    TCP[TCP Transport Layer]
    IP[IP Network Layer]

    APP --> RP
    HP --> RP
    CCS --> RP
    AP --> RP
    RP --> TCP
    TCP --> IP
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Functional Flow — Key Derivation Pipeline (TLS 1.3)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PSK[PSK optional] --> ES[Early Secret HKDF Extract]
    DH[ECDH Shared Secret] --> HS[Handshake Secret HKDF Extract]
    NULL[empty IKM] --> MS[Master Secret HKDF Extract]

    ES --> HS
    HS --> MS

    ES --> CETS[Client Early Traffic Secret]
    ES --> SETS[Server Early Traffic Secret]

    HS --> CHTS[Client Handshake Traffic Secret]
    HS --> SHTS[Server Handshake Traffic Secret]

    MS --> CAPS[Client Application Traffic Secret]
    MS --> SAPS[Server Application Traffic Secret]
    MS --> RES[Resumption Secret]

    CETS --> EK1[Early Data Key]
    CHTS --> EK2[Handshake Key]
    CAPS --> EK3[App Data Key Client]
    SAPS --> EK4[App Data Key Server]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — Session Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Connecting : TCP SYN
    Connecting --> Handshaking : ClientHello sent
    Handshaking --> Authenticated : ServerHello + Certificate verified
    Authenticated --> DataTransfer : Finished messages exchanged
    DataTransfer --> Resumable : NewSessionTicket
    DataTransfer --> Closing : close_notify alert
    Resumable --> Handshaking : Resumed session via PSK
    Closing --> [*] : TCP FIN
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*What is SSL/TLS? List any two differences between SSL 3.0 and TLS 1.2.*

**Model Answer:**

SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) and TLS (Transport Layer Security) are cryptographic protocols that establish an encrypted, authenticated session between a client and a server over an insecure network. They provide confidentiality through symmetric encryption, integrity through MACs, and authentication through X.509 certificates.

**Two differences between SSL 3.0 and TLS 1.2:**

1. **PRF Construction:** SSL 3.0 uses a non-standard ad-hoc combination of MD5 and SHA-1 in the PRF, whereas TLS 1.2 uses P_Hash based on a single configurable hash (typically HMAC-SHA256), improving both security and flexibility.
2. **Cipher Suite Authenticity:** TLS 1.2 separates authentication from key exchange (allowing combinations like ECDHE-RSA-AES-GCM-SHA384) while SSL 3.0 was more rigid and contained the export-grade suites that led to attacks like FREAK and Logjam.

*(Any two valid differences accepted — 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two differences.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define a **cipher suite**. Give one example of a TLS 1.2 cipher suite and explain each of its four components.*

**Model Answer:**

A **cipher suite** is a named combination of algorithms that defines how a TLS session will perform key exchange, authentication, bulk encryption, and message authentication.

**Example:** `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`

| Component | Algorithm | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `ECDHE` | Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral | Key exchange with forward secrecy |
| `RSA` | RSA signature scheme | Server authentication |
| `AES_128_GCM` | AES-128 in Galois/Counter Mode | Symmetric bulk encryption (also provides integrity) |
| `SHA256` | SHA-256 hash | PRF and digital signature hash |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for breakdown of components.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **TLS 1.2 handshake protocol** in detail. Discuss the purpose of each message exchanged. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **key derivation process** in TLS 1.2, clearly distinguishing between the pre-master secret, master secret, and key block. Show how AES-256-GCM keys and IVs are derived from the key block. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**1. ClientHello (Client → Server):**
The client initiates the handshake by sending a ClientHello message containing:
- Highest TLS version supported (e.g., TLS 1.2)
- **ClientRandom** — 32 bytes of fresh entropy
- A 32-byte **session ID** (used for resumption)
- An ordered list of acceptable **cipher suites** (e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` first)
- A list of supported **compression methods** (usually `null`)
- Optional **extensions**: SNI, ALPN, supported_groups, signature_algorithms, etc.

**2. ServerHello (Server → Client):**
The server selects one cipher suite from the client's list and replies with ServerHello, echoing the chosen version, its own 32-byte **ServerRandom**, the chosen session ID, cipher suite, compression method, and extensions.

**3. Certificate (Server → Client):**
The server transmits its **X.509 certificate chain** — leaf server certificate, intermediate CA certificates — used by the client to authenticate the server. The chain must lead to a trusted Root CA in the client's trust store.

**4. ServerKeyExchange (Server → Client):**
For DHE/ECDHE suites, the server sends its ephemeral DH/ECDH public parameters, signed using its long-term private key (RSA or ECDSA). This binds the DH parameters to the server's identity, preventing MITM substitution.

**5. ServerHelloDone (Server → Client):**
A single-byte marker indicating the server has finished its hello phase.

**6. Client Verification (Client side):**
The client:
- Validates the certificate chain (expiry, hostname, revocation via CRL/OCSP, signature).
- Verifies the ServerKeyExchange signature using the public key in the certificate.
- Generates its own ephemeral ECDH scalar $a$, computes $A = aG$, and prepares the pre-master secret.

**7. ClientKeyExchange (Client → Server):**
Sends $A$ (or, for RSA suites, the RSA-encrypted pre-master secret).

**8. ChangeCipherSpec (Client → Server):**
Signals that the client will now encrypt subsequent records using the newly negotiated keys.

**9. Finished (Client → Server):**
Sends `PRF(MS, "client finished", SHA256(handshake_messages))` — 12 bytes, encrypted and MACed with the new keys. This is the **key confirmation**.

**10. ChangeCipherSpec + Finished (Server → Client):**
The server switches keys and sends its own Finished, allowing the client to verify that the server indeed possesses the same master secret.

**Block Diagram** — see SECTION 4.1 above.

*(7 marks: 1 for ClientHello, 1 for ServerHello+Cert, 1 for ServerKeyExchange, 1 for client verification, 1 for Finished, 1 for block diagram, 1 for summary.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**1. Pre-Master Secret (PMS):**
- **RSA suite:** PMS is a 48-byte random number chosen by the client, RSA-encrypted under the server's public key, and sent in ClientKeyExchange. Only the server (with private key) can decrypt.
- **DHE/ECDHE suite:** PMS is the DH shared secret $K = a \cdot b \cdot G$ (in ECDHE), encoded into a 48-byte string.

**2. Master Secret (MS):**
$$
\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\; \text{"master secret"},\; \text{ClientRandom} \Vert \text{ServerRandom})
$$
The PRF is `P_SHA256` (for SHA-256-based suites). Output is **48 bytes**.

**3. Key Block:**
$$
\text{KeyBlock} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; \text{ServerRandom} \Vert \text{ClientRandom})
$$
Length depends on the negotiated cipher.

**4. Slicing for AES-256-GCM-SHA384 (176 bytes total):**
- `client_MAC_key` → 48 bytes (for HMAC-SHA384, but in GCM mode the MAC is integrated into AEAD, so this is unused; kept for the cipher suite family).
- `server_MAC_key` → 48 bytes.
- `client_write_key` → 32 bytes (AES-256).
- `server_write_key` → 32 bytes.
- `client_write_IV` → 4 bytes (salt for GCM).
- `server_write_IV` → 4 bytes.

**5. Per-Record IV Construction (AES-GCM):**
The 12-byte IV for each record is formed as:
$$
\text{IV} = \text{4-byte salt} \;\Vert\; \text{8-byte explicit nonce}
$$
The explicit nonce is randomly generated per record, ensuring nonce uniqueness (essential for GCM security).

**6. Key Derivation Flow Diagram** — see SECTION 4.4.

**7. Why this is Secure:**
- PMS never travels in the clear (RSA) or is computationally infeasible to derive (DH).
- Master Secret binds ClientRandom + ServerRandom, preventing replay.
- Separate client/server keys enforce directionality and prevent reflection attacks.
- Fresh key material per session prevents key reuse.

*(7 marks: 1 for PMS, 1 for MS, 1 for KeyBlock, 2 for slicing + IV, 1 for diagram, 1 for security reasoning.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2 Alternative]`

**(a)** What is **forward secrecy**? Why is RSA key exchange vulnerable to retrospective decryption, and how does (EC)DHE provide forward secrecy in TLS? Show the Diffie-Hellman computation with $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $a = 6$, $b = 15$, and verify the shared secret. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **SSL 3.0, TLS 1.2, and TLS 1.3** in a tabular form covering at least 7 features. Discuss why TLS 1.3 mandates (EC)DHE and what 0-RTT resumption is. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**1. Forward Secrecy Definition:**
Forward secrecy (FS), also called **perfect forward secrecy (PFS)**, is a property of a key agreement protocol in which the compromise of long-term private keys does **not** result in the compromise of past session keys. In other words, even if an adversary records all ciphertext of all past sessions and later steals a long-term private key, the adversary cannot decrypt the recorded traffic.

**2. Why RSA Key Exchange Lacks Forward Secrecy:**
In a static-RSA TLS 1.2 suite (e.g., `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA`):
- The client generates a random pre-master secret PMS and encrypts it under the **server's long-term RSA public key**.
- The server decrypts PMS using its long-term RSA private key.
- Both sides then derive the session keys from PMS.
- If an attacker records the encrypted PMS, then **years later** steals the server's RSA private key (e.g., via a court order, a coerced disclosure, or a future quantum break), the attacker can decrypt PMS and recover the session key. **All past sessions are compromised.**

**3. Why (EC)DHE Provides Forward Secrecy:**
In (EC)DHE:
- Both sides generate **fresh, ephemeral DH/ECDH key pairs per session**.
- The DH shared secret $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ depends on the private exponents $a, b$ which are **discarded** after the session ends.
- Even if the long-term signature key is later compromised, $a$ and $b$ are gone, and the DH problem (computing $g^{ab}$ from $g$, $g^a$, $g^b$) is computationally infeasible.
- Therefore, recorded traffic remains confidential.

**4. Numerical Computation (as in SECTION 3.2):**
With $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $a = 6$, $b = 15$:
- $A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23 = 8$.
- $B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23 = 19$.
- Shared secret $K = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23 = 2$.
- Verification: $K = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23 = 2$. ✓

**5. Engineering Impact:**
TLS 1.3 **removed all static-RSA and static-DH cipher suites** in 2018 because of this very property. Browsers, banks, and email servers all now use ECDHE for forward secrecy.

*(7 marks: 1 for FS definition, 2 for RSA vulnerability, 2 for DHE PFS, 1 for DH computation, 1 for verification + engineering comment.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Tabular Comparison (7 features):**

| Feature | SSL 3.0 | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1996 | 2008 | 2018 |
| **PRF** | MD5+SHA-1 ad-hoc | P_Hash (configurable, e.g., SHA-256) | HKDF (Extract + Expand) |
| **Key Exchange** | RSA, Fixed DH, Fortezza | RSA, DHE, ECDHE, PSK | **Only (EC)DHE, PSK** |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Optional | Optional | **Mandatory** |
| **Handshake RTT** | 2-RTT | 2-RTT | **1-RTT** (0-RTT for resumption) |
| **Cipher Suite Count** | ~30 | ~300+ | **5 suites** (all AEAD) |
| **Encrypted Handshake** | No (cert in clear) | No | Partial (post-ServerHello encrypted) |
| **CBC Mode Ciphers** | Allowed (vulnerable to BEAST/POODLE) | Allowed (with mitigations) | **Removed (only AEAD)** |
| **Status** | Deprecated (RFC 7568) | Widely deployed | Modern recommendation |

**Why TLS 1.3 Mandates (EC)DHE:**
- Any non-(EC)DHE suite allows retroactive decryption upon private key compromise.
- (EC)DHE is also more quantum-resilient than RSA for key exchange (though neither is post-quantum).
- ECDHE provides 128-bit security with a 256-bit curve, far smaller keys than equivalent RSA.

**0-RTT Resumption:**
- A previously connected client can send application data in its very first flight (the ClientHello), encrypted under a **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** derived from a `NewSessionTicket` issued in a prior session.
- The server accepts the 0-RTT data only if it recognizes the PSK.
- **Trade-off:** 0-RTT data is **not forward secret** and is vulnerable to replay attacks, so it must be limited to idempotent operations (e.g., GET requests).

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 1 for DHE mandate reason, 2 for 0-RTT explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in SSL/TLS questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the version numbers** — always state TLS 1.2 vs 1.3 explicitly. Mixing up versions and listing features under the wrong column loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PRF with MAC** — the PRF is a **key derivation** function used to produce key material; the MAC (e.g., HMAC-SHA256) is a **data integrity** primitive used on records. They are different.
> 3. **Omitting the Finished message** — many students forget that **Finished** provides key confirmation; without it, the handshake has no explicit proof that both sides derived the same master secret.
> 4. **Drawing the diagram without sequence labels** — a block diagram without the actual messages flowing (ClientHello, ServerHello, etc.) earns partial credit only. Always label arrows.
> 5. **Skipping the salt in GCM IV construction** — TLS 1.2 GCM uses a 4-byte salt + 8-byte explicit nonce = 12-byte IV. Writing only "12-byte nonce" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Writing RSA encryption formula as $C = M^e$** — must always include "$\bmod n$". RSA is modular, not integer exponentiation.
> 7. **Ignoring 0-RTT trade-offs** — if asked about 0-RTT, **must** mention replay vulnerability and that 0-RTT is **not** forward secret.
> 8. **Static-RSA still labelled as "secure"** — static RSA key exchange is **insecure** for forward secrecy; TLS 1.3 removed it. Markers deduct for presenting it as acceptable in modern TLS.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for SSL/TLS (Module 2, KTU 2024)**
>
> ✅ SSL/TLS provides **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, (forward secrecy)**.
> ✅ SSL 3.0 is **deprecated**; TLS 1.2 is **widely deployed**; TLS 1.3 is **modern recommended**.
> ✅ TLS has **4 sub-protocols**: Handshake, ChangeCipherSpec, Alert, Record.
> ✅ TLS Record carries: `Type ‖ Version ‖ Length ‖ Fragment` (max fragment = **16 384 bytes**).
> ✅ The TLS 1.2 handshake is **2-RTT**; TLS 1.3 is **1-RTT** (0-RTT for resumption).
> ✅ Three-stage key derivation: **PMS → MS → KeyBlock** (TLS 1.2); **HKDF Extract/Expand** (TLS 1.3).
> ✅ Cipher suite structure: `TLS_KEYX_AUTH_WITH_BULK_HASH` (e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`).
> ✅ **Forward secrecy** requires (EC)DHE — TLS 1.3 **removed** static RSA and static DH.
> ✅ **Finished message** uses PRF over the SHA hash of all handshake messages — provides **key confirmation**.
> ✅ **Server authentication** is via X.509 certificate chain; client authentication is optional (CertificateVerify).
> ✅ **AEAD ciphers** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) provide both **confidentiality + integrity** in one operation; non-AEAD ciphers require separate MAC.
> ✅ **Default HTTPS port** = **TCP 443**; **SMTP submission over TLS** = **TCP 587** (STARTTLS).
> ✅ RSA key transport: $C = M^e \bmod n$ (must include "mod n").
> ✅ DH shared secret: $K = g^{ab} \bmod p = (g^a)^b \bmod p = (g^b)^a \bmod p$.
> ✅ The PRF for TLS 1.2 with SHA-256 is **P_SHA256** based on HMAC.
> ✅ **TLS 1.3 cipher suites** are reduced to 5: `TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`, `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`, `TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256`, and their respective PSK variants.
> ✅ The **0-RTT resumption mode** is **not forward secret** and is vulnerable to replay; use only for idempotent GETs.
> ✅ **Downgrade attacks** (e.g., POODLE) motivated the deprecation of SSL 3.0 and led to version-fallback protections like TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV.
> ✅ **Certificate transparency (CT) logs** and **OCSP stapling** are modern additions that help detect mis-issued certificates.
> ✅ **HPKP** (HTTP Public Key Pinning) is deprecated; **CAA DNS records** and **Expect-CT** header have replaced it.
> ✅ **Quantum risk**: Both RSA and (EC)DH will be broken by a sufficiently large quantum computer (Shor's algorithm); **post-quantum KEMs** like Kyber are being standardized for TLS 1.3 (hybrid suites).
> ✅ **Mutual TLS (mTLS)** is TLS with **client certificates** — used heavily in microservices, service mesh (Istio, Linkerd), and zero-trust networks.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Applications of Cryptography: Secure Communications (SSL/TLS)**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Email Security (PGP, S/MIME)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Email Security (PGP, S/MIME)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Email Security

Electronic mail is the most widely used networked application, but the standard SMTP protocol transmits messages in **plaintext**, exposing content, sender identity, and attachments to interception, forgery, and tampering. **Email security** is therefore the application of cryptographic services — **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** — directly to user mail. Two industry-standard frameworks dominate this domain: **PGP (Pretty Good Privacy)** and **S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic tests your ability to describe the operational model of PGP (key rings, message format, services) and S/MIME (MIME structure, cryptographic choices, certificate hierarchy) and to critically compare both schemes. Marks are typically split evenly between **description (7 marks)** and **comparison / analysis (7 marks)**.

## 1.2 PGP — Pretty Good Privacy

**Formal Definition:**
PGP is a **de-facto standard, application-layer email-security protocol** introduced by **Phil Zimmermann in 1991**. It provides confidentiality and authentication by combining **symmetric-key encryption (for speed), public-key encryption (for key exchange), hashing, and digital signatures** into a single, portable message format. PGP is governed by the open standard **OpenPGP (RFC 4880)** and uses a **web-of-trust** model for key validation rather than a central certificate authority.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Triple-Sealed Envelope":**
Imagine you want to post a confidential letter to a friend:
- You first **sign** the letter with your unique seal (digital signature — proves it's really from you).
- You then fold the letter and place it inside an **envelope that only your friend can open** (encryption with the recipient's public key — confidentiality).
- Finally, the post office forces you to write the address in a special "wide-stripes" alphabet (Radix-64 encoding) so the letter survives any sorting machine intact (email compatibility).
PGP performs exactly these three layered operations: **sign → compress → encrypt → encode**.

## 1.3 S/MIME — Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions

**Formal Definition:**
S/MIME is an **IETF-standardized (RFC 5751) application-layer protocol** for securing MIME-format email. It provides **digital signatures, message encryption, integrity, and non-repudiation** using a **strict X.509 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** with Certificate Authorities (CAs). S/MIME is bundled into most commercial enterprise mail clients (Outlook, Apple Mail, Thunderbird) and is the dominant corporate standard, whereas PGP dominates among privacy-focused individual users and journalists.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Government Notarized Courier":**
If PGP is like sealing a letter with your own stamp and trusting friends-of-friends to vouch for you, S/MIME is like a registered post service: every sender's identity is **verified by a government-issued digital ID card (X.509 certificate)** signed by a **trusted Central Authority (the CA)**. Anyone receiving the letter can check the ID card against the CA's records before trusting the content.

## 1.4 Core Services Provided by Both Schemes

Both PGP and S/MIME offer the four foundational security services listed in the table below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the four services — "CAIN":** **C**onfidentiality, **A**uthentication, **I**ntegrity, **N**on-repudiation.

| Service | Mechanism | Threat Defended |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | Symmetric encryption (IDEA / 3DES / AES) with session key wrapped by RSA | Eavesdropping |
| Authentication | Digital signature (RSA / DSS / ECDSA) on hash (SHA-1 / SHA-2) | Forgery, impersonation |
| Integrity | Cryptographic hash function (MD5 / SHA family) | Tampering |
| Non-repudiation | Sender's private key signs the hash | Sender denial |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five PGP Services

PGP deliberately combines **five** services in a single processing pipeline. Understanding the order of these services is the single most-tested concept in KTU exams.

1. **Authentication** — Sender creates a hash of the message and signs it with their **private key**.
2. **Confidentiality** — Sender encrypts the message body with a **one-time random 128-bit symmetric session key** (IDEA / 3DES / CAST / AES).
3. **Compression** — The (signed) message is compressed using **ZIP (Lempel-Ziv)** to save bandwidth. Compression is applied **after signing but before encryption** to strengthen the cipher against known-plaintext attacks.
4. **Email Compatibility** — The binary ciphertext is encoded using **Radix-64 (Base64)** so that only printable ASCII characters traverse SMTP mail servers.
5. **Segmentation & Reassembly** — To comply with SMTP size limits (traditionally **\leq 50,000 octets**), large PGP messages are split and reassembled transparently by the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical order rule (very high KTU yield):** Signature is computed on the **plaintext hash** → message + signature are **compressed** → compressed blob is **encrypted** → ciphertext is **Base64-encoded** for transmission. Reversing the order of compression and encryption destroys security (compression of ciphertext is ineffective, encryption of compressed text leaks redundant patterns).

## 2.2 PGP Key Rings — The Heart of PGP

Each PGP user maintains two local data structures:

- **Private-Key Ring:** stores the user's own private keys, indexed by **Key ID**, plus a timestamp and the user ID. Each row is encrypted with a passphrase-derived key (a hash of the passphrase).
- **Public-Key Ring:** stores public keys of correspondents, indexed by **Key ID**, with fields for **Owner Trust, Key Legitimacy, Signature(s), and Owner Trust bytes**.

The **Key ID** is the **least-significant 64 bits** of the public key. It is short enough to be transmitted in an email signature line, yet long enough (\approx 1.8 \times 10^{19} possibilities) to make collisions negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> The signature field in a PGP public-key certificate is not issued by a CA but by **introducers** — other PGP users who have signed that key. This is the **web of trust**: if A trusts B, and B signed C's key, A may transitively trust C (configurable trust levels: *unknown, never, marginal, complete, ultimate*).

## 2.3 PGP Message Transmission and Reception

**Transmission pipeline** (sender side):

1. $M \xrightarrow{\text{SHA-1}} H(M)$
2. $H(M) \xrightarrow{\text{sign with } K_R^{s}} S$ (signature)
3. $M \,\vert\vert\, S \xrightarrow{\text{ZIP}} C_1$
4. $C_1 \xrightarrow{\text{IDEA, session key } K_S} C_2$
5. $K_S \xrightarrow{\text{RSA with } K_U^{r}} \text{Enc}(K_S)$
6. Transmit $\text{Enc}(K_S) \,\vert\vert\, C_2$ after **Radix-64** encoding.

**Reception pipeline** (receiver side) is the exact inverse.

## 2.4 S/MIME Architecture

S/MIME builds on the **MIME (RFC 2045–2049)** specification, which extends the format of email messages to support text in character sets other than ASCII, non-text attachments, and multipart message bodies.

### 2.4.1 MIME Structure

A MIME entity has three parts: **MIME header + Content-Type + Body**. Critical content types used by S/MIME:

| Content-Type | Description | S/MIME Class |
|---|---|---|
| `multipart/signed` | Clear-signed data; signature separate from text | signedData (clear) |
| `application/pkcs7-mime` | Wrapped signature (opaque) | signedData (enveloped) |
| `application/pkcs7-mime` with smime-type=enveloped-data | Encrypted | envelopedData |
| `multipart/signed` with `application/pkcs7-signature` | Detached signature | signedData |

### 2.4.2 S/MIME Functions (Four Modes)

S/MIME defines four content types under the CMS (Cryptographic Message Syntax, RFC 5652):

1. **envelopedData** — encrypted with recipient's public key (RSAES-OAEP, ECIES, etc.); provides **confidentiality**.
2. **signedData** — message digest signed with sender's private key; provides **authentication + integrity + non-repudiation**.
3. **clear-signedData** — signature only, content transmitted in clear (for readers without S/MIME).
4. **signedAndEnvelopedData** — both signing and encryption applied (rare, deprecated in modern profiles).

### 2.4.3 S/MIME Cryptographic Choices (RFC 5754 / RFC 5753)

| Function | Modern Algorithm |
|---|---|
| Asymmetric | RSA (\geq 2048-bit) or ECC (P-256, P-384) |
| Symmetric | AES-128 / AES-256 in CBC or GCM mode |
| Hash | SHA-256 (mandatory) / SHA-384 / SHA-512 |
| Key wrap | RSAES-OAEP (PKCS \#1 v2) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Radix-64 block size | $n$ bytes $\to \lceil 4n/3 \rceil$ output characters | Email compatibility encoding |
| Key ID derivation | $\text{KeyID} = \text{publicKey} \bmod 2^{64}$ | Identifying a key in a signature line |
| PGP packet integrity check | $H(M) = \text{verify}(S, K_U^{s})$ | Verifying sender's signature |
| Session key reuse | One-time per message, **never reused** | Prevents known-plaintext attacks |
| Hash length (SHA-1) | $\vert H \vert = 160$ bits | Signature size = 1024 bits (RSA) |
| Block size (IDEA) | $64$ bits | CBC mode encryption |
| AES block size | $128$ bits | CBC mode encryption |
| S/MIME canonicalization | CRLF line endings, **quoted-printable** base encoding | Format normalization before sign/verify |

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Industry

- **PGP** is mandated by the **GDPR data-minimization principle** for journalist–source confidentiality (used by The Guardian, NYT secure tip-lines).
- **S/MIME** is mandatory for **HIPAA-compliant medical email**, **FedRAMP** government email, and **enterprise B2B EDI** workflows.
- The **PGP/MIME vs S/MIME choice** in production depends on whether the organization runs an internal CA (choose S/MIME) or wishes to avoid CA infrastructure costs (choose PGP).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PGP Message Generation — Exhaustive Derivation

Let $A$ be Alice (sender) and $B$ be Bob (receiver). Alice has private key $K_{R}^{A}$ and public key $K_{U}^{A}$; Bob has $K_{R}^{B}, K_{U}^{B}$. Let $M$ be the plaintext message and $K_{S}$ the one-time session key.

**Step 1 — Hash the message.**
Alice computes a fixed-length digest of the message using SHA-1 (modern: SHA-256).

$$
H = \text{SHA-}1(M), \quad \vert H \vert = 160 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Sign the hash (authentication, non-repudiation).**
Alice encrypts the hash with her private key using RSA.

$$
S = E_{K_{R}^{A}}(H) = H^{d} \bmod n
$$

The output $S$ is a **signature packet** of length equal to the RSA modulus (e.g., 1024 or 2048 bits).

**Step 3 — Concatenate message and signature.**

$$
P = M \,\vert\vert\, S
$$

**Step 4 — Compress (bandwidth efficiency + pre-encryption redundancy removal).**

$$
Z = \text{ZIP}(P)
$$

ZIP exploits repeated byte patterns. After this step, common patterns in $M$ and the header fields of $S$ are removed.

**Step 5 — Generate a one-time symmetric session key.**

$$
K_{S} \leftarrow \text{CSPRNG}() \quad \text{with} \quad \vert K_{S} \vert = 128 \text{ bits}
$$

CSPRNG = Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator (e.g., `/dev/urandom`, Yarrow, Fortuna).

**Step 6 — Encrypt the compressed blob with $K_S$ using a block cipher (IDEA / 3DES / CAST5 / AES).**

$$
C = E_{K_{S}}^{\text{IDEA}}(Z)
$$

IDEA operates in **CFB (Cipher Feedback)** mode by default, allowing stream-cipher-like handling of arbitrary-length messages.

**Step 7 — Encrypt the session key with Bob's public RSA key (key transport).**

$$
K_{S}^{\text{enc}} = E_{K_{U}^{B}}(K_{S}) = K_{S}^{e} \bmod n_{B}
$$

**Step 8 — Prepend the encrypted session key to the ciphertext.**

$$
D = K_{S}^{\text{enc}} \,\vert\vert\, C
$$

**Step 9 — Apply Radix-64 encoding for email compatibility.**

$$
T = \text{Radix64}(D), \quad T \in \text{printable ASCII}
$$

SMTP gateways reject bytes with values 0–31 and 128–255; Radix-64 maps every 6-bit block to one of 64 printable characters from the set `A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /`, padding with `=`. The expansion factor is exactly $4/3$.

**Step 10 — Wrap with PGP packet headers and transmit.**

The complete transmitted object contains a series of PGP packets:
- **Public-Key Encrypted Session Key Packet** (tag 1)
- **Signature Packet** (tag 2)
- **Compressed Data Packet** (tag 8)
- **(Literal Data Packet inside, tag 11, after decompression on receiver)**

## 3.2 PGP Message Reception — Exhaustive Inverse Derivation

Bob receives $T$ and applies the inverse operations:

**Step 1 — Radix-64 decode.**

$$
D = \text{Radix64}^{-1}(T)
$$

**Step 2 — Separate encrypted session key from ciphertext.**

$$
K_{S}^{\text{enc}}, C \leftarrow \text{split}(D)
$$

**Step 3 — Decrypt the session key with Bob's private key.**

$$
K_{S} = D_{K_{R}^{B}}(K_{S}^{\text{enc}}) = (K_{S}^{e})^{d} \bmod n_{B}
$$

**Step 4 — Decrypt the ciphertext with $K_S$ (IDEA / AES).**

$$
Z = D_{K_{S}}^{\text{IDEA}}(C)
$$

**Step 5 — Decompress.**

$$
P = \text{ZIP}^{-1}(Z)
$$

**Step 6 — Separate message and signature.**

$$
M, S \leftarrow \text{split}(P)
$$

**Step 7 — Verify signature using Alice's public key.**

$$
H' = D_{K_{U}^{A}}(S), \quad H = \text{SHA-}1(M)
$$

If $H' = H$, the signature is valid. If $H' \neq H$, the message has been tampered with or the signature is forged.

**Step 8 — Deliver $M$ to Bob's MUA (mail user agent).**

## 3.3 Radix-64 Encoding — Worked Numerical Example

Encode the 3-byte input `0x4D 0x61 0x6E` (ASCII `Man`).

**Step 1 — Concatenate bits.**

$$
01001101 \; 01100001 \; 01101110
$$

Total = 24 bits.

**Step 2 — Split into 6-bit groups.**

$$
010011 \; 010110 \; 000101 \; 101110
$$

**Step 3 — Convert each 6-bit group to its Radix-64 index.**

$$
010011_2 = 19, \quad 010110_2 = 22, \quad 000101_2 = 5, \quad 101110_2 = 46
$$

**Step 4 — Map indices to the Radix-64 alphabet.**
Alphabet = `ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789+/`

$$
19 \to T, \quad 22 \to W, \quad 5 \to F, \quad 46 \to u
$$

**Step 5 — Result.**

$$
\text{Man} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{TWFu}
$$

A 3-byte input always produces 4 output characters — a **33% expansion** — which is why PGP adds the optional segmentation step.

## 3.4 PGP vs S/MIME — Comparative Analytical Matrix

This is the single highest-yield comparative table for KTU 2024 questions. Memorize the contrasts.

| Dimension | PGP | S/MIME |
|---|---|---|
| Standardization | OpenPGP (RFC 4880) | IETF S/MIME (RFC 5751) over CMS (RFC 5652) |
| Certificate model | **Web of Trust** (self-signed + introducer signatures) | **X.509 hierarchical PKI** with CA |
| Key management | Decentralized; PGP key servers (e.g., keys.openpgp.org) | Centralized; corporate CA, Active Directory, SCEP |
| Primary use | Personal, journalist, open-source developer, privacy | Enterprise, government, healthcare, legal, B2B |
| Algorithm binding | Per-message, embedded in packet header | Per-recipient, declared in CMS attributes |
| Format | OpenPGP packet stream, Radix-64 wrapped | Standard MIME; CMS object as `application/pkcs7-mime` |
| Transmission overhead | Lower (smaller headers) | Higher (ASN.1 DER encoding) |
| Deployment cost | Free, no infrastructure | Requires CA, certificate lifecycle, CRL/OCSP |
| Default key size | 2048–4096-bit RSA or Ed25519 | 2048-bit RSA or P-256 ECC (FIPS 140-2 mandated) |
| Support in clients | Thunderbird (Enigmail/GnuPG), K-9 Mail, ProtonMail | Native in Outlook, Apple Mail, Gmail (since 2018) |
| Trust bootstrapping | Out-of-band fingerprint verification | Automatic via trusted root CA store |
| Revocation mechanism | Own key revocation certificate, keyserver push | CRL distribution point or OCSP responder |
| Confidentiality algorithm | IDEA, 3DES, CAST5, AES, Twofish, Camellia | AES-128/256 CBC/GCM, 3DES (legacy) |
| Signature algorithm | RSA, DSA, ElGamal, EdDSA (Ed25519/Ed448) | RSA-PKCS1-v1_5, RSA-PSS, ECDSA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PGP Message Generation Flow (Sender Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plaintext Message M] --> B[Compute Hash H = SHA-1 of M]
    B --> C[Sign Hash with Sender Private Key KRs]
    C --> D[Signature Packet S = D KRs of H]
    D --> E[Concatenate M and S]
    E --> F[Compress using ZIP Algorithm]
    F --> G[Generate One Time Session Key Ks via CSPRNG]
    G --> H[Encrypt Compressed Data with Ks using IDEA or AES]
    H --> I[Encrypt Session Key Ks with Recipient Public Key KUr using RSA]
    I --> J[Prepend Encrypted Ks to Ciphertext]
    J --> K[Apply Radix-64 Encoding for SMTP Compatibility]
    K --> L[Wrap in OpenPGP Packet Headers]
    L --> M[End: Transmit over Email Network]
```

## 4.2 PGP Message Reception Flow (Receiver Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Received Base64 String T] --> B[Radix-64 Decode to Binary D]
    B --> C[Split D into Encrypted Ks and Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Decrypt Ks using Recipient Private Key KRr RSA]
    D --> E[Decrypt C using Ks with IDEA or AES]
    E --> F[Decompress using ZIP Inverse]
    F --> G[Split into Plaintext M and Signature S]
    G --> H[Recompute Hash H1 of M with SHA-1]
    H --> I[Decrypt Signature S using Sender Public Key KUs to get H2]
    I --> J{Compare H1 and H2}
    J -->|Equal| K[End: Message Authentic and Intact]
    J -->|Not Equal| L[End: Tampering Detected Reject Message]
```

## 4.3 S/MIME Message Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer MUA] --> B[MIME Entity Construction]
    B --> C[Content Type Selection]
    C --> D1[Signed Data multipart signed]
    C --> D2[Enveloped Data application pkcs7 mime]
    C --> D3[Clear Signed Data multipart signed]
    C --> D4[Compressed Data application pkcs7 mime]
    D1 --> E[CMS Cryptographic Message Syntax Engine]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[CMS Operations Hashing RSA Signature AES Encryption]
    F --> G[ASN.1 DER Encoding of CMS Object]
    G --> H[SMTP Transport via Base64 or Binary MIME]
    H --> I[Receiver MUA with X.509 Trust Store]
    I --> J[Certificate Validation CRL or OCSP]
    J --> K[CMS Decode and Verify]
    K --> L[Display Decrypted Authenticated Content]
```

## 4.4 PGP Key Ring Data Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrivateKeyRing[Private Key Ring Local Secret Storage]
        PK1[Timestamp]
        PK2[Key ID 64 bit]
        PK3[Public Key Material]
        PK4[Encrypted Private Key with Passphrase Hash]
        PK5[User ID email]
    end
    subgraph PublicKeyRing[Public Key Ring Local Public Storage]
        PB1[Timestamp]
        PB2[Key ID 64 bit]
        PB3[Public Key Material]
        PB4[Owner Trust Byte]
        PB5[Key Legitimacy]
        PB6[Signature Certificates]
        PB7[User ID]
    end
    PrivateKeyRing --> MSG[PGP Message Processing Engine]
    PublicKeyRing --> MSG
    MSG --> OP1[Sign Operation]
    MSG --> OP2[Verify Operation]
    MSG --> OP3[Encrypt Operation]
    MSG --> OP4[Decrypt Operation]
```

## 4.5 PGP vs S/MIME — Trust Model Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PGPModel[PGP Web of Trust Model]
        A1[Alice signs Bob] --> A2[Bob signs Carol]
        A2 --> A3[Alice transitively trusts Carol if configured]
        A4[Charlie signs David] --> A3
    end
    subgraph SMIMEModel[S/MIME X.509 Hierarchical Model]
        B1[Root CA] --> B2[Intermediate CA]
        B2 --> B3[Issuing CA]
        B3 --> B4[End Entity Certificate for User]
        B4 --> B5[Verified via CRL or OCSP]
    end
    PGPModel --> S1[No central authority required]
    SMIMEModel --> S2[Centralized trust chain required]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three PGP services and explain how confidentiality is achieved in PGP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PGP provides five services: **authentication, confidentiality, compression, email compatibility, and segmentation** (½ mark for listing three correctly).

**Confidentiality in PGP** is achieved through a hybrid (two-layer) scheme (1 mark):

1. The compressed message is encrypted with a **one-time symmetric session key $K_S$** (IDEA / 3DES / AES) of 128 bits generated by a CSPRNG. Symmetric encryption is used because it is **100–1000× faster** than public-key encryption for bulk data (½ mark).
2. The session key $K_S$ is then encrypted with the **recipient's public key $K_U^r$** using RSA, producing a small (~256-byte) key-transport packet prepended to the ciphertext. Only the recipient, holding the matching private key, can recover $K_S$ (½ mark).
3. The session key is used for **one message only** and discarded, preventing known-plaintext attacks across messages (½ mark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the role of Radix-64 encoding in PGP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SMTP mail transport is restricted to **7-bit ASCII printable characters**; raw binary ciphertext contains bytes that gateways may silently strip or mangle (1 mark).

**Radix-64 (Base64)** converts every 3-byte (24-bit) block of ciphertext into **4 printable ASCII characters** by mapping each 6-bit chunk to one of 64 characters from the alphabet `A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /`, padding with `=` (1 mark). The result is a 33% expansion, but it guarantees the message survives unmodified through any SMTP relay (½ mark).

In PGP, Radix-64 is applied **after** symmetric encryption, as the very last step before transmission, and is **inverted first** on reception. The inverse, **Radix-64 decoding**, recovers the original ciphertext byte stream (½ mark).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — PGP-Focused

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper PECDT412]**  
> *(a)* Describe the PGP message generation and reception processes with neat block diagrams. **\[7 marks\]**  
> *(b)* Explain the structure of PGP key rings and the role of the web of trust. **\[7 marks\]**

#### (a) PGP Message Generation and Reception — Model Solution

**Message Generation (Sender Alice → Receiver Bob):** (3½ marks)

1. **Authentication** — Alice computes $H = \text{SHA-}1(M)$ and signs it with her private key: $S = D_{K_R^A}(H)$. This produces the **Signature Packet (PGP tag 2)**.
2. **Compression** — The concatenation $M \,\vert\vert\, S$ is compressed using ZIP into $Z$. This reduces the data sent over the wire and removes redundancy that could weaken the symmetric cipher.
3. **Confidentiality** — A fresh 128-bit session key $K_S$ is generated. The compressed data is encrypted: $C = E_{K_S}^{\text{IDEA}}(Z)$. Then $K_S$ is encrypted with Bob's public key: $K_S^{\text{enc}} = E_{K_U^B}(K_S)$.
4. **Email compatibility** — The binary blob $K_S^{\text{enc}} \,\vert\vert\, C$ is encoded in Radix-64, then framed with PGP packet headers (tag 1 — public-key encrypted session key) and sent.

**Message Reception (Bob):** (3½ marks)

1. Radix-64 decoding recovers the binary blob.
2. Bob's private key decrypts $K_S$ from the key packet.
3. $K_S$ decrypts the ciphertext to recover the compressed signed message.
4. ZIP decompression produces $M$ and $S$.
5. Bob recomputes SHA-1 on $M$ and compares with $D_{K_U^A}(S)$. Equality proves authentication and integrity.

**Block Diagram:** (already drawn in SECTION 4.1 and 4.2 — refer to those schematics; 0 marks for diagram itself but it earns the presentation credit that examiners look for).

#### (b) PGP Key Rings and the Web of Trust — Model Solution

**Private-Key Ring:** (2 marks)
A local table indexed by **Key ID (least-significant 64 bits of public key)**. Each row stores the **timestamp, public-key material, encrypted private key, and user ID**. The private key is stored encrypted under a key derived from a user-chosen passphrase via repeated hashing (e.g., iterated SHA-1), so that stealing the file is insufficient to recover the key.

**Public-Key Ring:** (2 marks)
A local table of all public keys Alice has encountered. Each row contains **timestamp, Key ID, public-key material, user ID, owner-trust byte, key-legitimacy byte, and zero or more signature packets** (introducer certificates).

**Web of Trust:** (3 marks)
- PGP has no central CA. Trust is established **transitively** through **introducers** — other PGP users who have signed a public key with their own private key.
- A user assigns one of five trust levels to each introducer: *unknown, never, marginal, complete, ultimate*.
- The PGP algorithm computes a **key-legitimacy score** for each public key in the ring: a key is considered valid if it has been signed by at least one *ultimate* introducer, or by **two marginal** introducers, or by **one complete** introducer.
- This model scales well for small communities and open-source projects but fails to scale to large enterprises where a single, auditable root is preferred (which is why S/MIME was developed).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — S/MIME-Focused (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Suggested Question]**  
> *(a)* Explain the four content types of S/MIME and how they map to MIME content types. **\[7 marks\]**  
> *(b)* Compare PGP and S/MIME in terms of key management, trust model, and typical use cases. **\[7 marks\]**

#### (a) S/MIME Four Content Types — Model Solution

S/MIME uses **Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS, RFC 5652)** to define four content types (1 mark for naming them correctly):

1. **envelopedData** (2 marks) — Provides **confidentiality** only. The CMS object contains the encrypted content (encrypted with a content-encryption key, e.g., AES-128-CBC) and the **content-encryption key encrypted once for each recipient** using their public key (RSAES-OAEP). MIME mapping: `application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=enveloped-data`.

2. **signedData** (2 marks) — Provides **authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation**. The CMS object contains the message digest, the signer's certificate, and the encrypted hash (signature) of the content. MIME mapping: `application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=signed-data`. (The body may be omitted if a separate `multipart/signed` part is used.)

3. **clear-signedData** (1 mark) — Same as signedData but the **content is transmitted in plaintext** alongside the signature, so readers without S/MIME can still read the message. MIME mapping: `multipart/signed` with the original content as one part and `application/pkcs7-signature` as the other.

4. **signedAndEnvelopedData** (1 mark) — Both signing and encryption applied. Now deprecated by RFC 5751 because the operations are not commutative and order ambiguity caused interoperability problems.

#### (b) PGP vs S/MIME Comparison — Model Solution

| Dimension | PGP (3½ marks) | S/MIME (3½ marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Management** | Self-managed key pairs; published on decentralized keyservers (SKS, keys.openpgp.org). Users sign each other's keys out-of-band. | Managed by an organizational CA; keys are X.509 certificates with chain to a trusted root. |
| **Trust Model** | **Web of trust** — trust is transitive, decentralized, and quantified (marginal/complete/ultimate). | **Hierarchical PKI** — trust flows strictly from a single root CA downwards; no transitive trust between peers. |
| **Use Cases** | Personal privacy, journalism, whistleblowing, open-source developer PGP-commit signing (e.g., Linux kernel, Debian), encrypted backup. | Enterprise email (Outlook, Gmail S/MIME since 2018), HIPAA healthcare messaging, government and military (NSA Mode 1), legal signed contracts, B2B purchase orders. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Reversing the PGP service order** — writing "encrypt → compress → sign" instead of "sign → compress → encrypt". This single mistake costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the web of trust with a chain of trust** — these are diametrically opposite models. PGP is decentralized; S/MIME is hierarchical.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention that compression happens *after* signing but *before* encryption** — this is the only mathematically correct order.
> 4. **Stating "PGP uses certificates" without qualifying** — PGP has *self-signed certificates*, not X.509 certificates. S/MIME uses X.509.
> 5. **Omitting the role of one-time session keys** — the one-time nature is what prevents cross-message known-plaintext attacks and earns you the "depth" mark.
> 6. **Mixing up MIME content types** — `multipart/signed` is for clear-signing; `application/pkcs7-mime` with `smime-type=enveloped-data` is for encryption; both may appear but they are not interchangeable.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ PGP = **Pretty Good Privacy**, OpenPGP RFC 4880, introduced by **Phil Zimmermann (1991)**.
- ✅ PGP combines **5 services**: authentication, confidentiality, compression, email compatibility, segmentation.
- ✅ **Correct order of PGP operations:** Sign → Compress → Encrypt → Radix-64 encode.
- ✅ **Hash-then-sign** pattern: $S = D_{K_R}(H(M))$; verify by $H' = D_{K_U}(S)$.
- ✅ **One-time session key** for confidentiality; never reused; protects against known-plaintext attacks.
- ✅ **Key ID = least-significant 64 bits** of the public key.
- ✅ PGP uses **web of trust**; S/MIME uses **X.509 hierarchical PKI**.
- ✅ **S/MIME = RFC 5751** over **CMS (RFC 5652)**, an IETF standard.
- ✅ S/MIME four content types: **envelopedData, signedData, clear-signedData, signedAndEnvelopedData** (last deprecated).
- ✅ S/MIME cryptographic stack: **AES** for symmetric, **RSA-OAEP or ECC** for asymmetric, **SHA-256** for hashing.
- ✅ **Radix-64 (Base64)** = 3 bytes → 4 printable ASCII chars = **33% expansion**; needed because SMTP is 7-bit ASCII.
- ✅ **MIME** = the base format S/MIME rides on; S/MIME wraps CMS objects in MIME content types.
- ✅ PGP = personal, decentralized, free, web-of-trust; S/MIME = enterprise, CA-based, FIPS-compliant, hierarchical.
- ✅ Default algorithms in modern S/MIME: **AES-128/256-CBC or GCM** + **RSA-2048 or P-256 ECC** + **SHA-256**.
- ✅ Default algorithms in modern PGP: **AES-256** + **RSA-2048/4096 or Ed25519** + **SHA-256**.
