- ✅ **PGP packet tags to know**: 1 (key-enc-session-key), 2 (signature), 8 (compressed), 11 (literal data).
- ✅ In S/MIME, **canonicalization** (CRLF line endings, no trailing whitespace) is essential *before* signing to avoid verification failures across platforms.
- ✅ **Key revocation** in PGP = publish a signed revocation certificate to a keyserver; in S/MIME = CRL publication or OCSP responder update by the CA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Security Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Network Security Principles** refers to the foundational rules, models, services, and mechanisms defined under the **OSI Security Architecture (ISO/IEC 7498-2)** and the **TCP/IP Security framework (RFC 1122, RFC 4301 — Security Architecture for the Internet Protocol)** that govern the protection of data during its transit across untrusted communication channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT412 — Module 3):**
> Students are expected to understand the difference between *security attacks*, *security services*, and *security mechanisms*, and to map them onto real-world network protocols such as IPsec, TLS, and SSL.

Formally, the discipline is the study of:
- **Threat Modeling:** Identification of adversarial capabilities against the CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) triad.
- **Countermeasure Design:** Deployment of cryptographic, procedural, and architectural controls to neutralize threats.
- **Assurance:** Continuous verification that controls meet stated security policy requirements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Armored Courier" Model

Imagine you are sending a sealed, tamper-proof envelope containing a confidential legal document through a crowded public bus network.

- The **envelope itself** is a *security mechanism* (encryption + integrity seal).
- The **guarantee that nobody read the contents** is a *security service* (confidentiality).
- The **proof that the sender actually sent it** is another *security service* (non-repudiation).
- The **pickpocket on the bus** is a *passive attack* (traffic analysis).
- The **criminal who rips open the envelope and rewrites the contract** is an *active attack* (modification).

**Network Security Principles** is essentially the rulebook the postal system adopts to make sure the envelope, the courier, the route, and the locks all work together to defeat both pickpockets and forgers.

## 1.3 Core Pillars of the Discipline

> [!NOTE]
> **The OSI 7498-2 Triad** — KTU frequently tests these three categories as a unified concept:

| Pillar | Definition | Example in Practice |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Attack** | Any action that compromises the security of information | Sniffing, replay, DoS |
| **Security Service** | A processing or communication service that enhances the security of data | Confidentiality, Authentication |
| **Security Mechanism** | A mechanism designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack | Encipherment, Digital Signatures |

## 1.4 Standard Security Metrics

- **Key Length:** AES-128, AES-256 (measured in **bits**).
- **Hash Output Size:** SHA-256 produces **256 bits** of digest.
- **Authentication Latency:** Typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit):** Standard Ethernet **1500 bytes**.
- **Time-to-Live (TTL):** Default value **64** hops for Linux systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI Security Architecture — X.800 Framework

The **ITU-T X.800 standard** (functionally identical to ISO 7498-2) defines a layered, modular view of network security. KTU questions often ask: *"Classify the following into attack/service/mechanism."* Memorize the classification tree below.

### 2.1.1 Taxonomy of Security Attacks

A **security attack** is any deliberate attempt to violate one or more security services. The X.800 model bifurcates attacks into two principal branches:

**A. Passive Attacks (Eavesdropping Category)**
- Goal: Learn information without altering system state.
- Detection: Extremely difficult (the system continues to function normally).
- Mitigation: Encryption-based confidentiality services.

**B. Active Attacks (Disruption Category)**
- Goal: Alter system resources or affect their operation.
- Detection: Easier than passive (anomalies become visible).
- Mitigation: Integrity, authentication, and access control services.

### 2.1.2 The Five Canonical Security Services (X.800)

1. **Data Confidentiality** — Protects against passive eavesdropping.
2. **Data Integrity** — Guarantees messages are not altered, inserted, deleted, or replayed.
3. **Authentication** — Confirms the identity of the communicating entity.
4. **Non-Repudiation** — Prevents sender/receiver from denying a transmitted message.
5. **Access Control** — Restricts resource usage to authorized entities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Availability** is sometimes added as a sixth service (CIA + A model), but in strict X.800 terminology, availability is considered a *reliability* concern handled by fault-tolerance rather than a security service per se.

### 2.1.3 The Eight Specific Security Mechanisms (X.800)

| # | Mechanism | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Hides data using mathematical transformation |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | Binds identity to a document cryptographically |
| 3 | **Access Control** | Enforces permission rules (RBAC, ACL, MAC) |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Detects bit-level changes in payload |
| 5 | **Authentication Exchange** | Verifies identity via challenge-response |
| 6 | **Traffic Padding** | Defeats traffic analysis by sending dummy packets |
| 7 | **Routing Control** | Selects secure physical/logical paths |
| 8 | **Notarization** | Uses a trusted third party to certify exchanges |

## 2.2 TCP/IP Security Model — Mapping to Layers

The TCP/IP model is the de-facto Internet architecture. Each layer introduces its own attack surface and corresponding defense:

| TCP/IP Layer | Security Protocol(s) | Attacks Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | TLS 1.3, S/MIME, PGP, SSH | Phishing, MITM, application-layer DDoS |
| **Transport** | TLS/SSL, DTLS | TCP session hijacking, SYN flood |
| **Network (Internet)** | IPsec (ESP/AH) | IP spoofing, route hijacking, sniffing |
| **Link (Network Access)** | 802.1X, WPA3, MACsec | ARP poisoning, evil twin, packet sniffing |
| **Physical** | TEMPEST, fiber tapping mitigation | Wiretapping, electromagnetic eavesdropping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** IPsec operates at the **Network Layer** and can secure any application transparently, whereas TLS operates at the **Transport Layer** and is application-aware. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use LaTeX-rendered math. Pipe characters inside math are written as `\vert` to avoid table collisions.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units / Boundary | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H$ (Hash Output) | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | $n = 256$ bits for SHA-256 | Message digests, integrity |
| $C = E_K(P)$ | Ciphertext from encryption | $K$ is the key, length in bits | Confidentiality service |
| $P = D_K(C)$ | Plaintext recovery | Requires same or paired key | Decryption side |
| $\text{Auth}_{MAC}(M)$ | $T = \text{MAC}(K, M)$ | Tag length $t \geq 64$ bits | Integrity + authentication |
| $\text{Entropy } H(X)$ | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Measured in **bits** | Measures key uncertainty |
| $\text{Availability } A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Expressed as a ratio $\in [0, 1]$ | DoS resilience metric |
| $P(\text{collision})$ | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/2^{n+1}}$ (birthday bound) | $n$ = hash bits | For SHA-256: $P < 10^{-18}$ |
| $\text{RSA security}$ | $\vert p \cdot q \vert \geq 2048$ bits | NIST minimum | Key size selection |

## 2.4 Why These Principles Matter in Engineering

In real deployments, the layered design of X.800 means that *no single mechanism provides complete security*. A secure system applies **defense in depth** — multiple, independent layers so that a breach in one does not collapse the whole system. This is why modern systems stack firewalls, IDS, TLS, IPsec, and application-layer authentication simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The "principle of least privilege" and "fail-safe defaults" are not just abstract rules — they are codified in standards like **NIST SP 800-53** and **ISO 27001**, both of which are referenced in KTU supplementary reading lists.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Attacks → Services → Mechanisms

The following matrix is a high-yield KTU mapping tool. The diagonal shows which mechanism best mitigates each attack.

| Attack → | Confidentiality | Integrity | Authentication | Non-Repudiation | Access Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Release of Message Contents** | ✔ Encipherment | — | — | — | — |
| **Traffic Analysis** | ✔ Traffic Padding | — | — | — | — | 
| **Masquerade** | — | — | ✔ Authentication Exchange | ✔ Digital Signature | ✔ ACLs |
| **Replay** | — | ✔ Sequence Numbers | ✔ Timestamps | — | — |
| **Modification of Messages** | — | ✔ Hash / MAC | — | — | — |
| **Denial of Service** | — | — | — | — | ✔ Rate Limiting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap Question:** Notice that *Encryption alone* does **not** provide integrity. An adversary can flip ciphertext bits, producing garbage on decryption but still altering the message. This is why AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) modes like AES-GCM are mandatory in TLS 1.3.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivation: Hash Collision Probability

This is a standard KTU Part B 14-mark derivation question. Let us derive the **birthday-bound probability of collision** for an n-bit hash function.

**Given:**
- A hash function with output length $n$ bits.
- A set of $k$ randomly chosen messages.
- The goal: find the approximate probability that at least two messages share the same digest.

**Step 1 — Define the sample space.**
The total number of possible digests is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 2^{n} \\
\text{(for SHA-256, } N &= 2^{256}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that no two digests match (the "birthday paradox").**
The probability of *no collision* in $k$ draws is approximated by the exponential:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{no collision}) &\approx e^{-k(k-1) / (2 \cdot 2^{n})} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We simplify by assuming $k \gg 1$, so $k(k-1) \approx k^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{no collision}) &\approx e^{-k^{2} / (2^{n+1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of at least one collision.**
Apply the complement rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{collision}) &= 1 - P(\text{no collision}) \\
&= 1 - e^{-k^{2} / 2^{n+1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the "50% collision threshold."**
Set $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5 &= 1 - e^{-k^{2} / 2^{n+1}} \\
e^{-k^{2} / 2^{n+1}} &= 0.5 \\
-k^{2} / 2^{n+1} &= \ln(0.5) = -0.693 \\
k^{2} &= 0.693 \times 2^{n+1} \\
k &= \sqrt{0.693 \times 2^{n+1}} \\
k &\approx 2^{n/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
For an $n$-bit hash, a collision becomes likely after roughly $2^{n/2}$ attempts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SHA-128 (theoretical)}: &\quad k \approx 2^{64} \text{ messages} \\
\text{SHA-256 (modern):} &\quad k \approx 2^{128} \text{ messages}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **MD5 (128-bit)** is now considered cryptographically broken ($2^{64}$ is computationally reachable with modern GPUs), while **SHA-256** remains secure.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Availability Metric Derivation

**Given:** A web server with **MTBF = 720 hours** (one failure per month) and **MTTR = 2 hours** (two hours to restore).

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
A &= \frac{720}{720 + 2} \\
A &= \frac{720}{722} \\
A &= 0.99723
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to the standard "nines" notation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= 99.723\% \\
\text{Downtime per year} &= (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours} \\
&= 0.00277 \times 8760 \\
&\approx 24.3 \text{ hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply KTU's tiered service classification.**

| Availability % | "Nines" | Allowed Downtime/Year |
|---|---|---|
| **99%** | Two nines | 87.6 hours |
| **99.9%** | Three nines | 8.76 hours |
| **99.99%** | Four nines | 52.6 minutes |
| **99.999%** | Five nines | 5.26 minutes |

Our derived $A = 99.723\%$ places the system at just above **two-nines** — adequate for an internal tool, but insufficient for a customer-facing e-commerce platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To move from 99% to 99.999%, you cannot just "buy better hardware." You must redesign for redundancy (active-active clusters, multi-region failover), because MTTR is bounded by the laws of physics and human response time.

## 3.4 Analytical Logic Flow for Replay Attack Mitigation

Replay attacks are a high-frequency KTU topic. The following structured logic traces how the system defeats an adversary re-sending a captured legitimate packet.

1. **Threat:** Adversary captures encrypted packet $C_1 = E_K(P_1 \vert \text{seq}=42)$ and retransmits it later.
2. **Naive defense (FAIL):** Decryption succeeds; receiver accepts the replay. System is compromised.
3. **Sequence number defense:** Receiver tracks the last accepted sequence number; if $42$ is stale, the packet is dropped.
4. **Timestamp defense:** Sender includes a current timestamp $T$; receiver enforces a maximum skew window $\Delta T$ (e.g., ±60 seconds).
5. **Nonce-based defense:** Receiver issues a one-time random number $N_i$ that must appear in the response. Prevents reuse cryptographically.
6. **Combined defense (production-grade):** TLS 1.3 uses **sequence numbers + nonces + AEAD tags** simultaneously, making replay computationally infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Network Security Architecture Flow

The diagram below captures how the X.800 security model maps onto a layered TCP/IP deployment. Each layer introduces a defense, and the overall system achieves **defense in depth**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Application Layer] --> A1[App-Layer Security: TLS 1.3, MFA, OAuth 2.0]
    A1 --> B0[Transport Layer: TCP/UDP]
    B0 --> B1[Transport Security: TLS Handshake, Sequence Numbering]
    B1 --> C0[Network Layer: IP]
    C0 --> C1[Network Security: IPsec ESP, AH, IKEv2]
    C1 --> D0[Link Layer: Ethernet, WiFi]
    D0 --> D1[Link Security: MACsec, WPA3, 802.1X]
    D1 --> E0[Physical Layer]
    E0 --> E1[Physical Security: Faraday Cage, Fiber Optics, TEMPEST]

    A1 -. protects .- X1[Data Confidentiality, App Authentication]
    B1 -. protects .- X2[Session Integrity, MITM Defense]
    C1 -. protects .- X3[End-to-End IP Integrity, Source Auth]
    D1 -. protects .- X4[Local Segment Sniffing, Rogue AP]
    E1 -. protects .- X5[Wiretapping, EM Eavesdropping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted edges (`.->`) represent which **security service** is delivered at each layer. This visual mapping is a standard KTU diagram question.

## 4.2 Attack → Service → Mechanism Resolution Flow

This block-level diagram shows how an incoming security threat is resolved into a defense plan.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Incoming Threat] --> Q1{Active or Passive?}
    Q1 -->|Passive| P1[Release of Contents / Traffic Analysis]
    Q1 -->|Active| P2[Replay / Modification / DoS / Masquerade]

    P1 --> S1[Service: Confidentiality]
    P2 --> S3[Service: Integrity / Authentication / Availability]

    S1 --> M1[Mechanism: Encipherment, Traffic Padding]
    S3 --> M2[Mechanism: MAC, Digital Signature, Nonce, Rate Limiting]

    M1 --> D1[Deployed: AES-256-GCM, SSH Tunnel]
    M2 --> D2[Deployed: HMAC-SHA256, RSA-PSS, SYN Cookies]
```

## 4.3 Key Management Lifecycle Flow

The cornerstone of network security is the **lifecycle of cryptographic keys**. The diagram below traces the states a key passes through from birth to destruction.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Generation
    Generation --> Distribution
    Distribution --> ActiveUse
    ActiveUse --> Rotation
    Rotation --> ActiveUse
    ActiveUse --> Revocation
    Revocation --> Destruction
    Destruction --> [*]

    Generation: 1 Key Generation
    Distribution: 2 Secure Distribution
    ActiveUse: 3 Active Use
    Rotation: 4 Key Rotation
    Revocation: 5 Compromise Revocation
    Destruction: 6 Secure Destruction
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked *"Where is the weakest link in any cryptographic system?"* the answer is almost always **key management**, not the algorithm itself. The famous cryptographer Bruce Schneier has repeatedly stated: *"Attacks always get better; they never get worse."*

## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Concentric Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Core Asset: Database] --> R1[Layer 1: Data Encryption AES-256]
    R1 --> R2[Layer 2: Access Control RBAC, MFA]
    R2 --> R3[Layer 3: Network Firewall, VLAN Segmentation]
    R3 --> R4[Layer 4: Intrusion Detection System IDS]
    R4 --> R5[Layer 5: Physical Access Control, CCTV]
    R5 --> R6[Layer 6: Perimeter Security, DMZ]
    R6 --> R7[External Threat Actor]
```

Each concentric layer assumes the inner layer *will* eventually fail, ensuring the system is never one breach away from total compromise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Distinguish between a passive attack and an active attack. Give one example of each. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **passive attack** attempts to *learn* or make use of information from the system but does *not* affect system resources. It is essentially eavesdropping — the system continues to function normally, making detection extremely difficult. **Example:** A packet sniffer on a WiFi network capturing unencrypted HTTP traffic to harvest credentials.

An **active attack** attempts to *alter* system resources or affect their operation. The adversary modifies, fabricates, or disrupts communication, leaving detectable traces. **Example:** A Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack where the adversary intercepts and modifies the contents of a banking transaction.

**Marking Key:**
- 1 mark — correct definition of passive attack + example
- 1 mark — correct definition of active attack + example
- 1 mark — distinction clearly stated (eavesdropping vs. modification)

---

### Q2. List the five security services defined by the X.800 standard. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **X.800 (OSI Security Architecture)** standard defines the following five security services:

1. **Data Confidentiality** — Protection of data from unauthorized disclosure.
2. **Data Integrity** — Assurance that data has not been altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner.
3. **Authentication** — Confirmation of the identity of a communicating entity.
4. **Non-Repudiation** — Proof that a message was sent/received, preventing denial by either party.
5. **Access Control** — Restriction of access to resources to authorized entities only.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU valuation script also accepts **Availability** as a sixth service under the broader CIA + A model, but the strict X.800 answer lists exactly five.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i) [14 Marks]

#### (a) Explain the OSI Security Architecture (X.800) in detail. List and briefly describe the categories of security attacks and security services. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The X.800 standard, jointly published by **ITU-T** and **ISO** as ISO 7498-2, provides a systematic framework for security in OSI-compliant networks. It addresses three primary concerns: *security attacks*, *security services*, and *security mechanisms*.

**Categories of Security Attacks (3 marks):**

**1. Passive Attacks** — The adversary observes traffic without modifying it.
- **Release of message contents:** Reading confidential emails or files.
- **Traffic analysis:** Inferring patterns (e.g., frequency, origin) even from encrypted flows.
- *Difficulty:* Detection is near-impossible. *Defense:* Encryption and traffic padding.

**2. Active Attacks** — The adversary modifies, injects, or disrupts traffic.
- **Masquerade:** An entity pretends to be another (e.g., IP spoofing).
- **Replay:** Capturing and retransmitting valid data to produce an unauthorized effect.
- **Modification of messages:** Altering legitimate messages in transit.
- **Denial of Service (DoS):** Flooding a server to make it unavailable.
- *Difficulty:* Detection is feasible. *Defense:* Authentication, integrity checks, rate limiting.

**Security Services (3 marks):** (As covered in Part A Q2 above — 1 mark for each of the 5 services.)

---

#### (b) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the TCP/IP security model and the security protocols used at each layer. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The TCP/IP model consists of four functional layers. Each layer exposes a distinct attack surface and is paired with a specific class of security protocols.

| Layer | Function | Security Protocol(s) | Attacks Defended |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Application** | HTTP, SMTP, DNS | TLS 1.3, S/MIME, SSH, DNSSEC | Phishing, MITM, cache poisoning |
| **Transport** | TCP, UDP | TLS, DTLS, TCP-MD5 | Session hijacking, SYN flood |
| **Network (Internet)** | IP routing | IPsec (ESP, AH, IKEv2) | IP spoofing, route hijacking |
| **Link / Network Access** | Ethernet, WiFi | MACsec, WPA3, 802.1X | ARP poisoning, evil twin |

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- At the **Application Layer**, **TLS 1.3** provides end-to-end confidentiality and integrity using an ephemeral Diffie-Hellman key exchange and AEAD ciphers (AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305).
- At the **Transport Layer**, **TLS** is the dominant protocol. It also defends against TCP session hijacking by binding sessions to unique cryptographic identifiers.
- At the **Network Layer**, **IPsec** secures all IP traffic transparently. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) provides confidentiality; AH (Authentication Header) provides integrity and source authentication.
- At the **Link Layer**, **MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE)** encrypts Ethernet frames; **WPA3** secures WiFi with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals).

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
No single layer provides complete security. Production systems apply **defense in depth**, layering protocols to ensure redundancy.

---

### Question B — Option (ii) [14 Marks]

#### (a) Define a Denial of Service (DoS) attack. Differentiate between DoS and DDoS. Describe SYN flood attack with a sequence diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A **Denial of Service (DoS) attack** is a malicious attempt to make a network resource, server, or service unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with illegitimate requests, exploiting vulnerabilities, or exhausting computational resources (CPU, memory, bandwidth).

**DoS vs. DDoS (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Single attacker | Multiple distributed sources (botnet) |
| **Scale** | Limited | Massive (often Tbps-scale) |
| **Traceability** | Easier to trace | Harder — origins are spoofed/distributed |
| **Defense** | Single IP blocking | Requires scrubbing centers, anycast routing |

**SYN Flood Attack — Step-by-Step (4 marks):**

1. **Normal TCP Handshake:** Client sends SYN → Server replies SYN-ACK → Client sends ACK → Connection established.
2. **Attack Vector:** Attacker sends a flood of SYN packets with **spoofed source IP addresses**.
3. **Server Action:** For each SYN, the server allocates a **TCP control block (TCB)** in the half-open state, sends a SYN-ACK, and waits for an ACK that *will never arrive*.
4. **Resource Exhaustion:** The half-open connection queue fills up; legitimate clients cannot establish new connections.
5. **Result:** Server is unable to service valid users → **Denial of Service**.

**Defense:** **SYN cookies** (encode state in the sequence number, no TCB allocation) and **rate limiting** at the firewall.

---

#### (b) Discuss the principle of "Defense in Depth" and explain how it is implemented in modern networks. List any five security mechanisms defined by X.800. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Defense in Depth (2 marks):**
**Defense in Depth** is a security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping layers of controls (administrative, logical, and physical) so that the failure of any single control does not result in total compromise. It is based on the principle that *no single security mechanism is infallible*.

**Implementation in Modern Networks (3 marks):**
1. **Perimeter Layer:** Firewalls, DMZ segmentation, and Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) filter inbound traffic.
2. **Network Layer:** VLAN segmentation, IPsec VPNs for site-to-site confidentiality, and 802.1X for port-based access control.
3. **Host Layer:** Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR), host firewalls, OS hardening, and patch management.
4. **Application Layer:** Input validation, TLS encryption, MFA, and least-privilege application permissions.
5. **Data Layer:** AES-256 encryption at rest, tokenization of PII, and cryptographic key management (HSMs).
6. **Physical Layer:** Biometric access, CCTV, Faraday cages, and locked server rooms.

**Five Security Mechanisms (X.800) (2 marks):**
1. **Encipherment** — Hides plaintext using encryption algorithms.
2. **Digital Signature** — Cryptographically binds a sender's identity to a message.
3. **Access Control** — Enforces who can do what on a resource (RBAC, ACL).
4. **Data Integrity** — Uses MACs or hash functions to detect modification.
5. **Authentication Exchange** — Verifies identity through challenge-response (e.g., Kerberos).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Network Security Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Service" with "Mechanism":** A *service* is *what* you achieve (e.g., confidentiality). A *mechanism* is *how* you achieve it (e.g., encryption). Examiners deduct 1 mark for each such swap.
> 2. **Forgetting Non-Repudiation:** When listing the 5 X.800 services, students often list only 4. Remember the fifth is **Non-Repudiation**.
> 3. **OSI vs. TCP/IP Layer Confusion:** TLS is at the **Transport Layer** of TCP/IP, but at the **Presentation Layer** of OSI. Examiners may use either wording — answer the one they ask.
> 4. **Skipping Diagrams:** A 7-mark question often allocates **1–2 marks** for the diagram. A clearly labeled block diagram (even hand-drawn style) is essential.
> 5. **Missing the "Why":** Don't just list — explain *why* the defense works. For example, *"SYN cookies prevent TCB exhaustion"* gets full marks; *"SYN cookies help"* does not.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Network Security Principles:**

- **X.800 Triad:** Attacks, Services, Mechanisms. Memorize all three categories and their sub-items.
- **5 Services:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control.
- **8 Mechanisms:** Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.
- **2 Attack Types:** Passive (eavesdropping) vs. Active (modification/disruption). Passive is hard to detect; active is hard to prevent.
- **TCP/IP Security Layering:** Application (TLS) → Transport (TLS) → Network (IPsec) → Link (MACsec/WPA3) → Physical.
- **IPsec vs. TLS:** IPsec is at Layer 3 (transparent, app-agnostic). TLS is at Layer 4 (app-aware, requires socket support).
- **SYN Flood Defense:** SYN cookies + rate limiting + firewall rules.
- **DoS vs. DDoS:** DoS = one source, easy to block. DDoS = many sources, requires scrubbing.
- **Hash Collision Rule:** An $n$-bit hash becomes vulnerable at $2^{n/2}$ attempts (birthday paradox).
- **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **Defense in Depth:** Multiple overlapping layers, no single point of failure.
- **Principle of Least Privilege:** Grant only the minimum permissions required.
- **Fail-Safe Defaults:** On system failure, deny access rather than grant it.
- **Kerberos, SSL/TLS, IPsec** are the most exam-frequently-mentioned protocols.
- **RSA minimum key size (2024):** 2048 bits for general use; 4096 bits for high-assurance.
- **AEAD Modes (TLS 1.3 mandatory):** AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Network Security Principles**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Network Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Network Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT412 — Foundations of Security in Computing), **Common Network Threats** are defined as the standardized catalog of malicious activities, attack vectors, and exploitation techniques that target the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad)** of data traversing or residing within computer networks. These threats are broadly classified by **attack surface** (wired, wireless, application-layer, transport-layer), **intent** (passive vs. active), and **propagation mechanism** (self-replicating vs. human-mediated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** This module emphasizes threat identification over tool-based memorization. Students are expected to classify, differentiate, and propose countermeasures for the threat families listed below.

The principal threat families recognized by the KTU syllabus are:

| Threat Family | Primary CIA Dimension Violated | Attack Class |
|---|---|---|
| Malware (Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware) | Integrity & Availability | Active / Propagation-based |
| Phishing & Social Engineering | Confidentiality | Human-channel |
| Denial-of-Service (DoS / DDoS) | Availability | Active / Resource-exhaustion |
| Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) | Confidentiality & Integrity | Active / Interception |
| SQL Injection (SQLi) | Integrity & Confidentiality | Application-layer |
| Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) | Confidentiality & Integrity | Application-layer |
| DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning | Integrity | Active / Redirection |
| ARP Spoofing / Poisoning | Integrity | LAN-layer Active |
| Sniffing / Eavesdropping | Confidentiality | Passive |
| Password Attacks (Brute-force, Dictionary, Credential Stuffing) | Confidentiality & Authenticity | Active / Cryptanalytic |
| Insider Threat | All Three Dimensions | Trusted-agent |
| Zero-Day Exploits | Integrity & Availability | Active / Vulnerability-based |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mailroom of a Skyscraper"

Imagine a 50-storey office building (the **network**) where every employee (a **packet**) walks through a single mailroom (the **router/firewall**) to reach their desk. Now imagine the threats as follows:

- A **Virus** is a contaminated letter that only spreads when an employee *opens* it on their desk and then mails copies to colleagues.
- A **Worm** is a self-driving parcel cart that walks into the mailroom on its own and mails itself to every employee, no human help required.
- A **Trojan** is a beautifully gift-wrapped package labeled "Free Pizza" — looks legitimate, but inside is a security guard badge that lets intruders walk freely.
- A **Phishing email** is a forged memo from "HR" asking for your badge number — perfectly formatted, totally fake.
- A **DDoS attack** is 10,000 delivery trucks blocking the mailroom entrance — legitimate mail cannot get through.
- A **Man-in-the-Middle** is a malicious mail clerk who opens every letter, photocopies the cheque, reseals it, and forwards it on.
- **SQL Injection** is a delivery form where someone writes instructions in the "Receiver Name" field that actually reorder the building's internal database.

> [!NOTE]
> Every common network threat maps to one (or more) of three goals: **steal data** (confidentiality breach), **change data** (integrity breach), or **stop service** (availability breach). When analyzing a threat in your KTU exam, always anchor it to the CIA dimension it violates.

## 1.3 Threat Classification Taxonomy (KTU Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups network threats along two orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — By Attacker's Action:**
- **Passive Threats:** Observation without modification (e.g., Sniffing, Traffic Analysis).
- **Active Threats:** Modification, fabrication, or disruption (e.g., DoS, MitM, SQLi).

**Axis 2 — By Propagation Channel:**
- **Host-resident:** Require user action or host compromise (Virus, Trojan).
- **Network-resident:** Self-propagate over protocols (Worm, ARP Poisoning).
- **Human-channel:** Exploit psychology, not code (Phishing, Pretexting, Baiting).
- **Application-layer:** Exploit software bugs (SQLi, XSS, Buffer Overflow).

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

The following standard metrics are universally cited in KTU question papers and must be memorized:

- **Confidentiality:** preserved via *AES-128, AES-256, TLS 1.3*.
- **Integrity:** preserved via *SHA-256, SHA-3, HMAC-SHA256*.
- **Availability SLA target:** **99.999% ("Five Nines") = 5.26 minutes downtime/year**.
- **Mean Time To Recover (MTTR):** Average time to restore service after an incident.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Average operational interval between incidents.
- **Password Entropy Threshold:** **≥ 60 bits** is considered strong by NIST SP 800-63B.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Threat Family Analysis

### 2.1.1 Malware Family (The "Code-Based" Threats)

Malware (short for **malicious software**) is the umbrella term for software designed to gain unauthorized access, cause damage, or disrupt systems. KTU emphasizes distinguishing between the four major types:

**A. Virus**
- **Operational Logic:** Attaches itself to a *legitimate host program* (the "carrier"). Executes only when the host runs.
- **Lifecycle Phases:** *Dormant → Trigger → Replication → Detection-evasion → Damage payload*.
- **Why it matters:** Cannot self-propagate across the network alone; needs human-mediated file transfer (USB, email attachment, download).

**B. Worm**
- **Operational Logic:** A *standalone, self-contained program* that exploits network vulnerabilities to replicate.
- **Why it matters:** Spreads exponentially — a single infected host can compromise an entire subnet in minutes. Famous examples: *ILOVEYOU, Code Red, Conficker, WannaCry's SMB exploit*.

**C. Trojan Horse**
- **Operational Logic:** Disguises itself as legitimate software. Does not self-replicate but creates a *backdoor* for the attacker.
- **Why it matters:** Bypasses the human trust boundary — users install it willingly. Common delivery: pirated software, fake antivirus pop-ups.

**D. Ransomware**
- **Operational Logic:** Encrypts user files using strong cryptography (typically *RSA-2048* or *AES-256*), then demands payment (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.
- **Why it matters:** Direct, monetized attack on *availability* and *integrity*. Modern strains (WannaCry, NotPetya, LockBit) combine worm-like propagation with ransomware payload.

### 2.1.2 Social Engineering & Phishing

Exploits the human (weakest link) rather than code. The attacker manipulates psychology using:

- **Authority:** "I am the IT admin, give me your password."
- **Urgency:** "Your account will be locked in 10 minutes."
- **Fear:** "We detected suspicious activity; verify now."
- **Greed:** "You won a free iPhone — claim here."

**Phishing Sub-Categories (KTU-favorite question):**

| Variant | Target | Channel |
|---|---|---|
| Phishing | Mass audience | Email |
| Spear Phishing | Specific individual | Email with personalized context |
| Whaling | Senior executive ("big fish") | Email / Phone |
| Vishing | Voice phishing | Phone call |
| Smishing | SMS phishing | Text message |
| Pharming | DNS-level redirection | Compromised DNS server |

### 2.1.3 Denial-of-Service (DoS) and Distributed DoS (DDoS)

**Operational Goal:** Deplete one of three resources — *bandwidth, CPU cycles, or memory* — to deny service to legitimate users.

**Attack Sub-Types:**

- **SYN Flood:** Half-open TCP connections exhaust the server's connection table. Attacker sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK, attacker never sends ACK. State table fills up.
- **UDP Flood:** High-volume UDP packets to random ports force the server to repeatedly check for non-existent applications and reply with ICMP "Destination Unreachable."
- **ICMP (Ping) Flood:** Saturates the target with ICMP Echo Request packets.
- **HTTP Flood (Layer 7):** Sends seemingly legitimate HTTP GET/POST requests that consume server-side resources (database queries, sessions).
- **Slowloris:** Opens many connections and sends HTTP headers *very slowly*, never completing the request, exhausting the server's concurrent connection pool.
- **Amplification Attack (DNS / NTP):** Small query with spoofed source IP, large response sent to victim. Multiplier can be 50x–500x.
- **Botnet-Driven DDoS:** A network of compromised "zombie" machines (botnet) coordinates a synchronized flood. Botnet sizes have exceeded **1 million nodes** (e.g., Mirai botnet, 2016).

### 2.1.4 Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)

The attacker secretly positions themselves between two communicating parties, relaying and potentially altering messages.

**Operational Modes:**
- **Eavesdropping (Passive):** Reads traffic but does not modify it.
- **Tampering (Active):** Modifies messages in transit (e.g., altering bank transaction amount).
- **Session Hijacking:** Steals session cookies/tokens to impersonate an authenticated user.

**Common Enablers:**
- *ARP Spoofing* on LANs.
- *Rogue Wi-Fi Access Points* (Evil Twin attack).
- *SSL Stripping* — downgrade HTTPS to HTTP.
- *DNS Spoofing* — redirect victim to attacker's server.

### 2.1.5 Injection Attacks (Application-Layer)

**A. SQL Injection (SQLi)**

Attacker inserts malicious SQL code into an input field that the application blindly concatenates into a database query.

**Example (Kerala B.Tech Exam Classic):**
```sql
-- Vulnerable code (pseudo):
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + input + "' AND password='" + pwd + "'"

-- Attacker enters in username field: ' OR '1'='1' --
-- Resulting query:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='...'
-- The -- comments out the password check, '1'='1' is always true → returns ALL users.
```

**B. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)**

Attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page viewed by other users.

- **Reflected XSS:** Malicious script in URL parameters, immediately executed when victim clicks the crafted link.
- **Stored XSS:** Malicious script persisted in the database (e.g., in a forum post), executed every time any user views the page.
- **DOM-based XSS:** Vulnerable client-side JavaScript writes user input directly into the DOM without sanitization.

### 2.1.6 Network-Layer Spoofing Attacks

**A. ARP Spoofing / Poisoning**
- Sends forged ARP replies on a LAN, associating attacker's MAC address with the legitimate gateway's IP.
- All traffic from victims now flows through attacker's machine → enables MitM, sniffing, session hijacking.

**B. DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning**
- Corrupts a DNS resolver's cache with forged IP address mappings.
- Victim types `www.bank.com` → redirected to attacker's phishing site.

**C. IP Spoofing**
- Forges the source IP address in packet headers to bypass IP-based access controls or to mount reflection/amplification attacks.

### 2.1.7 Sniffing / Eavesdropping

**Passive Threat.** Attacker captures network traffic using:
- Promiscuous-mode NIC drivers (e.g., *Wireshark, tcpdump*).
- Network TAPs.
- Rogue access points.

Unencrypted protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP) expose credentials in plaintext. Countermeasure: end-to-end encryption (TLS 1.3, SSH, HTTPS).

### 2.1.8 Password-Based Attacks

| Attack Type | Operational Logic | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Brute-Force | Tries all possible character combinations | Account lockout, rate-limiting, CAPTCHA |
| Dictionary | Tries a precompiled list of common passwords | Password complexity, breached-password checks |
| Credential Stuffing | Reuses leaked credentials from other breaches | Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), unique passwords |
| Rainbow Table | Uses precomputed hash-to-password lookup tables | Salting + slow KDFs (bcrypt, Argon2) |
| Keylogger | Records keystrokes on compromised host | Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) |

### 2.1.9 Insider Threats

A trusted user (employee, contractor, ex-employee) intentionally or unintentionally causes a security incident. The 2024 KTU syllabus specifically highlights this as a high-impact category because:
- It bypasses perimeter defenses.
- Detection requires *User & Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA)*.
- Insider threats account for ~60% of data breaches (per industry reports).

### 2.1.10 Zero-Day Exploits

An attack that exploits a software vulnerability unknown to the vendor (and thus unpatched). The "zero-day" refers to the zero days the vendor has had to fix it. Famous examples: *Stuxnet (2010), Log4Shell (2021)*.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the high-yield formulas and quantitative metrics frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. All values are mathematically verifiable and conform to industry standards (NIST, RFC, IETF).

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Password Entropy** | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | bits | $L$ = password length, $N$ = size of character set (94 printable ASCII) |
| 2 | **NIST Strong Password Threshold** | $H \geq 60$ | bits | Minimum entropy for "strong" password |
| 3 | **Brute-Force Time (avg)** | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{N^{L}}{2 \cdot R}$ | seconds | $R$ = hashes per second the attacker can compute |
| 4 | **DDoS Amplification Factor** | $AF = \dfrac{\text{Response Size}}{\text{Request Size}}$ | dimensionless | DNS ≈ 28x–54x; NTP ≈ 556x |
| 5 | **Five-Nines Availability** | $A = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Downtime}}{8760 \text{ hrs}}$ | fraction | $99.999\% \rightarrow 5.26$ min/yr downtime |
| 6 | **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability engineering metric |
| 7 | **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Maintainability metric |
| 8 | **Worm Propagation (Simple Epidemic)** | $I(t) = N \left( 1 - e^{-\beta t} \right)$ | infected hosts | $N$ = vulnerable population, $\beta$ = infection rate |
| 9 | **Virus Replication Ratio** | $R_0 = \beta \cdot \dfrac{N}{\gamma}$ | dimensionless | $R_0 > 1 \Rightarrow$ outbreak spreads |
| 10 | **Packet Capture Rate (Sniffing)** | $P_{cap} = \dfrac{N_{captured}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Measure of network sensor coverage |
| 11 | **Session Hijack Window** | $\Delta t = t_{ack} - t_{syn}$ | milliseconds | TCP MitM timing analysis |
| 12 | **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday)** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{b})}$ | fraction | $b$ = hash bits, $n$ = attempts |
| 13 | **Phishing Click-Through Rate** | $CTR = \dfrac{C_{clicks}}{C_{delivered}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Industry baseline ≈ 2%–5% |
| 14 | **SQLi Risk Score (CVSS-like)** | $S = (C + I + A) / 3$ | 0–10 | Base on Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability impact |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Operations Centers (NOCs)** use MTBF/MTTR to allocate maintenance windows and SLAs.
- **Penetration Testers** compute password entropy to advise clients on credential policy.
- **Incident Response Teams** estimate worm propagation curves to predict outbreak scope and prioritize containment.
- **DDoS Mitigation Services** (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai) rely on amplification factor math to size scrubbing capacity.
- **Insider Threat Programs** use UEBA scoring algorithms derived from the CVSS-style risk formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Password Entropy and Brute-Force Time

**Problem Statement (Sample KTU-style problem):**
*"A user creates an 8-character password using the 94 printable ASCII characters. Compute (a) the entropy, (b) the total keyspace, (c) the average brute-force time assuming an attacker can compute $10^{10}$ SHA-256 hashes per second."*

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $L$ = password length = **8 characters**
- $N$ = character-set size = **94** (printable ASCII: a–z, A–Z, 0–9, 32 symbols)
- $R$ = attacker's hash rate = $10^{10}$ hashes/sec

### Step 2 — Compute the Total Keyspace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Keyspace} &= N^{L} \\
&= 94^{8} \\
&= 6{,}095{,}689{,}385{,}410{,}816 \\
&\approx 6.095 \times 10^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each of the 8 positions can independently be filled by any of 94 characters. By the multiplication principle, the total number of distinct passwords is $94^8$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= L \cdot \log_{2}(N) \\
&= 8 \cdot \log_{2}(94) \\
&= 8 \cdot 6.554 \\
&\approx 52.43 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each character contributes $\log_2(94) \approx 6.554$ bits of uncertainty. For 8 independent characters, the total entropy is the sum.

### Step 4 — Verify NIST Compliance

Since $H = 52.43 \text{ bits} < 60 \text{ bits}$, the password is **NOT** NIST-compliant (it is "moderate," not "strong"). To meet the 60-bit threshold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min} &= \left\lceil \frac{60}{\log_{2}(94)} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{60}{6.554} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 9.155 \right\rceil \\
&= 10 \text{ characters}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Average Brute-Force Time

On average, the attacker finds the correct password after searching **half** the keyspace.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{N^{L}}{2 \cdot R} \\
&= \frac{6.095 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= \frac{6.095 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= 3.048 \times 10^{5} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 3.53 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An 8-character ASCII password is crackable in under 4 days on commodity GPU hardware. This is why NIST now recommends **length-based** passwords (passphrases) over complex short ones.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DDoS Amplification Factor and Effective Attack Bandwidth

**Problem Statement:**
*"An attacker exploits a misconfigured DNS server with amplification factor $AF = 50$. The attacker has a 1 Gbps uplink. What is the effective attack bandwidth delivered to the victim?"*

### Step 1 — Define Variables

- $AF$ = amplification factor = **50** (dimensionless)
- $B_{up}$ = attacker upload bandwidth = **1 Gbps**

### Step 2 — Apply the Amplification Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= AF \times B_{up} \\
&= 50 \times 1 \text{ Gbps} \\
&= 50 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each 1-byte request causes the DNS server to send ~50 bytes of response to the *spoofed victim IP*. The attacker only pays for the 1 Gbps upload, but the victim absorbs 50 Gbps.

### Step 3 — Reflection/Reflection-DDoS Linkage

With $N$ reflectors each contributing $B_{eff,i}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} AF_i \cdot B_{up,i}
\end{aligned}
$$

For uniform reflectors: $B_{total} = N \cdot AF \cdot B_{up}$.

**Engineering Implication:** A modest botnet of 1000 hosts, each with 100 Mbps, attacking via 50x DNS amplification can saturate a **5 Tbps** pipe — exceeding the 2024 global average DDoS peak (which crossed **5.6 Tbps** in Cloudflare's 2024 reports).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worm Propagation Modeling (Simple Epidemic Model)

**Problem Statement:**
*"A new worm exploits a vulnerability and infects 100,000 vulnerable hosts on a campus network. The infection rate is $\beta = 0.2$ per hour. Assuming a simple epidemic model with no recovery ($\gamma = 0$), estimate the number of infected hosts after $t = 24$ hours."*

### Step 1 — Define the Model

The simple epidemic differential equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot \left(1 - \frac{I}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $I$ = infected, $N$ = vulnerable population, $\beta$ = contact rate.

### Step 2 — Solve the ODE

Separating variables and integrating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dI}{I(1 - I/N)} &= \int \beta \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Using partial fractions: $\dfrac{1}{I(1 - I/N)} = \dfrac{1}{I} + \dfrac{1/N}{1 - I/N}$

Integrating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(I) - \ln(N - I) &= \beta t + C
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying initial condition $I(0) = I_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I}{N - I} &= \frac{I_0}{N - I_0} \cdot e^{\beta t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $I(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(t) &= \frac{N \cdot I_0 \cdot e^{\beta t}}{N - I_0 + I_0 \cdot e^{\beta t}} \\
&= \frac{N}{1 + \left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numbers

With $N = 100{,}000$, $I_0 = 1$ (single initial infection), $\beta = 0.2$ hr$^{-1}$, $t = 24$ hr:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(24) &= \frac{100{,}000}{1 + \left(\frac{99{,}999}{1}\right) e^{-0.2 \times 24}} \\
&= \frac{100{,}000}{1 + 99{,}999 \cdot e^{-4.8}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $e^{-4.8}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-4.8} &\approx 0.00823
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(24) &\approx \frac{100{,}000}{1 + 99{,}999 \times 0.00823} \\
&\approx \frac{100{,}000}{1 + 822.97} \\
&\approx \frac{100{,}000}{823.97} \\
&\approx 121.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** After 24 hours, the worm has infected ~121 hosts (out of 100,000 vulnerable). The S-shaped epidemic curve means infection is slow initially, then explodes around the inflection point $I = N/2$.

### Step 4 — Find the Inflection Time

The inflection (steepest growth) occurs at $I = N/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{inflection} &= \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2} \ln(99{,}999) \\
&= 5 \times 11.51 \\
&\approx 57.6 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Takeaway:** Network defenders have a ~57-hour window of "low-and-slow" infection before the worm's exponential phase. Early detection (honeypots, IDS signatures) is critical.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Threat → Countermeasure Mapping

| Threat | Primary Countermeasure | Secondary Countermeasure | KTU-Mandated Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| Virus | Antivirus + heuristic scanning | Disable autorun, sandboxing | Signature vs. heuristic detection |
| Worm | Patch management, network segmentation | IDS/IPS, ingress/egress filtering | Zero-trust architecture |
| Trojan | Application whitelisting | User education, code signing | Trust boundary violation |
| Ransomware | Offline backups, EDR | Network segmentation, MFA | 3-2-1 backup rule |
| Phishing | Email filtering (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) | User awareness training | Psychological manipulation |
| DoS/DDoS | Rate limiting, scrubbing centers | Anycast, BGP blackholing | Amplification factor |
| MitM | TLS 1.3, certificate pinning | VPN, HSTS, HPKP | Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) |
| SQLi | Parameterized queries / prepared statements | Input validation, WAF | Defense-in-depth |
| XSS | Output encoding, Content-Security-Policy | Input sanitization, HttpOnly cookies | Same-Origin Policy |
| ARP Spoofing | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) | Static ARP entries, 802.1X | Layer-2 attack surface |
| DNS Spoofing | DNSSEC, DoH (DNS over HTTPS) | VPN, trusted resolvers | Chain of trust in PKI |
| Sniffing | TLS / IPsec / SSH | Network segmentation, WPA3 | Passive vs. active attack |
| Password Attack | MFA, salting + slow KDF | Rate limiting, breach monitoring | Entropy & keyspace |
| Insider Threat | UEBA, least privilege | Audit logs, DLP (Data Loss Prevention) | Need-to-know principle |
| Zero-Day | Defense-in-depth, behavioral analysis | Bug bounty, virtual patching | CVSS scoring |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modeled)]
**Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm with respect to their propagation mechanism. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as shown):**

| Aspect | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| **Propagation** | Requires a host program and human action to spread (1 mark) | Self-propagating across networks; no human action needed (1 mark) |
| **Carrier** | Attaches to legitimate executable files | Standalone executable program |
| **Example** | *Melissa*, *CIH* (1 mark) | *ILOVEYOU*, *Conficker*, *Code Red* (1 mark) |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modeled)]
**What is a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack? Explain the role of a botnet in mounting such an attack.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack** is a coordinated attempt to render a network service unavailable by overwhelming it with traffic from multiple sources. (1 mark)

A **botnet** is a network of compromised computers (called *zombies* or *bots*) controlled remotely by an attacker via a **Command-and-Control (C2) channel**. (1 mark)

**Role of botnet:**
- The attacker sends a single command to the C2 server.
- The C2 server instructs thousands (or millions) of bots to simultaneously send malicious traffic to the target.
- The aggregate traffic saturates the victim's bandwidth, CPU, or memory.
- The distributed nature makes mitigation difficult because traffic comes from *many* legitimate-looking IPs. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often confuse DoS and DDoS. **DoS = single source; DDoS = multiple distributed sources (botnet).** Confusing the two will cost you 1 full mark. Also, the question asks for "role of botnet" — naming botnet alone is worth only 1 mark; you must explain its *function* in the attack chain.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry an internal choice. Both alternatives below are designed as full 14-mark ESE (End-Semester Examination) questions. Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Modeled for 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack in detail. Describe at least three techniques used to launch a MitM attack. For each technique, suggest one specific countermeasure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the operational steps of a SQL Injection attack. Differentiate between **SQL Injection** and **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** based on the attack surface, target, and impact.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition (1 mark):** A Man-in-the-Middle attack is an active eavesdropping attack in which the attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties (e.g., a client and a server) and intercepts, reads, and potentially alters the messages exchanged without either party being aware.

**Why it works (1 mark):** It exploits the lack of strong *endpoint authentication* and *message integrity* in unencrypted or poorly encrypted protocols.

**Three Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **ARP Spoofing on a LAN:**
   - The attacker sends forged ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) reply packets on the local network.
   - These packets map the attacker's MAC address to the legitimate gateway's IP address.
   - Victim machines update their ARP cache and start sending all outbound traffic to the attacker's machine first.
   - The attacker forwards traffic to the real gateway, while silently logging or modifying it.
   - **Countermeasure:** Enable *Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)* on managed switches; use static ARP entries for critical infrastructure.

2. **Rogue / Evil Twin Wi-Fi Access Point:**
   - The attacker sets up a wireless access point with the same SSID (network name) as a legitimate public Wi-Fi (e.g., "Airport_Free_WiFi").
   - Unsuspecting users connect to the attacker's AP.
   - All user traffic flows through the attacker's machine before reaching the internet.
   - **Countermeasure:** Use a trusted VPN; verify SSL/TLS certificates; avoid connecting to open/unknown Wi-Fi networks.

3. **SSL Stripping (HTTPS Downgrade Attack):**
   - The attacker intercepts the initial HTTP request from a victim and establishes an HTTPS connection with the server on behalf of the user.
   - The user is left communicating over plain HTTP with the attacker.
   - The attacker reads/modifies traffic in plaintext, then re-encrypts it to the server.
   - **Countermeasure:** Enable *HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)* on the web server, forcing browsers to always use HTTPS.

**Mitigation Summary (1 mark):** Use end-to-end encryption (TLS 1.3), certificate pinning, and Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) verification.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**SQL Injection Attack — Operational Steps (with diagram description, 4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Input Fields:** The attacker probes the web application for input fields (login forms, search boxes, URL parameters) that are not properly sanitized.

**Step 2 — Craft Malicious Input:** The attacker submits specially crafted SQL syntax inside a normal-looking input. Example: entering `' OR '1'='1' --` in the username field.

**Step 3 — Backend Query Manipulation:** The vulnerable application concatenates this input directly into the SQL query string. The malicious syntax alters the query's logic. For example:
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='anything'
```
The `--` comments out the password check, and `OR '1'='1'` makes the WHERE clause always true, returning all rows.

**Step 4 — Exfiltration / Privilege Escalation:** The attacker gains unauthorized access to view, modify, or delete database records. Advanced SQLi attacks can execute OS commands via features like `xp_cmdshell` in SQL Server.

**ASCII Flow Diagram (1 mark):**

```
User (Attacker) --> Web Form --> Application Server --> Database Server
                    [Malicious SQL in input]   [Altered query execution]
                    <------------ Sensitive Data Returned ------------
```

**SQLi vs. XSS Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Dimension | SQL Injection (SQLi) | Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Attack Surface** | Server-side (database layer) | Client-side (browser / user session) |
| **Target** | Database server, sensitive records | End-user's browser, session cookies |
| **Vector** | SQL syntax in input fields | JavaScript / HTML in input fields |
| **Impact** | Data theft, data corruption, OS command execution | Session hijacking, defacement, credential theft |
| **Countermeasure** | Parameterized queries, stored procedures, WAF | Output encoding, Content-Security-Policy, HttpOnly cookies |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Modeled for 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of malware. Compare and contrast **Ransomware** and **Trojan Horse** with respect to propagation, payload, and impact. State two real-world examples of ransomware attacks and their financial impact.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture and operational phases of a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack using a botnet. Compute the **effective attack bandwidth** for a botnet of 500 nodes, each with 200 Mbps uplink, exploiting a DNS amplification factor of 50.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Malware Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Virus:** Attaches to a host program; requires user action to propagate.
2. **Worm:** Standalone, self-replicating across networks; no host program required.
3. **Trojan Horse:** Disguises as legitimate software; creates backdoor access.
4. **Ransomware:** Encrypts victim data and demands ransom for decryption.
5. **Rootkit:** Hides deep within the OS, granting persistent privileged access.
6. **Spyware / Adware:** Covertly monitors user activity or displays unwanted ads.

**Ransomware vs. Trojan Horse — Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Ransomware | Trojan Horse |
|---|---|---|
| **Propagation** | Often via phishing emails, exploit kits, or worm-like SMB exploits | Disguised as legitimate software; user installs voluntarily |
| **Payload** | File encryption using AES-256 / RSA-2048; ransom demand | Backdoor installation, keylogging, data exfiltration |
| **Impact** | Direct financial extortion; complete loss of data access if ransom unpaid | Stealthy long-term compromise; intellectual property theft, credential theft |
| **Detection** | High (loud — encryption activity is conspicuous) | Low (silent — designed to remain undetected) |

**Real-World Ransomware Examples (1 mark):**
- **WannaCry (2017):** Exploited the EternalBlue SMB vulnerability; infected 230,000+ computers in 150 countries; estimated damage **\$4 billion**.
- **NotPetya (2017):** Initially targeted Ukrainian infrastructure; caused **\$10 billion** in global damages to Maersk, Merck, FedEx/TNT, and others.
- **Colonial Pipeline (2021):** DarkSide ransomware; paid **\$4.4 million** ransom; led to fuel shortages across the US East Coast.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**DDoS with Botnet — Architecture (4 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Recruitment:** The attacker infects vulnerable machines worldwide using malware (Trojans, worms) to create a *botnet* (zombie network). Each bot connects back to a **Command-and-Control (C2) server**.

**Phase 2 — Command & Control:** The attacker, via the C2 server, sends the attack command (target IP, attack type, duration) to all bots.

**Phase 3 — Attack Execution:** All bots simultaneously flood the target with malicious traffic. The traffic is *distributed*, making it hard to filter by source IP alone.

**Phase 4 — Amplification (optional):** Bots send small queries to misconfigured DNS/NTP servers with the *target's IP* spoofed as the source. The servers send large responses to the victim. Amplification factor $AF$ can be 50x–500x.

**Phase 5 — Impact:** The target's bandwidth, CPU, or memory is exhausted, denying service to legitimate users.

**Effective Bandwidth Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- Number of botnet nodes: $N = 500$
- Per-node uplink: $B_{up} = 200$ Mbps
- DNS amplification factor: $AF = 50$

Total effective bandwidth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= N \times AF \times B_{up} \\
&= 500 \times 50 \times 200 \text{ Mbps} \\
&= 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ Mbps} \\
&= 5 \text{ Tbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A modest 500-node botnet can deliver a **5 Tbps** attack via DNS amplification. This exceeds the 2024 average peak DDoS attack size and is sufficient to overwhelm most enterprise uplinks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B:**
> 1. **Always label units** in numerical answers. Writing "5,000,000" without "Mbps" or "Tbps" will cost you 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Show substitution step** before final answer. Examiners award partial credit for correctly identifying variables.
> 3. For SQLi/MitM theory questions, **draw the diagram** even if not explicitly asked — it fetches 1–2 free marks.
> 4. **Avoid one-word answers** like "antivirus" or "firewall" for "countermeasure" questions. The examiner expects the *mechanism* (e.g., "signature-based detection with heuristic analysis").
> 5. For comparison questions, a **table format** is preferred — it makes differentiation scannable for the examiner.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Common Network Threats**

- **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — every threat maps to at least one of these.
- **Virus vs. Worm:** Virus needs a host and human action; worm self-propagates.
- **Trojan:** Disguised legitimate software; creates a backdoor.
- **Ransomware:** Encrypts files; demands payment in cryptocurrency.
- **Phishing variants:** Phishing (mass), Spear (targeted), Whaling (executives), Vishing (voice), Smishing (SMS), Pharming (DNS).
- **DoS vs. DDoS:** Single source vs. distributed (botnet-driven).
- **SYN Flood:** Half-open TCP connections exhaust the server state table.
- **Amplification Factor:** $AF$ for DNS ≈ 28–54; NTP ≈ 556.
- **Botnet:** Network of compromised "zombie" machines controlled via C2.
- **Man-in-the-Middle:** Eavesdropping + tampering; mitigated by TLS 1.3, PKI, certificate pinning.
- **SQL Injection:** Malicious SQL in input fields; mitigated by parameterized queries.
- **XSS:** Malicious JavaScript in web pages; reflected, stored, and DOM-based variants.
- **ARP Spoofing:** LAN-level MitM; mitigated by Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI).
- **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning:** Forged DNS responses; mitigated by DNSSEC and DoH.
- **Password Entropy Formula:** $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$.
- **NIST Strong Password Threshold:** $\geq 60$ bits.
- **Brute-Force Average Time:** $T_{avg} = N^{L} / (2R)$.
- **Worm Propagation:** S-shaped curve; slow start, exponential middle, saturation end.
- **Insider Threat:** Trusted user causes harm; mitigated by UEBA, DLP, least privilege.
- **Zero-Day Exploit:** Vulnerability unknown to vendor; no patch available.
- **Sniffing is passive**; **MitM and DoS are active**.
- **Five-Nines Availability:** $99.999\% \rightarrow 5.26$ min/yr downtime.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data → Identity → Monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security Protocols: IPsec, SSL/TLS, VPNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Security Protocols: IPsec, SSL/TLS, VPNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Network Security Protocols — The Triad of Modern Secure Communication

Network Security Protocols are the cryptographic and procedural rulesets that govern how data is securely transmitted, authenticated, and protected across untrusted networks (particularly the Internet). In the KTU 2024 scheme for **Foundations of Security in Computing (PECDT412)**, this triad forms the cornerstone of Module 3, focusing on **layered defense** at the Network Layer (IPsec), the Transport/Application Layer (SSL/TLS), and the overlay tunneling layer (VPNs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> - **IPsec (Internet Protocol Security):** A suite of protocols (AH, ESP, IKE) operating at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model, providing end-to-end security for IP packets.
> - **SSL/TLS (Secure Sockets Layer / Transport Layer Security):** Cryptographic protocols operating between the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** and **Application Layer (Layer 7)**, securing HTTP, SMTP, FTP, etc.
> - **VPN (Virtual Private Network):** A technology that creates an **encrypted tunnel** over a public network, using protocols like IPsec, SSL/TLS, or MPLS to provide confidentiality and remote access.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎬 The "Secure Postal System" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you need to send a confidential contract across a busy city. You have three options:
>
> 1. **IPsec = Armored Diplomatic Pouch (Layer 3)** — You hand your sealed envelope to a **diplomatic courier** at the post office. The entire post office system — trucks, planes, sorting centers — handles the armored pouch. From the moment it leaves your hands until it reaches the recipient, the **entire transit path is protected**, but the postman (router) doesn't know what's inside; it just sees a classified diplomatic shipment.
>
> 2. **SSL/TLS = Sealed Letter with Notarized Wax Seal (Layer 4-7)** — You write your letter, lock it in a tamper-proof box, and only the recipient (using a key) can open it. The postal service (TCP/IP) handles delivery normally, but the **content itself is scrambled**. Anyone intercepting it sees gibberish, but the postal workers don't even know it's protected.
>
> 3. **VPN = Underground Tunnel from Your House to the Office (Overlay)** — Instead of sending letters through the post, you build a **private underground tunnel** directly from your home to your office. Everything you send — every letter, every package — travels inside this tunnel. The outside world never even sees your mail; they just see two entry/exit points of a tunnel.

---

### 1.3 Why This Triad Matters — The CIA Triad in Action

| Goal | IPsec Contribution | SSL/TLS Contribution | VPN Contribution |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | ESP encryption (AES, 3DES) | Symmetric session keys (AES-GCM) | Tunnel encryption |
| **Integrity** | AH + ESP ICV (HMAC-SHA256) | MAC (HMAC, AEAD) | Inner packet integrity |
| **Authentication** | IKE (PSK, Certificates) | X.509 certificates, RSA/ECDHE | User + Tunnel auth |
| **Anti-Replay** | Sequence numbers, anti-replay window | TLS 1.3 explicit sequence | Tunnel sequence control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics You MUST Know for KTU Exams:**
> - **IPsec SA Lifetime:** Typically **28800 seconds (8 hours)** for Phase 2 (IKEv2 default).
> - **TLS Record Max Size:** **16 KB (16384 bytes)** per record.
> - **Typical VPN MTU:** **1400 bytes** (to accommodate IPsec overhead of ~100 bytes).
> - **IKE Port:** **UDP 500** (IKEv1/v2), UDP **4500** for NAT-Traversal.
> - **HTTPS Port:** **TCP 443**.

---

### 1.4 Layering Perspective — The OSI Stack

| OSI Layer | Protocol(s) | Function |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| 7 (Application) | HTTPS, SFTP, SMTPS | Secure applications using TLS |
| 6 (Presentation) | SSL/TLS Handshake | Encryption, encoding |
| 5 (Session) | SSL/TLS Sessions | Session resumption, tickets |
| 4 (Transport) | TCP (TLS over) | Reliable transport for TLS |
| **3 (Network)** | **IPsec (AH, ESP, IKE)** | **Network-layer packet protection** |
| 2 (Data Link) | MACsec (not in this module) | LAN-level security |
| 1 (Physical) | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IPsec — Internet Protocol Security (The Network Layer Shield)

#### 2.1.1 IPsec Architecture (Three Pillars)

IPsec is not a single protocol but a **suite** of three core components:

**1. AH (Authentication Header) — Protocol 51**
- Provides **integrity + authentication + anti-replay**.
- Does **NOT** provide **confidentiality** (no encryption).
- Authenticates the **entire IP packet** (except mutable fields like TTL).

**2. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) — Protocol 50**
- Provides **confidentiality + integrity + authentication + anti-replay**.
- Encrypts the **payload** (and optionally the original IP header in tunnel mode).
- This is the **most commonly deployed** IPsec protocol.

**3. IKE (Internet Key Exchange) — UDP 500**
- Handles **mutual authentication** + **key exchange** between peers.
- **IKEv1**: Two phases (Phase 1: ISAKMP SA, Phase 2: IPsec SA).
- **IKEv2** (RFC 7296, current standard): Simplified, single 4-message exchange, NAT-T built-in, MOBIKE support.

#### 2.1.2 IPsec Modes of Operation

**Transport Mode:**
$$
\text{Original IP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{AH/ESP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{TCP/UDP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{Data}
$$
- Used for **host-to-host** communication.
- **No new IP header** added.
- Saves bandwidth (~20 bytes saved).

**Tunnel Mode (Default for VPNs):**
$$
\text{New IP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{AH/ESP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{Original IP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{TCP/UDP Header} \quad \vert \quad \text{Data}
$$
- Used for **gateway-to-gateway** (site-to-site VPN) or **remote access VPN**.
- **Entire original packet is encapsulated** inside a new IP packet.
- Original source/destination IP addresses are hidden.

> [!NOTE]
> **🔍 Why Two Modes?** Transport mode is lightweight for direct host communication. Tunnel mode hides internal IP addressing — critical for VPN gateways where private IPs (e.g., 10.0.0.0/8) must traverse the public Internet invisibly.

#### 2.1.3 Security Associations (SA) and the SAD/SPD

A **Security Association (SA)** is a **one-way logical connection** between two IPsec peers, identified by a 32-bit **SPI (Security Parameters Index)**.

- **SAD (Security Association Database):** Stores active SAs.
- **SPD (Security Policy Database):** Stores rules defining what traffic to protect, bypass, or drop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often think one SA is enough. **TWO SAs are required** for bidirectional communication (one inbound, one outbound), each with its own SPI.

---

### 2.2 SSL/TLS — The Web's Cryptographic Backbone

#### 2.2.1 Evolution Timeline (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Version | Year | Status | Key Improvement |
|---------|------|--------|-----------------|
| SSL 1.0 | (Never released) | — | Netscape internal, broken |
| SSL 2.0 | 1995 | **Deprecated** | Weak MAC, export-grade ciphers |
| SSL 3.0 | 1996 | **Broken (POODLE, 2014)** | Replaced by TLS |
| TLS 1.0 | 1999 | Deprecated (RFC 8996) | SSL 3.0 rename + fixes |
| TLS 1.1 | 2006 | Deprecated | CBC attack mitigation |
| TLS 1.2 | 2008 | Widely used | SHA-256, AEAD, PRF rework |
| **TLS 1.3** | **2018 (RFC 8446)** | **Current standard** | **1-RTT handshake, 0-RTT, forward secrecy by default** |

#### 2.2.2 The TLS 1.2 Handshake (Most Asked in KTU)

**Step-by-step flow (2-RTT):**
1. **Client → Server:** `ClientHello` (supported cipher suites, random nonce, extensions like SNI).
2. **Server → Client:** `ServerHello` (chosen cipher suite, server random, session ID), `Certificate` (X.509), `ServerHelloDone`.
3. **Client → Server:** `ClientKeyExchange` (Pre-master secret encrypted with server's public key), `ChangeCipherSpec`, `Finished` (MAC of handshake).
4. **Server → Client:** `ChangeCipherSpec`, `Finished`.
5. **Application Data** (encrypted with session keys derived via PRF).

#### 2.2.3 TLS 1.3 — The Modern Revolution

- **1-RTT Handshake** (down from 2-RTT).
- **0-RTT Resumption** (for returning clients, with replay risk).
- **Forward Secrecy Mandatory** (only DHE/ECDHE key exchange allowed; RSA key exchange REMOVED).
- **Cipher Suite Simplification:** Only 5 suites allowed (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`).
- **Encrypted Extensions** (SNI is now encrypted — ESNI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Derivation in TLS (HKDF):**
> - **PRF (TLS 1.2):** `P_hash(secret, label || seed)` using HMAC-SHA256.
> - **HKDF (TLS 1.3):** `Extract(salt, IKM)` then `Expand(PRK, info, L)`.

---

### 2.3 VPNs — Virtual Private Networks

#### 2.3.1 VPN Taxonomy

**By Topology:**
- **Site-to-Site VPN:** Connects two networks (e.g., branch office ↔ HQ). Usually IPsec tunnel mode.
- **Remote Access VPN:** Connects individual user to corporate network. SSL/TLS-based (e.g., OpenVPN, Cisco AnyConnect).

**By Protocol Stack:**
- **IPsec VPN:** Layer 3, strong, hardware-accelerated.
- **SSL/TLS VPN:** Layer 4-7, firewall-friendly (only TCP 443 needed).
- **MPLS L3VPN:** Carrier-grade, but not encrypted (out of scope).
- **WireGuard:** Modern, ~4000 lines of code, uses ChaCha20 + Curve25519.

#### 2.3.2 VPN Tunneling Process

A VPN tunnel involves **three core operations**:
1. **Encapsulation:** Original packet wrapped inside a new packet with VPN header.
2. **Encryption:** Payload encrypted (e.g., AES-256-GCM).
3. **Decapsulation:** At the receiving end, the outer header is stripped, and the inner packet is decrypted and forwarded.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Value / Equation | Use Case |
|---------------------|------------------|----------|
| IPsec AH ICV (HMAC) | $\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(K, \text{Header} \Vert \text{Payload})$ | Integrity check |
| IPsec ESP Encryption | $C = E_K(P)$ where $E$ is AES-CBC or AES-GCM | Confidentiality |
| IKEv2 SA Lifetime | $T_{\text{SA}} = 28800$ seconds | Rekey interval |
| TLS 1.2 Master Secret | $\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{premaster secret}, \text{``master secret''} \Vert \text{client\_random} \Vert \text{server\_random}, 48)$ | Session key derivation |
| HKDF Extract | $\text{PRK} = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{salt}, \text{IKM})$ | TLS 1.3 key derivation |
| HKDF Expand | $\text{OKM} = T(1) \Vert T(2) \Vert \ldots$ where $T(i) = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{PRK}, T(i-1) \Vert \text{info} \Vert i)$ | Expand keying material |
| VPN Overhead | $\text{Overhead} = \text{ESP Header} + \text{ESP Trailer} + \text{ESP ICV} + \text{New IP Header}$ $\approx 50$–$\mathbf{100}$ **bytes** | MTU calculation |
| Effective VPN Throughput | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{link}} \times \dfrac{L_{\text{MTU}} - L_{\text{overhead}}}{L_{\text{MTU}}}$ | Performance estimation |
| Anti-Replay Window | $W \in [32, 64, 128, 256]$ packets | Bitmask for replay protection |
| TLS Record Max | $L_{\text{max}} = 16384$ bytes ($= 2^{14}$) | Per-record plaintext limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value and "such that" operators above are rendered using $\vert$ and $\Vert$ to avoid markdown table corruption.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Enterprise Networks:** Site-to-site IPsec VPNs connect branch offices globally (Cisco ASA, Fortinet FortiGate).
- **Web Security:** TLS 1.3 secures every HTTPS connection — the **"S" in HTTPS**.
- **Zero Trust Architecture:** Modern SDP (Software-Defined Perimeter) uses TLS 1.3 + mutual TLS (mTLS) for identity-based access.
- **IoT Security:** Lightweight IPsec profiles (RFC 9075) for constrained devices.
- **Cloud Networking:** AWS Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway — all IPsec-based.
