## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — IPsec ESP Packet Structure in Tunnel Mode

**Given:** An original IPv4 packet of total size $L_{\text{orig}}$ bytes, with original IP header $H_{\text{orig}}$ (20 bytes, no options), TCP header $H_{\text{TCP}}$ (20 bytes), and payload $P$.

**Find:** Total size of the IPsec ESP-protected tunnel-mode packet, $L_{\text{total}}$.

**Step 1 — Identify ESP header components (RFC 4303):**
- SPI: 4 bytes
- Sequence Number: 4 bytes
- IV (for AES-CBC): 16 bytes
- Padding: variable (0–255 bytes), $L_{\text{pad}}$
- Padding Length: 1 byte
- Next Header: 1 byte
- ESP ICV (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256 truncated): 16 bytes (recommended in RFC 4868)

**Step 2 — Identify outer IP header overhead:**
- New IP header $H_{\text{new}}$: 20 bytes (IPv4, no options)

**Step 3 — Construct the packet layout:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{H_{\text{new}}} + L_{\text{ESP Header}} + L_{H_{\text{orig}}} + L_{H_{\text{TCP}}} + L_{P} + L_{\text{pad}} + L_{\text{pad\_len}} + L_{\text{NH}} + L_{\text{ICV}} \\
&= 20 + (4 + 4 + 16) + 20 + 20 + L_P + L_{\text{pad}} + 1 + 1 + 16 \\
&= 102 + L_P + L_{\text{pad}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply block cipher alignment:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block size for AES} &= 16 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Encrypted payload length} &= L_P + L_{\text{pad}} + 2 \\
\text{Constraint: } (L_P + L_{\text{pad}} + 2) \bmod 16 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $L_{\text{pad}}$ when $L_P = 1200$ bytes (a typical web request):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(L_P + L_{\text{pad}} + 2) \bmod 16 &= 0 \\
(1200 + L_{\text{pad}} + 2) \bmod 16 &= 0 \\
(1202 + L_{\text{pad}}) \bmod 16 &= 0 \\
1202 \bmod 16 &= 1202 - 75 \times 16 = 1202 - 1200 = 2 \\
\text{So } (2 + L_{\text{pad}}) \bmod 16 &= 0 \\
L_{\text{pad}} &= 14 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute final total length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= 102 + 1200 + 14 = 1316 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate effective VPN throughput for a 1 Gbps link with MTU = 1500:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{eff}} &= 10^9 \times \frac{1500 - 102}{1500} \text{ bps} \\
&= 10^9 \times \frac{1398}{1500} \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 932 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **🔍 Interpretation:** The encryption overhead reduces usable bandwidth by ~6.8%. For a 1500-byte MTU, the maximum effective payload before fragmentation is **1398 bytes**, which is why the standard VPN MTU is set to **1400 bytes** for safety.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — TLS 1.2 Master Secret and Key Block Derivation

**Given:** Pre-master secret (PMS) of 48 bytes, client random $C_R$ (32 bytes), server random $S_R$ (32 bytes).

**Find:** The 48-byte Master Secret and the key block for session encryption.

**Step 1 — Master Secret computation (RFC 5246):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Master Secret} &= \text{PRF}(PMS, \text{``master secret''} \Vert C_R \Vert S_R, 48)
\end{aligned}
$$
The PRF for TLS 1.2 with SHA-256 is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{hash}}(\text{secret}, \text{seed}) &= \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{secret}, A(1) \Vert \text{seed}) \Vert \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{secret}, A(2) \Vert \text{seed}) \Vert \ldots \\
A(0) &= \text{seed} \\
A(i) &= \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{secret}, A(i-1))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Key Block derivation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key Block} &= \text{PRF}(\text{Master Secret}, \text{``key expansion''} \Vert S_R \Vert C_R, L)
\end{aligned}
$$
where $L$ is the total key material length:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \times L_{\text{MAC}} + 2 \times L_{\text{enc}} + 2 \times L_{\text{IV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (a classic suite):**
- $L_{\text{MAC}} = 20$ bytes (SHA-1)
- $L_{\text{enc}} = 16$ bytes (AES-128 key)
- $L_{\text{IV}} = 16$ bytes (AES-CBC IV)
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2(20) + 2(16) + 2(16) = 40 + 32 + 32 = 104 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Key block assignment (in order):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{client\_write\_MAC\_key} &= \text{KeyBlock}[0:20] \\
\text{server\_write\_MAC\_key} &= \text{KeyBlock}[20:40] \\
\text{client\_write\_encryption\_key} &= \text{KeyBlock}[40:56] \\
\text{server\_write\_encryption\_key} &= \text{KeyBlock}[56:72] \\
\text{client\_write\_IV} &= \text{KeyBlock}[72:88] \\
\text{server\_write\_IV} &= \text{KeyBlock}[88:104]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The labeling order — **client MAC, server MAC, client enc, server enc, client IV, server IV** — is a **frequently asked 2-mark question**. Memorize this sequence!

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — VPN Overhead and Fragmentation Analysis

**Given:** Original IP packet of size 1480 bytes (just under MTU 1500), ESP tunnel mode with AES-CBC-128 + HMAC-SHA1-96.

**Find:** Whether fragmentation occurs and the number of resulting fragments.

**Step 1 — Compute total overhead (in bytes):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{overhead}} &= L_{\text{new IP}} + L_{\text{SPI}} + L_{\text{Seq}} + L_{\text{IV}} + L_{\text{pad len}} + L_{\text{NH}} + L_{\text{ICV}} \\
&= 20 + 4 + 4 + 16 + 1 + 1 + 12 \\
&= 58 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute padded encrypted payload length (must be AES-block aligned):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{enc}} &= L_{\text{orig payload}} + L_{\text{pad}} + 2 \\
(1480 + L_{\text{pad}} + 2) \bmod 16 &= 0 \\
1482 \bmod 16 &= 1482 - 92 \times 16 = 1482 - 1472 = 10 \\
L_{\text{pad}} &= 6 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{\text{enc}} &= 1480 + 6 + 2 = 1488 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total packet size:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= 58 + 1488 = 1546 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine fragmentation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &> 1500 \text{ (MTU)} \\
\text{Therefore, fragmentation is required.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute fragment sizes (with fragment headers, $L_{\text{frag\_hdr}} = 20$ bytes):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max data per fragment} &= 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Number of fragments} &= \lceil 1546 / 1480 \rceil = \lceil 1.044 \rceil = 2 \text{ fragments}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final fragment sizes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fragment 1:} \quad & 20 \text{ (hdr)} + 1480 \text{ (data)} = 1500 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Fragment 2:} \quad & 20 \text{ (hdr)} + 66 \text{ (data)} = 86 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **🔍 Engineering Insight:** This is why **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD, RFC 1191)** is critical for IPsec VPNs. The classic "PMTU black hole" problem occurs when ICMP messages are blocked, causing VPN tunnels to fail silently. Modern solutions include **IKEv2 fragmentation (RFC 7383)** which fragments at the IKE layer, not the IP layer.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — IPsec vs. SSL/TLS vs. VPN

| Attribute | IPsec | SSL/TLS | VPN (Generic) |
|-----------|-------|---------|---------------|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4-7 (Transport-Presentation) | Layer 2/3 (Tunnel) |
| **Encryption Scope** | Entire IP packet (tunnel) / Payload (transport) | Application payload | Entire tunneled traffic |
| **Key Exchange** | IKEv1/v2 (Diffie-Hellman) | RSA, ECDHE, DHE | Depends on underlying protocol |
| **Authentication** | Pre-shared key, X.509 certs | X.509 certs (server), optional client certs | User credentials + tunnel auth |
| **NAT Traversal** | Requires NAT-T (UDP 4500) | NAT-friendly (TCP 443) | Varies |
| **Firewall Friendly** | Poor (ESP protocol 50, AH protocol 51 often blocked) | Excellent (looks like HTTPS) | Good for SSL-VPN, poor for IPsec-VPN |
| **Handshake RTTs** | 4 messages (IKEv2) | 1 RTT (TLS 1.3), 2 RTT (TLS 1.2) | Depends |
| **Typical Use Case** | Site-to-site VPN, host-to-host | HTTPS, secure email, APIs | Remote access, site-to-site |
| **Performance** | Hardware-accelerated (AES-NI) | Software-dominant | Varies |
| **PKI Required?** | Optional (PSK possible) | Yes (server cert mandatory in TLS 1.3) | Optional |

sequenceDiagram

    participant C as Client

    C->>S: ClientHello (cipher suites, random)
    S->>C: ServerHello + Certificate + ServerHelloDone
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange + ChangeCipherSpec + Finished
    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec + Finished
    C->>S: Application Data (encrypted)

    Note over C,S: TLS 1.3 Handshake 1-RTT

```mermaid

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between IPsec Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode. When would you recommend each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **IP Header** | Original IP header preserved | New outer IP header added |
| **Protection Scope** | Payload only (TCP/UDP header + data) | Entire original IP packet |
| **Overhead** | ~24-36 bytes (smaller) | ~50-100 bytes (larger) |
| **Use Case** | Host-to-host communication within trusted network | Gateway-to-gateway (VPN), remote access |
| **Visibility** | Internal IPs visible on the wire | Internal IPs hidden (privacy) |

**Recommendation (1 mark):**
- Use **Transport Mode** for end-to-end host communication (e.g., securing traffic between two servers in the same datacenter).
- Use **Tunnel Mode** for site-to-site VPN or remote access VPN, where the entire original packet must be hidden from intermediate routers.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of IKE in IPsec. Why is IKEv2 preferred over IKEv1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of IKE (Internet Key Exchange):**
1. **Authentication** of IPsec peers (using PSK, RSA certificates, or ECDSA).
2. **Key Exchange** using Diffie-Hellman to establish shared secrets.
3. **SA Negotiation** — agreeing on cryptographic algorithms and parameters.
4. **Key Refresh** — periodic rekeying to limit exposure.

**Why IKEv2 is preferred over IKEv1 (3 points):**
1. **Simpler:** 4 messages total (vs. IKEv1's 9+ messages across 2 phases).
2. **Built-in NAT Traversal:** No need for separate NAT-T negotiation.
3. **Reliability:** Uses TCP-like sequence numbers and acknowledgments; supports retransmission.
4. **MOBIKE support:** Seamless roaming between networks without dropping the VPN.
5. **Dead Peer Detection (DPD):** Built-in mechanism to detect failed peers.
6. **EAP support:** Native support for flexible authentication (certificates, tokens, etc.).

---

### 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Internal Choice: Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) Describe the SSL/TLS handshake protocol with a neat sequence diagram. Explain the cryptographic operations performed at each step. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The TLS 1.2 handshake establishes a secure session through the following steps:

**Step 1 — ClientHello (1 mark)**
- Client sends: protocol version, 32-byte client random $C_R$, session ID (for resumption), list of supported cipher suites, compression methods, and extensions.
- *Cryptographic purpose:* $C_R$ provides freshness to prevent replay.

**Step 2 — ServerHello + Certificate + ServerHelloDone (2 marks)**
- **ServerHello:** Selected cipher suite (e.g., `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA256`), 32-byte server random $S_R$, session ID.
- **Certificate:** Server's X.509 certificate containing its RSA public key.
- **ServerHelloDone:** Indicates server has finished its initial messages.
- *Cryptographic purpose:* Server authenticates itself via the certificate chain validated against trusted CAs.

**Step 3 — ClientKeyExchange + ChangeCipherSpec + Finished (2 marks)**
- **ClientKeyExchange:** Client generates 48-byte **Pre-Master Secret (PMS)**, encrypts it with server's public RSA key, sends ciphertext.
- **ChangeCipherSpec:** Signals that subsequent messages will use negotiated cipher.
- **Finished:** Encrypted MAC of the entire handshake transcript (verifies no MITM tampering).
- *Cryptographic purpose:* PMS is the seed for all session keys. Only the server (with private key) can decrypt it.

**Step 4 — Server response: ChangeCipherSpec + Finished (1 mark)**
- Server decrypts PMS, derives master secret, switches to encrypted mode, sends its own Finished message.

**Step 5 — Key Derivation (1 mark)**
Both parties compute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Master Secret} &= \text{PRF}_{SHA256}(\text{PMS}, \text{``master secret''} \Vert C_R \Vert S_R, 48) \\
\text{Key Block} &= \text{PRF}_{SHA256}(\text{MS}, \text{``key expansion''} \Vert S_R \Vert C_R, L)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sequence Diagram (from Section 4.2 above, reproduced for reference):**
The handshake achieves: **(1) authentication, (2) key exchange, (3) agreement on ciphers, (4) verified channel** before any application data is exchanged.

---

**(b) Explain IPsec ESP in Tunnel Mode with packet format. How does it provide confidentiality, integrity, and authentication? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ESP Tunnel Mode Packet Format (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tunneled ESP Packet: } & \text{New IP Hdr (20B)} \Vert \text{ESP Hdr (24B: SPI+Seq+IV)} \Vert \\
& \text{[Original IP Hdr (20B)} \Vert \text{TCP Hdr (20B)} \Vert \text{Payload} \Vert \\
& \text{ESP Trailer (Pad + PadLen + NextHdr, 2-257B)}] \Vert \\
& \text{ESP ICV (12-32B HMAC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bracketed portion is **encrypted**.

**Cryptographic Operations (4 marks):**

1. **Confidentiality (Encryption):**
   - The original packet + ESP trailer are encrypted using a symmetric algorithm negotiated in the SA (e.g., AES-256-CBC or AES-256-GCM).
   - The IV (Initialization Vector) ensures that identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertexts.
   - Padding ensures the data is a multiple of the block size.

2. **Integrity (via ESP ICV):**
   - The **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** is computed over the ESP header (excluding IV) + ciphertext + ESP trailer using HMAC-SHA-256.
   - Any modification in transit is detected at the receiver.
   - For AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM), encryption and integrity are fused into a single operation (AEAD = Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data).

3. **Authentication (via SPI and IKE):**
   - The **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** identifies which SA (and thus which key/algorithm) to use.
   - The peer was authenticated during IKE phase, ensuring the SPI belongs to a legitimate, mutually-authenticated peer.
   - ESP alone does NOT authenticate the IP identities; that is the role of IKE/AH.

4. **Anti-Replay:**
   - The monotonically increasing **Sequence Number** + receiver's sliding window (typically 32 or 64 packets) prevents replay attacks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Compare and contrast IPsec and SSL/TLS as security protocols. Discuss their typical deployment scenarios. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Detailed Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | IPsec | SSL/TLS |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4-7 (Transport-Application) |
| **Scope of Protection** | Entire IP packet | Application data only |
| **Key Exchange** | IKEv1/v2 (Diffie-Hellman) | RSA, ECDHE, DHE (ephemeral) |
| **Authentication** | PSK, X.509, EAP | X.509 (server), optional client certs |
| **NAT Traversal** | Requires NAT-T (UDP 4500) | NAT-friendly by design |
| **Firewall/Proxy Traversal** | Often blocked (protocol 50/51) | Easy (TCP 443 allowed everywhere) |
| **PKI Required** | Optional | Yes (mandatory server cert in TLS 1.3) |
| **Performance** | Hardware-accelerated in NICs/crypto chips | Mostly software (improving with TLS offload) |
| **Granular Control** | Subnet-level, host-level | Per-application, per-URL |
| **Browser Support** | Not built-in | Native (every browser supports HTTPS) |
| **Revocation** | OCSP, CRL for certs | OCSP stapling, CRL |
| **Standards** | RFC 4301-4309 | RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3) |

**Deployment Scenarios (3 marks):**

**IPsec is preferred for:**
1. **Site-to-site VPN** between geographically separated corporate offices.
2. **Host-to-host secure communication** in private networks.
3. **Network-layer enforcement** (e.g., encrypting all traffic from a server regardless of application).
4. **Government/military networks** with strict compliance (e.g., FIPS 140-2).
5. **Layer 2 tunneling** (L2TP/IPsec) for legacy PPP connections.

**SSL/TLS is preferred for:**
1. **Web traffic security** (HTTPS — over 90% of web traffic).
2. **API security** (REST/GraphQL over TLS).
3. **Email protocols** (SMTPS, IMAPS, POP3S).
4. **Remote access VPN** (SSL-VPN) for BYOD scenarios — no client software needed.
5. **Microservices communication** (mTLS in service meshes like Istio/Linkerd).
6. **Cloud-native applications** where Layer 7 visibility is required.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a VPN. Differentiate between site-to-site and remote access VPN. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of VPN (4 marks):**

A VPN establishes a **secure, encrypted tunnel** over an untrusted public network using the following steps:

1. **Authentication:** The VPN client authenticates to the VPN gateway using credentials (username/password + MFA), certificates, or pre-shared keys.
2. **Tunnel Establishment:** Using a tunneling protocol (PPTP, L2TP, IPsec, OpenVPN/WireGuard), a logical point-to-point connection is negotiated.
3. **Key Exchange:** Cryptographic keys are exchanged (via Diffie-Hellman in IPsec, or RSA/ECDHE in SSL-VPN) to derive session encryption keys.
4. **Encryption:** All outbound traffic from the client is encrypted (e.g., AES-256-GCM) before being encapsulated with a new outer header containing the VPN gateway's public IP.
5. **Transmission:** The encapsulated packet traverses the public Internet. Intermediate routers see only the encrypted tunnel traffic.
6. **Decapsulation:** At the VPN gateway, the outer header is stripped, the payload is decrypted, and the original packet is forwarded to the internal destination.
7. **Return Path:** The process is reversed for response packets.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.4 — Site-to-Site vs Remote Access Architecture)**

**Site-to-Site vs Remote Access VPN (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Site-to-Site VPN | Remote Access VPN |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Endpoints** | Network-to-Network (Gateway-to-Gateway) | Individual user-to-Network |
| **Users Involved** | Transparent to end users | User explicitly connects via client |
| **Configuration** | Always-on, configured on routers/firewalls | On-demand, per-user |
| **Typical Protocol** | IPsec Tunnel Mode | SSL/TLS, IKEv2 |
| **Use Case** | Branch office connecting to HQ | Employee working from home, traveling |
| **Authentication** | Per-device (certificate/PSK) | Per-user (credentials + MFA) |
| **IP Assignment** | Static, based on internal subnets | Dynamic via DHCP/pool |
| **Scalability** | Hundreds of sites | Thousands of concurrent users |
| **Example Product** | Cisco ASA, Fortinet FortiGate, AWS Site-to-Site VPN | Cisco AnyConnect, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, OpenVPN Access Server |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting SPI and Sequence Number sizes** — Both are 4 bytes (32 bits). Students often write 2 bytes.
> 2. **Confusing AH and ESP** — AH does NOT encrypt; ESP can provide both encryption and integrity. A common question: "Which IPsec protocol provides confidentiality?" — Answer: **ESP** (NOT AH).
> 3. **Mixing up IKEv1 and IKEv2** — IKEv1 has TWO phases (Phase 1 establishes ISAKMP SA, Phase 2 establishes IPsec SA). IKEv2 has a single 4-message exchange.
> 4. **TLS key derivation order** — The order **client_MAC, server_MAC, client_enc, server_enc, client_IV, server_IV** is tested directly.
> 5. **VPN MTU confusion** — Students write 1500 instead of 1400. Always account for **ESP + IPsec overhead (~50-100 bytes)**.
> 6. **TLS 1.3 vs TLS 1.2 handshakes** — TLS 1.2 is **2-RTT**, TLS 1.3 is **1-RTT**. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 7. **Writing "SSL" instead of "TLS"** — SSL is deprecated and broken (POODLE). Use **TLS** in answers.
> 8. **Omitting "Forward Secrecy" in TLS 1.3** — TLS 1.3 mandates forward secrecy (only DHE/ECDHE allowed). This is a frequently-missed point.

---

### 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 CRITICAL THINGS TO REMEMBER (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**

- [x] **IPsec operates at Layer 3; TLS operates between Layer 4 and 7; VPN is an overlay technology.**
- [x] **IPsec = AH (integrity only, Protocol 51) + ESP (confidentiality + integrity, Protocol 50) + IKE (key exchange, UDP 500).**
- [x] **Two IPsec modes: Transport (host-to-host) and Tunnel (gateway-to-gateway, default for VPNs).**
- [x] **Each IPsec SA is unidirectional; bidirectional traffic needs 2 SAs (one inbound, one outbound).**
- [x] **SPI is 32 bits; Sequence Number is 32 bits; ESP ICV is typically 12-32 bytes.**
- [x] **IKEv1 has 2 phases; IKEv2 has 1 phase with 4 messages (simplified, more reliable).**
- [x] **IKEv2 features: NAT-T built-in, MOBIKE, DPD, EAP support.**
- [x] **SSL is deprecated; TLS 1.2 is widely used; TLS 1.3 is the current standard (RFC 8446, 2018).**
- [x] **TLS 1.2 = 2-RTT handshake; TLS 1.3 = 1-RTT (with optional 0-RTT resumption).**
- [x] **TLS 1.3 mandates forward secrecy (RSA key exchange REMOVED, only DHE/ECDHE allowed).**
- [x] **TLS 1.3 cipher suites simplified to 5 (e.g., TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384).**
- [x] **TLS key block order: client_MAC, server_MAC, client_enc, server_enc, client_IV, server_IV.**
- [x] **TLS Master Secret is 48 bytes; pre-master secret is 48 bytes (RSA) or derived from DH.**
- [x] **VPN MTU is typically 1400 bytes (not 1500) to accommodate ~100 bytes of IPsec overhead.**
- [x] **Site-to-site VPN = gateway-to-gateway (always-on); Remote access VPN = user-to-network (on-demand).**
- [x] **SSL-VPN uses TCP 443 (firewall-friendly); IPsec-VPN uses UDP 500 + UDP 4500 (NAT-T).**
- [x] **VPN protocols: IPsec, SSL/TLS, L2TP, PPTP (weak), OpenVPN, WireGuard (modern, lightweight).**
- [x] **ESP trailer contains: Padding + Pad Length (1 byte) + Next Header (1 byte).**
- [x] **IPsec Anti-replay window: typically 32 or 64 packets using a bitmask.**
- [x] **TLS runs over TCP (typically 443); DTLS runs over UDP (for VoIP, gaming).**
- [x] **IKEv2 default SA lifetime: 28800 seconds (8 hours) for Phase 2.**
- [x] **HKDF (TLS 1.3) = Extract + Expand; PRF (TLS 1.2) = P_hash using HMAC-SHA256.**
- [x] **X.509 certificates are the backbone of TLS authentication (server cert mandatory in TLS 1.3).**
- [x] **AES-GCM provides AEAD = Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (single operation).**
- [x] **PMTUD (Path MTU Discovery) is critical for IPsec VPNs to prevent fragmentation issues.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firewalls and Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): Types of Firewalls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firewalls and Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): Types of Firewalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Firewall — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Firewall (RFC 2979 / KTU 2024 Definition):** A firewall is a hardware device, software program, or a combined system that manages, filters, and controls the flow of network traffic between two or more network segments (typically between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network such as the Internet) based on a predefined set of security rules called a **Security Policy**.

A firewall acts as a **policy enforcement point** — it does not *prevent* attacks by itself, but it implements a security policy that decides which packets, sessions, or applications are allowed to pass and which must be dropped, rejected, or logged.

## 1.2 Intrusion Detection System (IDS) — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **IDS Definition:** An Intrusion Detection System is a security device or software application that monitors network traffic or host system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or signature-based attack patterns, and generates alerts or reports to a management console.

There are three logical families of IDS:
- **NIDS** — Network-based IDS
- **HIDS** — Host-based IDS
- **Hybrid IDS** — Combined approach

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Secured Building**
>
> Imagine a high-security corporate building. The **firewall** is the *security gate and reception desk* combined. It checks the ID badge of every person (packet) entering or leaving. Only people whose names are on the approved visitor list (security policy/rule set) are allowed inside. If a person's ID is missing or invalid, they are turned away at the gate itself.
>
> The **IDS** is the *CCTV surveillance room inside the building*. It doesn't stop people at the door; instead, it watches what happens *after* they are inside — looking for suspicious behaviour (anomalies) or known criminals (signatures). If it spots something, it raises an alarm.

## 1.4 Why Both Are Required?

- A firewall is a **preventive (proactive)** control — it blocks traffic *before* entry.
- An IDS is a **detective (reactive)** control — it detects *and alerts* about intrusions in progress or already successful.
- Together, they form the **Defense-in-Depth** architecture mandated in modern KTU 2024 cyber-security modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** A well-tuned firewall must operate with a **False Positive Rate < 5%** and must not exceed a **throughput drop of < 10%** of the link bandwidth under full load. These are standard KTU/industry benchmarks (NIST SP 800-41).

## 1.5 Core Terminology (Bold Reference List)

- **Perimeter** — the logical boundary between trusted and untrusted networks.
- **Default-Deny Policy** — *block everything unless explicitly allowed* (the secure posture).
- **Default-Allow Policy** — *allow everything unless explicitly blocked* (insecure posture).
- **Stateful Inspection** — tracking the state of active network connections.
- **Stateless Filtering** — examining each packet in isolation, no memory of prior packets.
- **Bastion Host** — a hardened system exposed directly to the untrusted network.
- **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone)** — a buffer network segment between the firewall and the Internet.
- **SYN Flood** — a Denial-of-Service attack that firewalls help mitigate.
- **Honeypot** — a decoy system used in conjunction with IDS to study attacker behaviour.
- **Zero-Day Attack** — a previously unknown exploit that signature-based IDS cannot detect.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Firewalls (KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies firewalls into **five canonical generations/types**. Each generation inspects traffic at a progressively deeper level of the OSI/TCP-IP stack.

### Type 1 — Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless)

- Operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model.
- Examines each IP packet **independently** — no memory of previous packets.
- Filtering criteria: Source IP, Destination IP, Protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP), Source Port, Destination Port.
- Fastest type because no state tracking is required.

**How it works (logic):**
1. Extract the IP header fields of the incoming packet.
2. Compare them against the access control list (ACL) rules.
3. If a `match` is found → apply the rule's action (ACCEPT / DROP / REJECT).
4. If no `match` → apply the default policy (usually DROP — default-deny).

**Why it is weak:** It cannot detect **IP Spoofing** (fake source IP) and is vulnerable to attacks fragmented across multiple packets (fragmentation attacks).

### Type 2 — Stateful Inspection Firewall (Stateful Filter)

- Operates at **Layer 3 + Layer 4** (Network + Transport).
- Maintains a **State Table** that tracks every active TCP connection (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, FIN, RST flags).
- Allows return traffic for *outgoing* connections automatically (because the state table records the outbound SYN).

**State Table Entry Structure (logical):**

| Field | Description |
|:---|:---|
| Source IP | Initiator's IP address |
| Source Port | Initiator's port number |
| Destination IP | Responder's IP address |
| Destination Port | Responder's port number |
| Protocol | TCP / UDP / ICMP |
| State | NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, INVALID |
| Timeout | Idle time before entry expires |

### Type 3 — Application-Layer Firewall (Proxy Firewall / Application Gateway)

- Operates at **Layer 7 (Application Layer)** of the OSI model.
- Acts as a **proxy** between the client and the server. The client thinks it is talking to the real server, but it is actually talking to the firewall.
- Can inspect **payload content** — for example, it can block HTTP requests containing the string `"cmd.exe"`.
- Types: **Circuit-Level Proxy** (SOCKS) and **Application-Level Proxy** (HTTP, FTP, DNS proxies).

### Type 4 — Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)

- Combines **stateful inspection + deep packet inspection (DPI) + Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) + application awareness**.
- Identifies applications regardless of port number (e.g., detects Facebook traffic even on port 80).
- Maintains a real-time threat intelligence feed.
- Industry examples: Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Networks PA-Series, Fortinet FortiGate.

### Type 5 — Circuit-Level Gateway

- Operates at the **Session Layer (Layer 5)** of the OSI model.
- Verifies the **TCP three-way handshake** is legitimate; once established, packets are passed without further inspection.
- Faster than application proxies, more secure than packet filters.
- Used commonly in **SOCKS-based VPN tunnels**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Units | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Rule Lookup Time | $T_{lookup} = O(\log n)$ for indexed ACL | milliseconds (ms) | $n$ = number of ACL rules |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Packets Processed}}{\text{Time}}$ | packets per second (pps) | Higher = better firewall performance |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | dimensionless ratio | For IDS evaluation |
| False Negative Rate | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | dimensionless ratio | For IDS evaluation |
| Detection Rate (Sensitivity) | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless ratio | Higher = better IDS |
| CIDR Subnet Match | $Network = IP \ \ AND \ \ Mask$ | bitwise AND | Used in packet-filter rules |
| State Table Memory | $M = n_{conn} \times s_{entry}$ | bytes | $n_{conn}$ = active sessions, $s_{entry}$ = size per entry (~100 bytes) |
| Maximum Concurrent Sessions | $S_{max} = \frac{RAM_{available}}{s_{entry}}$ | integer count | Upper bound on simultaneous connections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You will be expected to compute simple CIDR matching, state-table size, and FPR/FNR from confusion matrix data. Memorize the four-cell confusion matrix: **TP, FP, TN, FN**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Networks:** Enterprise-grade firewalls (NGFW) are deployed at the network perimeter and between VLANs to enforce **micro-segmentation**.
- **Cloud Computing:** AWS Security Groups, Azure NSGs, and Google Cloud Firewalls are all *virtual packet-filtering* and *stateful* firewalls.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS):** Specialized firewalls like **DPI-aware** firewalls protect SCADA networks in power plants and smart factories.
- **Web Applications:** Web Application Firewalls (WAF) — a subclass of application-layer firewalls — protect against **SQL injection, XSS, and CSRF** attacks.
- **5G/IoT Edge:** Lightweight stateful firewalls are embedded in 5G User Plane Function (UPF) and IoT gateways.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Packet-Filtering Rule Evaluation

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A packet-filtering firewall has the following ACL (applied top-to-bottom, first match wins):

```
Rule 1:  deny   tcp  any   host 10.0.0.5  eq 23
Rule 2:  allow  tcp  10.1.0.0/16  any  eq 80
Rule 3:  allow  udp  any   any  eq 53
Rule 4:  deny   ip   any   any   (default-deny)
```

A packet arrives with the following header:

- **Source IP:** 10.1.0.50
- **Destination IP:** 10.0.0.5
- **Protocol:** TCP
- **Source Port:** 4567
- **Destination Port:** 23

**Decision Process:**

The firewall compares the packet's header against each rule sequentially.

- **Rule 1 Check:** Destination IP = 10.0.0.5 ✓, Protocol = TCP ✓, Port = 23 ✓ → **MATCH → Action = DENY**.

The packet is **dropped immediately** by Rule 1. Rules 2, 3, and 4 are not evaluated because of the *first-match* semantic.

**Final Decision:** `DENY` (blocked — Telnet to internal host 10.0.0.5 is forbidden).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CIDR Subnet Matching Derivation

**Given:**
- Packet Source IP = `192.168.10.45`
- Rule: `allow tcp 192.168.10.0/24 any`

**Derivation (step-by-step):**

$$
\text{Convert IP to binary (last octet only):} \quad 45_{10} = 0010 1101_2
$$

$$
\text{Subnet mask /24 → last octet mask:} \quad 255.255.255.0 \rightarrow 1111 1111_2
$$

$$
\text{Network address (IP AND Mask):}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network} & = 192.168.10.45 \ \ AND \ \ 255.255.255.0 \\
& = 192.168.10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Rule network (IP AND Mask):} \quad 192.168.10.0 \ \ AND \ \ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.10.0
$$

$$
\text{Since Packet Network} = \text{Rule Network} = 192.168.10.0 \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH}
$$

**Conclusion:** The packet's source IP belongs to the `192.168.10.0/24` subnet, so the rule **matches**, and the packet is allowed.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — IDS Confusion Matrix Computation

**Given:** An IDS processed 1000 network events. The results are:
- True Positives (TP) = 80
- False Positives (FP) = 20
- True Negatives (TN) = 850
- False Negatives (FN) = 50

**Step 1 — Verify totals:**

$$
TP + FP + TN + FN = 80 + 20 + 850 + 50 = 1000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR & = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
& = \frac{80}{80 + 50} \\
& = \frac{80}{130} \\
& = 0.6154 \\
& = 61.54\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR & = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
& = \frac{20}{20 + 850} \\
& = \frac{20}{870} \\
& = 0.02299 \\
& \approx 2.30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Accuracy & = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} \\
& = \frac{80 + 850}{1000} \\
& = \frac{930}{1000} \\
& = 0.93 \\
& = 93\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Precision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision & = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
& = \frac{80}{80 + 20} \\
& = \frac{80}{100} \\
& = 0.80 \\
& = 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Firewall Types

| Feature | Packet Filter | Stateful Filter | App-Layer Proxy | NGFW | Circuit-Level |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| OSI Layer | L3 | L3 + L4 | L7 | L3–L7 | L5 |
| Inspects Payload | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Maintains State Table | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Partial |
| Protects Against IP Spoofing | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Application Awareness | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Performance Speed | Very High | High | Low | Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium |
| Detects Zero-Day | ✗ | ✗ | Partial | With IPS | ✗ |
| Example Tool | iptables | pfSense | Squid Proxy | Palo Alto | SOCKS5 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Firewall Placement in a Network (Perimeter + DMZ Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inet["INTERNET UNTRUSTED NETWORK"]
    extfw["EXTERNAL FIREWALL NGFW"]
    dmz["DMZ ZONE WEB AND MAIL SERVERS"]
    intfw["INTERNAL FIREWALL STATEFUL"]
    lan["INTERNAL LAN TRUSTED CORPORATE NETWORK"]
    ids["NIDS SENSOR PASSIVE MONITOR"]
    mgmt["SIEM LOG MANAGEMENT CONSOLE"]
    admin["NETWORK SECURITY ADMINISTRATOR"]

    inet -- Packet Arrives --> extfw
    extfw -- Allowed HTTP and HTTPS --> dmz
    extfw -- Blocked Malicious --> inet
    dmz -- Filtered Internal Traffic --> intfw
    intfw -- Verified Sessions Only --> lan
    intfw -- Mirror Port Traffic --> ids
    ids -- Alert Stream --> mgmt
    mgmt -- Notification --> admin
    admin -- Policy Update --> extfw
    admin -- Policy Update --> intfw
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Firewall Decision Flow (Packet-Filtering Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["INCOMING PACKET ARRIVES AT FIREWALL"]
    parse["STEP 1 PARSE IP AND TCP HEADERS EXTRACT FIELDS"]
    rule1{"STEP 2 CHECK RULE 1 FIRST MATCH"}
    rule2{"STEP 3 CHECK RULE 2"}
    rule3{"STEP 4 CHECK RULE 3"}
    default{"STEP 5 DEFAULT RULE"}
    accept["ACTION ALLOW PACKET FORWARDED"]
    drop["ACTION DENY PACKET DROPPED SILENTLY"]
    reject["ACTION REJECT PACKET ICMP UNREACHABLE SENT"]
    log1["LOG EVENT TO SYSLOG AND SIEM"]
    endnode["PROCESS NEXT PACKET"]

    start --> parse
    parse --> rule1
    rule1 -- MATCH --> accept
    rule1 -- NO MATCH --> rule2
    rule2 -- MATCH --> drop
    rule2 -- NO MATCH --> rule3
    rule3 -- MATCH --> reject
    rule3 -- NO MATCH --> default
    default --> log1
    default --> endnode
    accept --> log1
    drop --> log1
    reject --> log1
    log1 --> endnode
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — IDS Detection Methodologies (Signature vs Anomaly)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pkg["NETWORK PACKET OR HOST EVENT"]
    capture["STEP 1 CAPTURE VIA TAP OR MIRROR PORT"]
    engine{"STEP 2 DETECTION ENGINE TYPE"}
    sigdb["SIGNATURE DATABASE KNOWN ATTACK PATTERNS"]
    anmodel["ANOMALY MODEL BASELINED NORMAL BEHAVIOUR"]
    sigmatch{"STEP 3 PATTERN MATCH RESULT"}
    anothold{"STEP 3 DEVIATION FROM BASELINE"}
    alert1["ALERT GENERATED KNOWN ATTACK DETECTED"]
    alert2["ALERT GENERATED ANOMALY DETECTED"]
    norm["NO ALERT TRAFFIC IS BENIGN"]
    resp["STEP 4 RESPONSE LOG ALERT BLOCK OR NOTIFY IPS"]

    pkg --> capture
    capture --> engine
    engine -- SIGNATURE --> sigdb
    engine -- ANOMALY --> anmodel
    sigdb --> sigmatch
    anmodel --> anothold
    sigmatch -- MATCH --> alert1
    sigmatch -- NO MATCH --> norm
    anothold -- DEVIATION --> alert2
    anothold -- NORMAL --> norm
    alert1 --> resp
    alert2 --> resp
    norm --> resp
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Defense-in-Depth Layered Security Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["LAYER 1 PERIMETER FIREWALL NGFW"]
    layer2["LAYER 2 DMZ SEGMENTATION"]
    layer3["LAYER 3 INTERNAL FIREWALL"]
    layer4["LAYER 4 NIDS INTERNAL MONITORING"]
    layer5["LAYER 5 HIDS ON ENDPOINT HOSTS"]
    layer6["LAYER 6 APPLICATION WAF"]
    layer7["LAYER 7 DATA ENCRYPTION AT REST AND IN TRANSIT"]
    attacker["EXTERNAL ATTACKER"]

    attacker -- ATTEMPT 1 --> layer1
    layer1 -- BLOCKED OR LOGGED --> attacker
    attacker -- ATTEMPT 2 BYPASS --> layer2
    layer2 -- CONTAINED --> attacker
    attacker -- ATTEMPT 3 LATERAL MOVE --> layer3
    layer3 -- BLOCKED --> attacker
    attacker -- ATTEMPT 4 SLOW PROBE --> layer4
    layer4 -- ALERT AND LOG --> attacker
    attacker -- ATTEMPT 5 MALWARE --> layer5
    layer5 -- QUARANTINE --> attacker
    attacker -- ATTEMPT 6 APP LAYER --> layer6
    layer6 -- BLOCKED --> attacker
    layer7 --> attacker
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between a Firewall and an Intrusion Detection System (IDS).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Firewall | IDS |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Function | Prevents unauthorized traffic from entering the network | Detects and alerts on suspicious activity that has already entered the network |
| Mode | Inline / Active (in the data path) | Passive / Out-of-band (monitors mirrored traffic) |
| OSI Layer | Layer 3, 4, 5, or 7 depending on type | Primarily Layer 7 (payload analysis) |
| Action | Allow / Drop / Reject | Alert / Log / Notify |
| Response | Automatic blocking | Human-driven response (or IPS for auto-block) |
| Example | iptables, Cisco ASA | Snort, Suricata, OSSEC |

*(3 marks — Full credit if all five differences are clearly stated.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is a stateful inspection firewall? How does it differ from a stateless packet-filtering firewall?**

**Model Answer:**

A **stateful inspection firewall** is a firewall that maintains a **state table** to track the state of active TCP/UDP connections and uses this context to make filtering decisions. It records outbound connection requests and automatically allows return traffic matching the state of those connections.

A **stateless packet-filtering firewall** examines each packet **independently** based solely on its header fields, with **no memory of prior packets**, making it unable to distinguish a legitimate return packet from a forged one.

**Key Differences:**
- Stateful remembers the connection; stateless does not.
- Stateful prevents **session hijacking and SYN flood** better.
- Stateless is faster but less secure.

*(3 marks — Definition 1 mark, key difference 1 mark, security advantage 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model QP, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the different **types of firewalls** based on their working principle. Compare their features in a tabular form. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)**. Discuss **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** detection methodologies. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The five main types of firewalls are:

1. **Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless)** — Operates at the Network Layer (Layer 3). Examines each packet's header (source IP, destination IP, protocol, ports) against a static rule set. Fast and efficient but cannot inspect payload and is vulnerable to IP spoofing.

2. **Stateful Inspection Firewall** — Operates at Layer 3 and Layer 4. Maintains a state table of all active connections and uses this context to allow legitimate return traffic. Provides better security against session-based attacks.

3. **Application-Layer Gateway (Proxy Firewall)** — Operates at Layer 7. Acts as a proxy between client and server, inspecting application-layer payload (e.g., HTTP, FTP). Can block application-specific attacks like SQL injection but introduces latency.

4. **Circuit-Level Gateway** — Operates at Layer 5 (Session Layer). Verifies the TCP three-way handshake is legitimate. Used commonly in SOCKS-based environments. Faster than application proxies but does not inspect payload.

5. **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)** — Combines stateful inspection, deep packet inspection (DPI), intrusion prevention, and application awareness. Industry standard for modern enterprise networks.

| Type | OSI Layer | State Tracking | Payload Inspection | Speed |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Packet Filter | L3 | ✗ | ✗ | Very High |
| Stateful | L3–L4 | ✓ | ✗ | High |
| App-Layer | L7 | ✓ | ✓ | Low |
| Circuit-Level | L5 | Partial | ✗ | High |
| NGFW | L3–L7 | ✓ | ✓ | Medium |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per type explanation + 2 marks for comparison table.)*

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

A **Network-based IDS (NIDS)** is placed at strategic points inside the network (typically behind the firewall) and monitors traffic flowing through the network. It uses a network TAP or port mirroring (SPAN) to capture packets for analysis.

**Architecture of NIDS:**

1. **Packet Capture Module** — Uses libpcap / WinPcap to capture packets from the wire.
2. **Pre-processor / Decoder** — Decodes protocols (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP) layer by layer.
3. **Detection Engine** — Compares decoded data against signatures or statistical models.
4. **Alert/Logging Module** — Generates alerts to syslog/SIEM on detection.
5. **Response Module** — Notifies the administrator; in IPS mode, blocks the traffic inline.

**Signature-Based Detection:**
- Uses a database of known attack patterns (signatures), e.g., Snort rules.
- Example signature: `alert tcp any any → any 80 (content: "/etc/passwd"; msg:"Password file access attempt")`
- **Strength:** Very low false positive rate, fast for known attacks.
- **Weakness:** Cannot detect **zero-day** attacks or novel malware.

**Anomaly-Based Detection:**
- Builds a statistical baseline of *normal* network behaviour (bandwidth, packet size, protocol distribution).
- Any significant deviation from the baseline triggers an alert.
- Techniques: statistical models, machine learning, neural networks.
- **Strength:** Can detect **zero-day** and unknown attacks.
- **Weakness:** Higher false positive rate; requires training period.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for NIDS architecture with diagram description, 2 marks for signature-based, 2 marks for anomaly-based.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model QP, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What is a **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone)**? With a neat diagram, explain the **three-legged firewall architecture** and justify why it is preferred over a single-firewall architecture. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **False Positive** and **False Negative** in the context of an IDS. A network IDS evaluated on 2000 events produced the following results: TP=150, FP=50, TN=1700, FN=100. Calculate the **Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Accuracy, and Precision**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**DMZ Definition:** A Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) is a buffer network segment that sits between the untrusted external network (Internet) and the trusted internal LAN. It hosts public-facing servers (web, mail, DNS, FTP) that need to be accessible from the Internet but must not be placed directly inside the trusted network.

**Three-Legged Firewall Architecture:**

The three-legged firewall uses **two firewalls** with a DMZ in between, creating three distinct interfaces:
- **External Interface** → connected to the Internet.
- **DMZ Interface** → connected to public servers.
- **Internal Interface** → connected to the trusted LAN.

External traffic destined for DMZ servers is filtered by Firewall 1. Even if a DMZ server is compromised, the attacker must still bypass Firewall 2 to reach the internal LAN — providing **double-layered defense**.

**Justification over Single-Firewall:**
- Single firewall = single point of failure. If breached, the entire LAN is exposed.
- Three-legged architecture isolates the DMZ, so a compromised public server cannot directly attack internal hosts.
- Enables **granular policy** — different rules per interface.

*(7 marks — 2 marks DMZ definition, 3 marks architecture description, 2 marks justification.)*

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**False Positive (FP):** A legitimate event is incorrectly flagged as malicious by the IDS.
**False Negative (FN):** An actual attack event is missed by the IDS and classified as benign.

**Given Data:**
- TP = 150
- FP = 50
- TN = 1700
- FN = 100
- Total = 150 + 50 + 1700 + 100 = **2000** ✓

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (Sensitivity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR & = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
& = \frac{150}{150 + 100} \\
& = \frac{150}{250} \\
& = 0.60 \\
& = 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR & = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
& = \frac{50}{50 + 1700} \\
& = \frac{50}{1750} \\
& = 0.02857 \\
& \approx 2.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Accuracy & = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} \\
& = \frac{150 + 1700}{2000} \\
& = \frac{1850}{2000} \\
& = 0.925 \\
& = 92.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Precision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision & = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
& = \frac{150}{150 + 50} \\
& = \frac{150}{200} \\
& = 0.75 \\
& = 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks — 1 mark for definitions, 1.5 marks per calculation × 4.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FPR with FNR.** FPR is computed over actual *negatives* (FP / (FP+TN)); FNR is computed over actual *positives* (FN / (FN+TP)). Mixing these is the most common 1-mark loss in Part B numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert decimals to percentages** in the final answer. KTU examiners award the final mark only if the % sign is present.
> 3. **Writing only one type of firewall** in the Part B (a) answer. KTU mandates all five types — partial answers get only 3/7.
> 4. **Drawing the NIDS diagram without labeling the packet flow direction** — at least 2 marks are reserved for direction-of-traffic arrows in the architecture diagram.
> 5. **Using the default-allow posture** in firewall rule design questions. KTU strictly expects the **default-deny** policy in every model answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**
>
> - **Firewall** = preventive, **IDS** = detective. Both are needed for Defense-in-Depth.
> - Five types of firewalls: Packet Filter, Stateful, Application-Layer, Circuit-Level, NGFW.
> - Packet Filter = L3 only, stateless, fastest, weakest.
> - Stateful = L3+L4, uses state table (NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, INVALID).
> - Application-Layer Proxy = L7, can inspect payload, slow.
> - NGFW = L3–L7, integrates IPS, DPI, application awareness.
> - **Default-Deny** is the secure posture; **Default-Allow** is insecure.
> - **DMZ** sits between two firewalls in a three-legged architecture.
> - IDS detection methods: **Signature-based** (low FP, misses zero-day) and **Anomaly-based** (detects zero-day, higher FP).
> - Confusion Matrix metrics to memorize: **DR, FPR, FNR, Accuracy, Precision, Recall**.
> - Industry tool examples to quote: **iptables, pfSense, Squid, Snort, Suricata, Palo Alto, Fortinet**.
> - KTU expects CIDR subnet matching derivations — practice bitwise AND operations.
> - Remember: **SYN flood defense** is a classic KTU question — answered using stateful inspection or SYN cookies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDS and IPS Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IDS and IPS Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intrusion Detection System (IDS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Standardized):**
> An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism — software, hardware, or a hybrid of both — that **monitors, analyzes, and detects** malicious activities, policy violations, or anomalous behavior in a computer system or network. Unlike firewalls that *prevent* traffic based on rules, an IDS operates as a **passive observational and auditing system** that raises alerts upon detection but does **not block** the traffic itself.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IDS as a **CCTV surveillance system with motion sensors installed in a bank vault**.
- A **firewall** is the *security guard at the door* — it decides who enters.
- An **IDS** is the *silent CCTV camera in the corner* — it records *everything* that happens inside, and the moment it sees a masked person (a signature match) or a person crawling instead of walking (an anomaly), it triggers an **alarm**.
- However, the camera **does not physically stop** the robber — it only *detects and reports*.

This is the single most important distinction a KTU examiner tests: **IDS = Detect + Alert, but NEVER block**.

## 1.2 Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Standardized):**
> An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is the **active, inline evolution** of an IDS. It is placed **directly in the data path** (typically in bridge or gateway mode) and not only detects malicious traffic using the same analytical engines (signature/anomaly/stateful) but also **automatically blocks, drops, resets, or quarantines** the offending traffic in real-time without requiring human intervention.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

If an IDS is the **CCTV camera**, then an IPS is the **automatic sliding door that slams shut the instant the camera detects a threat**.
- It is **inline**, meaning all traffic *must pass through it*.
- It has the authority to **drop packets, terminate TCP sessions, or block IP addresses** on the spot.
- A common real-world example: **Snort in IPS mode (Snort Inline / Cisco Sourcefire)**.

## 1.3 Core Distinction at a Glance

| Feature | **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** | **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** |
|---|---|---|
| Operational Mode | **Passive** (out-of-band / tap mode) | **Active** (inline) |
| Traffic Handling | Mirrors traffic, does NOT touch it | All traffic flows *through* it |
| Response | Alerts / logs / notifications | Drops, blocks, resets, quarantines |
| Failure Impact | Failure = loss of visibility (safe) | Failure = traffic blackout (single point of failure) |
| Latency | Near-zero (passive listener) | Adds processing latency |
| Analogy | CCTV Camera | Bouncer who throws out troublemakers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU frequently asks the question *"Distinguish between IDS and IPS"* — the inline vs. passive nature is the **single most weighted differentiator** in the marking scheme.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU Board Exams

- **False Positive:** Legitimate traffic flagged as malicious. Cost: alert fatigue.
- **False Negative:** Malicious traffic missed by the system. Cost: breach.
- **True Positive:** Correctly identified attack.
- **True Negative:** Correctly identified benign traffic.
- **Sensitivity (Threshold τ):** The configurable detection threshold that determines the trade-off between false positives and false negatives.
- **Sensor:** The data collection component (network tap, host agent).
- **Console / Management Server:** The central control unit.
- **Event Database:** Storage for logs and alerts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** KTU examiners expect the term **Base Rate Fallacy** — the phenomenon where, in low-attack environments, even a 99% accurate IDS will generate overwhelmingly more false positives than true positives.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of IDS — The KTU Taxonomy

A KTU board question will most likely ask you to *"Classify IDS based on data source and detection method."* You must produce **both** classifications.

### 2.1.1 Based on Data Source (Where does it look?)

#### A) Network-based IDS (NIDS)

- Sits at a **chokepoint** (e.g., between firewall and switch).
- Captures packets via a **network tap or port mirroring (SPAN port)**.
- Analyzes **protocol-level activity** (e.g., DNS, HTTP, SMTP).
- Example: **Snort (NIDS mode), Suricata, Zeek (formerly Bro).**

#### B) Host-based IDS (HIDS)

- Installed as an **agent on individual endpoints/servers**.
- Monitors **system calls, log files (syslog, auth.log), file integrity, registry changes**.
- Example: **OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE.**

#### C) Hybrid / Distributed IDS

- Combines multiple NIDS sensors feeding a **central correlation engine**.
- Example: **Cisco Secure IPS, IBM QRadar, Splunk Enterprise Security.**

### 2.1.2 Based on Detection Method (How does it think?)

#### A) Signature-based Detection (Misuse Detection)

- Compares traffic against a **database of known attack patterns (signatures / rules)**.
- Written in rule formats such as Snort's `rule header + rule options`.
- **Pros:** High accuracy, low false positives for known attacks.
- **Cons:** **Zero-day blind** — cannot detect novel attacks.

#### B) Anomaly-based Detection

- Builds a **statistical / behavioral baseline** of "normal" activity.
- Anything deviating beyond threshold τ is flagged.
- Techniques: **Statistical profiling, Machine Learning (SVM, Random Forest, Deep Autoencoders).**
- **Pros:** Can detect **zero-day** attacks.
- **Cons:** High false positive rate (the base rate fallacy).

#### C) Stateful Protocol Analysis

- Understands the **state machine** of protocols (e.g., RFC 793 TCP handshake).
- Detects violations like a SYN scan, half-open connections, or out-of-order flags.

#### D) Hybrid Detection

- Combines signature + anomaly engines in a **parallel or sequential pipeline**.

## 2.2 Classification of IPS

- **Network-based IPS (NIPS):** Inline at gateway, e.g., Cisco Firepower.
- **Host-based IPS (HIPS):** Inline at OS kernel level, e.g., Windows Defender HIPS.
- **Content-based / Application-layer IPS:** Deep packet inspection (DPI) at Layer 7.
- **Rate-based / Threshold IPS:** Triggers on volumetric thresholds (e.g., SYN flood).

## 2.3 IPS Response Actions (The "Prevention" Part)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU expects at least 3 response actions in any 7-mark IPS question.**

- **Drop:** Silently discard the offending packet.
- **Block:** Add source IP to a temporary blacklist (ACL update).
- **Reset (TCP RST):** Send a TCP Reset to tear down the connection.
- **Quarantine:** Move the source to a VLAN jail.
- **Alert + Log:** Forward to SIEM/Syslog server.
- **Rate Limit:** Throttle suspicious source (e.g., 10 pps).

## 2.4 Evasion Techniques (Favoured KTU Sub-topic)

A clever attacker tries to **bypass** the IDS/IPS:

| Technique | Description |
|---|---|
| **Fragmentation** | Splitting payload across many small fragments to hide signature |
| **Encoding** | Using Unicode, URL encoding, or Base64 to obfuscate payload |
| **Encryption** | Tunneling via TLS/HTTPS so DPI cannot inspect payload |
| **TTL Manipulation** | Crafting packets with low TTL so IDS sees them but victim doesn't |
| **Insertion / Evasion (Ptacek & Newsham 1998)** | Sending packets the IDS accepts but the host rejects, confusing the reassembly |
| **Protocol Mismatch** | Exploiting differences in how OS and IDS reassemble TCP streams |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize units.**

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (DR) / True Positive Rate (TPR) | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Probability an attack is correctly flagged |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Probability benign traffic is falsely flagged |
| False Negative Rate (FNR) | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Probability attack slips through |
| Precision (P) | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many are real attacks |
| Recall / Sensitivity | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Same as Detection Rate |
| F1-Score (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balances Precision and Recall |
| Accuracy | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| Specificity (TNR) | $TNR = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Correct rejection of benign traffic |
| Bayesian Posterior (Alert is Real) | $P(A \vert Alert) = \frac{P(Alert \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(Alert)}$ | Used in the **Base Rate Fallacy** proof |
| Matthew's Correlation Coefficient (MCC) | $MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust metric for imbalanced datasets |
| ROC-AUC | $AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Measures separability of classes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are the **gold standard** used by SIEM vendors (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic Security) to tune detection rules. In real SOC operations, lowering FPR is often more valued than raising DR, because alert fatigue causes analysts to ignore the dashboard entirely.

## 2.6 The Base Rate Fallacy — A KTU Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield conceptual question worth 3-5 marks.** "Explain the base rate fallacy in the context of IDS."

Suppose an IDS has **99% accuracy** (1% false positive rate, 99% true positive rate). The actual prior probability of an attack is $P(A) = 0.0001$ (1 in 10,000 packets).

Using **Bayes' Theorem**, the probability that an alert is a **real attack** is:

$$P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)}$$

This will be expanded numerically in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: The Base Rate Fallacy

> **Problem:** An IDS claims **99% true positive rate (sensitivity)** and **99% specificity**. In a corporate network of 1,000,000 packets per day, only **0.01%** (i.e., 100 packets) are actual attacks. Compute the **probability that a raised alert is a genuine attack**.

### Step 1: Define the Known Values

Let $A$ = the event that a packet is malicious.
Let $B$ = the event that the IDS raises an alert.

- Prior probability of attack: $P(A) = 0.0001$
- Prior probability of benign traffic: $P(\neg A) = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$
- Sensitivity (True Positive Rate): $P(B \mid A) = 0.99$
- Specificity (True Negative Rate): $P(\neg B \mid \neg A) = 0.99$
- False Positive Rate: $P(B \mid \neg A) = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$

### Step 2: Set Up Bayes' Theorem

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{0.99 \times 0.0001}{0.99 \times 0.0001 + 0.01 \times 0.9999}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Numerator

$$0.99 \times 0.0001 = 0.000099$$

### Step 5: Compute the Denominator

$$0.000099 + (0.01 \times 0.9999) = 0.000099 + 0.009999 = 0.010098$$

### Step 6: Final Division

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{0.000099}{0.010098} \approx 0.0098 \text{ (i.e., less than 1%)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even with a 99%-accurate IDS, **less than 1% of the alerts** correspond to real attacks. The other 99% are false positives. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**, and it is the **fundamental reason** why anomaly-based IDS in low-traffic networks suffers from alert fatigue.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Detection Rate, FPR, Precision, F1-Score

> **Problem:** During a red-team engagement, an IDS generated the following confusion matrix for 1000 events:
> - True Positives (TP) = 50
> - False Positives (FP) = 30
> - False Negatives (FN) = 10
> - True Negatives (TN) = 910
>
> **Compute:** Detection Rate (TPR), FPR, Precision, F1-Score, and Accuracy.

### Step 1: Compute the Detection Rate (TPR / Recall)

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 \text{ or } 83.33\%$$

### Step 2: Compute the False Positive Rate

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 910} = \frac{30}{940} = 0.0319 \text{ or } 3.19\%$$

### Step 3: Compute Precision

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 30} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625 \text{ or } 62.5\%$$

### Step 4: Compute F1-Score

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.625 \times 0.8333}{0.625 + 0.8333}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5208}{1.4583} = 2 \cdot 0.3571 = 0.7142$$

### Step 5: Compute Accuracy

$$Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{50 + 910}{1000} = \frac{960}{1000} = 0.96 \text{ or } 96\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that **Accuracy is 96%** but **F1 is only 0.71**. This is why F1 (or MCC) is preferred over accuracy in highly imbalanced security datasets where attacks are rare.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Signature vs. Anomaly Detection

| Evaluation Criterion | **Signature-based IDS** | **Anomaly-based IDS** |
|---|---|---|
| Detection of Known Attacks | **Excellent (near 100%)** | Good |
| Detection of Zero-Day | **Poor (Blind)** | **Moderate to Good** |
| False Positive Rate | **Very Low** | **High** |
| False Negative Rate | Moderate (for novel attacks) | Moderate |
| Computational Cost | Low (string matching) | High (statistical/ML inference) |
| Maintenance Overhead | Signature DB updates | Baseline retraining |
| Example | Snort, Suricata, ClamAV | Anomaly-based WAF, AWS GuardDuty |

## 3.4 ROC Curve Interpretation (High-Yield Diagram Concept)

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** plots **TPR (y-axis)** against **FPR (x-axis)** as the detection threshold τ is varied.

- **τ → very high (strict):** FPR ↓, TPR ↓ (almost no alerts, attacks missed).
- **τ → very low (lenient):** FPR ↑, TPR ↑ (too many alerts, alert fatigue).
- **Optimal operating point:** Closest to the **top-left corner (0,1)**.
- **AUC = 0.5** is random guessing; **AUC = 1.0** is a perfect classifier.

## 3.5 Honeypots — A Related Concept Often Bundled with IDS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU often adds a 2-mark sub-question:** "What is a honeypot?"

A **honeypot** is a **decoy system** deployed to attract attackers, study their techniques, and divert them from real production assets. Variants include:

- **Low-interaction honeypot:** Emulates services (e.g., Cowrie, Dionaea). Safe, lightweight.
- **High-interaction honeypot:** Real OS, vulnerable apps. Higher risk, rich telemetry.
- **Honeynet:** Network of honeypots.

It is **passive** (it does not block) and complements an IDS by serving as an **early warning system** and **intelligence source**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Network-Based IDS (NIDS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Internet / External Network] --> nodeB[Perimeter Firewall]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Core Switch / Router]
    nodeC -->|SPAN Port Mirror| nodeD[NIDS Sensor Tap]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Signature Engine]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Anomaly Engine]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Correlation and Decision Engine]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|Alerts| nodeI[Management Console]
    nodeH -->|Logs| nodeJ[SIEM and Event Database]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Security Analyst]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeC -->|Live Traffic Continues| nodeL[Internal LAN and Servers]
```

> **Diagram Note:** The NIDS is **out-of-band** (passive) — it only receives mirrored traffic and never sits in the live data path. The live traffic continues to the internal LAN unaffected.

## 4.2 Architecture of a Network-Based IPS (NIPS) — Inline Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Internet] --> nodeB[Edge Firewall]
    nodeB --> nodeC[NIPS Inline Bridge]
    nodeC -->|1 Inspect Packet| nodeD[Detection Engine]
    nodeD -->|2a Malicious| nodeE[Drop or Reset Action]
    nodeD -->|2b Benign| nodeF[Forward to Internal Network]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Alert to SIEM]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Internal LAN]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Security Operations Center SOC]
```

> **Diagram Note:** The NIPS is **inline**. Every packet is held and inspected. If malicious, the IPS issues a **drop/reset** and never forwards the packet. This introduces latency but provides active prevention.

## 4.3 IDS/IPS Placement Strategy in a Layered Defense

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[External Internet] --> nodeB[Layer 1: Edge Firewall]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Layer 2: DMZ IPS Inline]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Layer 3: Core Switch with NIDS Tap]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Layer 4: Internal Firewall]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Layer 5: Host Based IPS on Critical Servers]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Database and Application Servers]
    nodeD -->|Mirrored Traffic| nodeH[SIEM Correlation Server]
    nodeC -->|Blocked Event Logs| nodeH
    nodeF -->|Host Alerts| nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
```

> **Best Practice (KTU expects this):** Defense-in-Depth — combine perimeter firewall + DMZ IPS + internal NIDS + host-based IPS. Each layer has a distinct role.

## 4.4 Anomaly Detection Pipeline (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Raw Network Traffic] --> nodeB[Feature Extraction]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Statistical Baseline Model]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Live Behavior Profile]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Live Stream Features]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Deviation greater than threshold tau}
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Flag as Anomaly]
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH[Mark Normal]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Alert and Log]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Update Baseline]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram: IDS vs IPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDS_Sub [IDS Subsystem - Passive]
        nodeA1[Packet Capture from SPAN] --> nodeA2[Detection Engine]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Alert Generator]
        nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Log to SIEM Only]
    end

    subgraph IPS_Sub [IPS Subsystem - Inline]
        nodeB1[Receive Packet Inline] --> nodeB2{Is Malicious?}
        nodeB2 -->|Yes| nodeB3[Action: Drop or Reset]
        nodeB2 -->|No| nodeB4[Forward to Destination]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB5[Log and Alert]
        nodeB4 --> nodeB6[Continue Normal Flow]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) in terms of operational mode and response capability.**

**Model Answer:**
An **IDS** is a **passive security mechanism** that monitors network or host activity for malicious behavior. It operates in **out-of-band mode** (typically connected to a SPAN port or network tap) and **does not sit in the live data path**. Upon detecting an intrusion, it generates an **alert and logs the event** but **does not block** the traffic. Examples include Snort in IDS mode and Suricata.

An **IPS**, on the other hand, is an **active, inline security mechanism**. All traffic **must flow through it**. It performs the same detection analysis as an IDS but additionally takes **automated preventive actions** such as dropping malicious packets, sending TCP RST resets, blocking source IPs via ACLs, or quarantining sessions. Examples include Snort Inline, Cisco Firepower, and Suricata in IPS mode.

The key difference is that **IDS = detect and alert**, while **IPS = detect, alert, and block**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three evasion techniques used by attackers to bypass signature-based IDS.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Packet Fragmentation:** Splitting the attack payload across many small IP fragments so that the IDS cannot reconstruct the full signature, while the target host reassembles them.
2. **Payload Encoding / Obfuscation:** Using URL encoding, Unicode, Base64, or double-encoding to hide the malicious string from signature matching.
3. **Encryption (TLS Tunneling):** Wrapping the attack inside HTTPS/TLS so the IDS cannot perform deep packet inspection.
4. **TTL Manipulation:** Crafting packets with low TTL values so the IDS records them but they expire before reaching the victim host.
5. **Insertion and Evasion Attacks (Ptacek-Newsham 1998):** Sending packets that the IDS interprets differently from the end host, causing reassembly mismatch.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q.A)** 
**(a)** Classify Intrusion Detection Systems based on **data source** and **detection method**. Explain each category with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Network-based IDS (NIDS)**. Compare it with a Host-based IDS (HIDS) in terms of deployment, visibility, and resource consumption. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of IDS:**

**1. Based on Data Source:**

- **Network-based IDS (NIDS):** Monitors traffic across the entire network segment by capturing packets via a network tap or SPAN port. It provides a **bird's-eye view** of the network. Example: **Snort, Suricata, Zeek.**
- **Host-based IDS (HIDS):** Installed as an agent on a specific host or server. It monitors **system calls, log files, file integrity, and registry changes** on that host only. Example: **OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE.**
- **Hybrid/Distributed IDS:** Combines multiple sensors (NIDS + HIDS) feeding a central correlation engine. Example: **IBM QRadar, Cisco Secure IPS.**

**2. Based on Detection Method:**

- **Signature-based (Misuse Detection):** Compares traffic against a database of known attack patterns. High accuracy for known attacks, **zero blind to novel threats.** Example: **Snort rules, ClamAV.**
- **Anomaly-based Detection:** Establishes a baseline of normal behavior; flags deviations. Can detect **zero-day attacks** but suffers from **high false positive rates.** Techniques include statistical profiling and machine learning (SVM, Autoencoders).
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Understands protocol state machines (e.g., TCP handshake) and flags violations. Useful for detecting scans and malformed packets.
- **Hybrid Detection:** Combines both signature and anomaly engines.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Architecture of NIDS (Block Diagram):**

A NIDS consists of:
1. **Packet Capture Module:** Uses libpcap/WinPcap to capture packets from a SPAN port or network tap.
2. **Pre-processor / Decoder:** Decodes protocol layers (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP).
3. **Detection Engine:** Runs signature matching and anomaly detection rules.
4. **Alert/Logging Module:** Generates alerts and writes to a database or SIEM.
5. **Management Console:** Web/CLI interface for the analyst to view alerts and tune rules.

**Comparison Table (NIDS vs HIDS):**

| Parameter | **NIDS** | **HIDS** |
|---|---|---|
| Deployment Location | Network chokepoint (SPAN/Tap) | On individual host |
| Visibility | Sees entire network segment | Sees only local host activity |
| Resource Consumption | External sensor, no host impact | Consumes host CPU, RAM, disk |
| Encrypted Traffic Analysis | Limited (needs MITM proxy) | Can see post-decryption (TLS terminated) |
| Cost | Lower (one sensor for many hosts) | Higher (one agent per host) |
| Detection of Internal Threats | Weaker (sees at chokepoint only) | Stronger (monitors local file/process changes) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q.B)**
**(a)** Explain the **Base Rate Fallacy** in the context of Intrusion Detection Systems. An IDS has a true positive rate of 95% and a false positive rate of 5%. In a network where only 1 in 10,000 packets is malicious, compute the probability that a raised alert corresponds to an actual attack. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a Network-based IPS (NIPS)**. List any four **active response actions** performed by an IPS when malicious traffic is detected. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Explanation of Base Rate Fallacy:**
The Base Rate Fallacy is a statistical phenomenon where the **prior probability (base rate) of an event** is ignored when interpreting the result of a classifier. In IDS, even a highly accurate (e.g., 99%) detector can produce **overwhelmingly more false alerts than true alerts** when the actual attack rate in the network is extremely low (e.g., 0.01%). This leads to **alert fatigue**, where SOC analysts begin ignoring the dashboard.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- True Positive Rate (Sensitivity): $P(Alert \mid Attack) = 0.95$
- False Positive Rate: $P(Alert \mid \neg Attack) = 0.05$
- Prior probability of attack: $P(Attack) = \frac{1}{10000} = 0.0001$
- $P(\neg Attack) = 0.9999$

Using **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(Attack \mid Alert) = \frac{P(Alert \mid Attack) \cdot P(Attack)}{P(Alert \mid Attack) \cdot P(Attack) + P(Alert \mid \neg Attack) \cdot P(\neg Attack)}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$P(Attack \mid Alert) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.0001}{0.95 \times 0.0001 + 0.05 \times 0.9999}$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$0.95 \times 0.0001 = 0.000095$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$0.000095 + (0.05 \times 0.9999) = 0.000095 + 0.049995 = 0.05009$$

**Final result:**

$$P(Attack \mid Alert) = \frac{0.000095}{0.05009} \approx 0.001896 \approx 0.19\%$$

**Conclusion:** Out of every 10,000 alerts, only about **19 will be real attacks**. The remaining 9,981 are false positives. This numerical result vividly demonstrates why the base rate fallacy cripples anomaly-based IDS in real-world deployment.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**NIPS Architecture (Inline Block Diagram):**

A Network-based IPS is deployed **inline** at the network perimeter (typically between the firewall and the core switch). The architecture includes:

1. **Packet Reception Module:** All inbound and outbound packets pass through the IPS.
2. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) Engine:** Inspects headers and payloads up to Layer 7.
3. **Detection Engine:** Applies signature rules, anomaly profiles, and protocol state checks.
4. **Decision Module:** If malicious → invoke response action; if benign → forward packet.
5. **Response Engine:** Executes preventive actions.
6. **Logging and Reporting:** Sends to SIEM and consoles.

**Active Response Actions (Any Four):**

1. **Drop:** Silently discard the malicious packet, so it never reaches the destination.
2. **TCP Reset:** Send a TCP RST packet to both endpoints to terminate the connection.
3. **Block Source IP:** Dynamically add the source IP to a temporary Access Control List (ACL) blacklist.
4. **Quarantine / VLAN Reassignment:** Move the offending host to an isolated VLAN for forensic analysis.
5. **Rate Limiting:** Throttle traffic from the source to a configurable packets-per-second cap.
6. **Alert Escalation:** Forward the alert with packet payload to a SIEM for further correlation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS:** Students frequently write "IDS blocks traffic" — this is **incorrect** and costs 1-2 marks. IDS **only alerts**.
> 2. **Forgetting the inline property:** When describing IPS, you **must** mention "inline" or "in the data path." Vague descriptions lose marks.
> 3. **In the Base Rate Fallacy question,** failing to **state Bayes' Theorem explicitly** before substituting values costs 1 mark. Always write the formula first.
> 4. **Mixing up NIDS and HIDS:** NIDS sees packets, HIDS sees logs and system calls. Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Numerical Errors in Bayes' Theorem:** A common mistake is to use $1 - 0.0001 = 0.0009$ instead of $0.9999$. Double-check the complement.
> 6. **Ignoring units / percentages:** Final answer should be expressed as a percentage (e.g., 0.19% or 0.0019), not just a decimal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ **IDS = Detect + Alert + Passive (out-of-band). IPS = Detect + Block + Active (inline).**
- ✅ The **inline** property of IPS makes it a **single point of failure** — failure can black out the network.
- ✅ **NIDS** monitors network traffic via SPAN port; **HIDS** monitors host logs, system calls, and file integrity.
- ✅ **Signature-based detection** cannot detect zero-day attacks; **anomaly-based** can but suffers from high FPR.
- ✅ **Snort** is the most cited example in KTU papers — know its rule format: `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (msg:"..."; sid:...)`.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is essential — memorize: $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) P(A)}{P(B)}$.
- ✅ **Base Rate Fallacy:** 99% accurate IDS in a 1-in-10,000 attack environment still produces <1% true alerts among all alerts raised.
- ✅ **Key Metrics:** TPR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Accuracy, Specificity, MCC, AUC.
- ✅ **IPS Response Actions:** Drop, Block, Reset (TCP RST), Quarantine, Rate Limit, Alert.
- ✅ **Evasion Techniques:** Fragmentation, Encoding, Encryption, TTL manipulation, Insertion/Evasion.
- ✅ **Honeypot:** Decoy system to attract attackers; low-interaction (safe) vs high-interaction (rich telemetry).
