- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** Combine firewall + NIPS (DMZ) + NIDS (internal) + HIPS (hosts) + SIEM (correlation).
- ✅ **ROC Curve:** TPR vs FPR plot; the closer to the top-left corner, the better the classifier.
- ✅ **F1-Score is preferred over Accuracy** in imbalanced datasets (security data is always imbalanced).
- ✅ **Snort rule example to remember:** `alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 (msg:"HTTP attack"; content:"/etc/passwd"; sid:1000001;)`.
- ✅ **Ptacek & Newsham (1998)** seminal paper on IDS evasion — frequently cited in KTU reference material.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Network Security: Wireless Encryption Protocols (WEP, WPA2)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wireless Network Security: Wireless Encryption Protocols (WEP, WPA2) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Wireless Encryption Protocols — Formal Definition A **Wireless Encryption Protocol** is a cryptographic mechanism defined within the IEEE 802.11 family of standards that protects the confidentiality and integrity of frames transmitted over the radio-frequency (RF) air interface of a Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN). Because the wireless medium is a *shared, broadcast, and physically exposed* channel, every adjacent device can passively sniff frames; encryption protocols are therefore mandatory countermeasures against eavesdropping, frame injection, and session hijacking. The two protocols covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus — **WEP** and **WPA2** — represent opposite ends of the security evolution of 802.11: > [!IMPORTANT] > **WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy)** — defined in the original IEEE **802.11 (1997)** standard. Its stated goal was to provide security *equivalent* to that of a wired LAN. It uses the **RC4 stream cipher** with a 24-bit IV concatenated to a 40-bit (or 104-bit) static secret key. It is now **cryptographically broken** and is presented in the syllabus primarily as a *case study of design failure*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **WPA2 (Wi-Fi Protected Access 2)** — ratified in **2004** as the certified implementation of the IEEE **802.11i** amendment. It mandates the **AES-CCMP** (Advanced Encryption Standard in Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol) suite and introduces a **4-Way Handshake**, **Robust Security Network (RSN)** information elements, and either **PSK** (Personal) or **802.1X/EAP** (Enterprise) authentication. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whispering Hallway" Imagine a long, open hallway (the radio spectrum) where every student (every Wi-Fi device) can hear every conversation. There is no wall, no door, no lock. * **Unencrypted Wi-Fi** is equivalent to shouting your secrets down that hallway. Anyone with a paper cone (a packet sniffer like Wireshark) can read every word. * **WEP** is like the same shout, but whispered through a simple *paper cup-and-string telephone*. The cup and string (the RC4 keystream) are made from a tiny, fixed set of materials (the 40-bit key), and you reuse the *same short piece of string* (24-bit IV) for every conversation. After a few hours of eavesdropping, an attacker can figure out the material, recreate the cup-and-string, and listen in forever. * **WPA2** is like a **bank vault with two keys, a biometric scanner, and a fresh combination dialed for every visit**. Each session generates a *new* key (the 4-Way Handshake + PTK), the lock mechanism is mathematically certified (AES-128 in CCMP), and even if one conversation is recorded, it cannot be replayed because the combination changes every time. > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** WEP illustrates *how not to design* a wireless protocol; WPA2 illustrates the *modern* design principles (fresh keys per session, mutual authentication, strong block ciphers, integrity via MAC). ### 1.3 Why Wireless Encryption Is a Harder Problem Than Wired Encryption On a wired Ethernet switch, only the intended recipient receives the frame (in unicast). On wireless: 1. The medium is **broadcast** — every station in range receives every frame. 2. **Association / authentication** is itself a wireless message exchange and so is exposed to forgery. 3. **Replay** is trivial because RF frames can be captured and re-injected. 4. **Mobility** forces frequent re-keying as stations roam between APs. These constraints drove the need for the layered security architecture seen in WPA2. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 WEP — Architecture and Operational Logic WEP was intended to be lightweight, so it uses the **RC4 stream cipher**, which is a symmetric-key cipher that produces a *pseudorandom keystream* XORed with the plaintext. **WEP Encryption Process (logical steps):** 1. A **24-bit Initialization Vector (IV)** is generated (often by incrementing a counter or using a weak PRNG). 2. The IV is concatenated with the **shared secret key** $K$ (40 bits in WEP-64, 104 bits in WEP-128) to form the RC4 seed: $S = IV \,\|\, K$ (total 64 or 128 bits). 3. RC4 produces a keystream $KS$ of length equal to the plaintext: $KS = \text{RC4}(S)$. 4. The **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** is computed as $ICV = \text{CRC-32}(P)$ over the plaintext $P$. 5. The ciphertext frame body is built as: $C = (P \,\|\, ICV) \oplus KS$. 6. The IV is transmitted **in the clear** in the frame header; the receiver knows $K$ and can regenerate $KS$. **WEP Decryption** simply reverses the XOR: $P \,\|\, ICV' = C \oplus KS$, and the receiver checks that $\text{CRC-32}(P) = ICV'$. **The Five Fatal Weaknesses of WEP (high-yield for KTU 14-mark answers):** > [!IMPORTANT] > 1. **IV Reuse / Collision:** The 24-bit IV space has only $2^{24} \approx 16.7$ million values. On a busy AP, the **birthday paradox** guarantees a collision in roughly $\sqrt{2^{24}} \approx 4096$ frames — within minutes. > 2. **Weak IVs (FMS attack):** Fluhrer, Mantin, and Shamir (2001) showed that certain IVs leak the secret key bytes directly. Tools like **Aircrack-ng** can recover a 104-bit WEP key after capturing ~40,000–85,000 frames. > 3. **CRC-32 is linear, not cryptographic.** It is an *integrity check*, not a *message authentication code*; bit-flipping attacks (e.g., the **chopchop attack** by KoreK) can decrypt ciphertexts without the key. > 4. **No replay protection** — the same ciphertext can be reinjected. > 5. **No mutual authentication** — only the AP is implicitly trusted; there is no proof that the client is talking to a legitimate AP (vulnerable to **evil twin** / rogue AP attacks). ### 2.2 WPA2 — Architecture and Operational Logic WPA2 is the certified implementation of **IEEE 802.11i**. Its security architecture is built around the **Robust Security Network (RSN)**. **Core Components of WPA2:** * **AES-CCMP** — the mandatory encryption suite. CCMP = Counter Mode (CTR) for confidentiality + CBC-MAC (Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code) for integrity and authenticity. * **TKIP** (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) — retained from WPA for *legacy* compatibility; uses RC4 internally but adds per-packet key mixing, extended IV, and **Michael MIC**. WPA2 deprecates TKIP. * **PMK (Pairwise Master Key)** — the long-term secret. In WPA2-Personal, it is derived from the **PSK** (Pre-Shared Key) + SSID. In WPA2-Enterprise, it is delivered by the **802.1X/EAP** authentication to an Authentication Server (typically RADIUS). * **4-Way Handshake** — establishes fresh *temporal* keys between AP and client. * **Group Key Handshake** — distributes the GTK (Group Transient Key) for broadcast/multicast. **AES-CCMP Frame Construction (operational logic):** 1. The **48-bit Packet Number (PN)** is incremented for every frame (replaces WEP's IV). 2. A **temporal key (TK, 128 bits)** is used as the seed material. 3. A **CCM Nonce** is constructed: 48-bit PN $\vert$ 16-bit A2 (Address 2 / source address) $\vert$ 8-bit Priority. 4. **AES-CTR** encrypts the plaintext under a counter sequence derived from the nonce and TK. 5. **AES-CBC-MAC** computes an **8-byte MIC (Message Integrity Code)** over the MAC header (with masked fields) + CCMP header + plaintext. 6. The output frame contains: **CCMP Header (8 bytes) with PN** $\vert$ **Encrypted Payload** $\vert$ **MIC (8 bytes)**. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | WEP Seed | $S = IV \,\Vert\, K$ | $IV$ is **24 bits**; $K$ is **40 bits** (WEP-64) or **104 bits** (WEP-128) | | WEP Ciphertext | $C = (P \,\Vert\, \text{CRC-32}(P)) \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \,\Vert\, K)$ | XOR with keystream | | WEP IV space | $\vert \text{IV space} \vert = 2^{24}$ | $\approx 1.67 \times 10^{7}$ values | | Birthday collision (WEP) | $n \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot 2^{24}} \approx 5792$ frames | ~50% collision probability | | CRC-32 size | $\vert ICV \vert = 32$ bits | Linear — not cryptographic | | AES key size (CCMP) | $\vert TK \vert = 128$ bits | Block size = 128 bits | | CCMP PN | $\vert PN \vert = 48$ bits | Replay-protected, monotonically increasing | | MIC (CCMP) | $\vert MIC \vert = 64$ bits | Computed by AES-CBC-MAC | | PSK (WPA2-Personal) | $PSK = \text{PBKDF2}(passphrase, SSID, 4096, 256)$ | 256-bit output | | PMK = PSK | $PMK = PSK$ (Personal mode) | 256 bits | | PTK derivation | $PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{``Pairwise key expansion''}, AA \,\Vert\, SA \,\Vert\, ANonce \,\Vert\, SNonce)$ | 384 bits total | | PTK components | $PTK = TK \,\Vert\, MIC_{TX} \,\Vert\, MIC_{RX} \,\Vert\, EAPOL-Key\_ENC\_KEY \,\Vert\, EAPOL-Key\_Key\_IV$ | 128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 64 = 576 bits *or* 384 bits in some implementations | | GTK distribution | $GTK$ wrapped with $KEK$ (Key Encryption Key) part of PTK | 128 bits encrypted under AES-Key-Wrap | | Aircrack-ng WEP key recovery | $\approx 40{,}000$ to $85{,}000$ IVs | Empirical figure | | KRACK attack (2017) | Reinstalls same TK via 4-Way Msg 3 replay | Forces nonce reuse on CCMP | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive WEP Encryption Walkthrough Let us encrypt a plaintext frame $P$ = `"HELLO"` (40 bits) with a 40-bit shared key $K$ and a chosen IV. **Given:** - $K = \text{0x1234567890}$ (5 bytes = 40 bits) - $IV = \text{0xABCDEF}$ (3 bytes = 24 bits) - $P = \text{0x48454C4C4F}$ (ASCII "HELLO", 5 bytes = 40 bits) **Step 1: Concatenate IV and Key to form the RC4 seed** $$ S = IV \,\Vert\, K = \text{0xABCDEF1234567890} \;\; (\text{8 bytes} = 64 \text{ bits}) $$ **Step 2: Run RC4 key-scheduling algorithm (KSA) and PRGA to generate keystream** The RC4 state consists of a 256-byte permutation $S_{rc4}[0..255]$, initialized via the KSA. After KSA and PRGA, RC4 emits a keystream of length equal to the plaintext + ICV (here, 9 bytes): $$ KS = \text{RC4}(S) = k_0, k_1, k_2, \dots, k_8 $$ where each $k_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, 255\}$. **Step 3: Compute the Integrity Check Value (ICV)** $$ ICV = \text{CRC-32}(P) = \text{CRC-32}(\text{0x48454C4C4F}) $$ Suppose (illustratively) $ICV = \text{0xABCD1234}$ (32 bits). **Step 4: Concatenate plaintext and ICV, then XOR with keystream** $$ C = (P \,\Vert\, ICV) \oplus KS $$ Written out bit-by-bit (assuming illustrative $KS$ values): $$ \begin{aligned} P \,\Vert\, ICV &= \text{0x48454C4C4F}\,\Vert\,\text{0xABCD1234} \\ &= \text{0x48454C4C4FABCD1234} \quad (\text{9 bytes, 72 bits}) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_i &= (P_i \,\Vert\, ICV_i) \oplus KS_i, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \dots, 8 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Transmit $IV$ in clear in the frame header; send $C$ as the encrypted body.** The receiver, knowing $K$, regenerates $KS$ from $IV \,\Vert\, K$, XORs to recover $(P \,\Vert\, ICV')$, recomputes CRC-32, and checks equality. ### 3.2 Exhaustive WPA2 4-Way Handshake Derivation **Given (assumed values for derivation):** - $PMK$ = a 256-bit master key (derived from PSK or 802.1X). - $AA$ = MAC address of the Authenticator (AP), e.g., $\text{00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E}$. - $SA$ = MAC address of the Supplicant (Client), e.g., $\text{00:6F:7G:8H:9I:0J}$. - $ANonce$ = AP nonce (random 256 bits). - $SNonce$ = Client nonce (random 256 bits). **Step 1: Client and AP each independently compute the PTK** $$ PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{``Pairwise key expansion''}, \min(AA,SA) \,\Vert\, \max(AA,SA) \,\Vert\, \min(ANonce, SNonce) \,\Vert\, \max(ANonce, SNonce)) $$ **Step 2: Parse the 384-bit PTK into five sub-keys** $$ \begin{aligned} PTK[0:128] &\rightarrow TK \quad \text{(Temporal Key, 128 bits)} \\ PTK[128:256] &\rightarrow \text{MIC}_{TX} \quad \text{(for AP→STA frames)} \\ PTK[256:384] &\rightarrow \text{MIC}_{RX} \quad \text{(for STA→AP frames)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ (The remaining portion holds KEK and the EAPOL-Key IV, used to wrap the GTK. The exact split depends on the cipher suite; CCMP-128 uses a 384-bit PTK with the above structure.) **Step 3: Message 1 (AP → Client): ANonce transmission** The AP sends an EAPOL-Key frame containing $ANonce$ but **without** a MIC. The client now has all four ingredients to derive the PTK. **Step 4: Message 2 (Client → AP): SNonce + MIC** The client sends its own $SNonce$ and computes: $$ MIC_2 = \text{MIC}(EAPOL-Key\_frame_2, \text{MIC}_{RX}) $$ The AP, which also knows $K$ and has received $SNonce$, derives the same PTK and validates $MIC_2$. **Step 5: Message 3 (AP → Client): GTK delivery + install flag** The AP: - Sends the **GTK encrypted** under the $KEK$ (Key Encryption Key, part of PTK). - Sets the **Install** bit to 1, instructing the client to install the TK. - Sends its own $MIC_3 = \text{MIC}(EAPOL-Key\_frame_3, \text{MIC}_{TX})$. **Step 6: Message 4 (Client → AP): Acknowledgement** The client sends $MIC_4$ to confirm TK installation. After this, both parties are ready to use CCMP-encrypted unicast data frames. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | WEP | WPA2 | |---|---|---| | Standard | IEEE 802.11 (1997) | IEEE 802.11i (2004) | | Cipher | RC4 (stream) | AES-CCMP (block, CTR + CBC-MAC) | | Key size | 40 or 104 bits | 128 bits (TK) | | IV/PN size | 24 bits | 48 bits (PN) | | Integrity | CRC-32 (linear) | CBC-MAC (cryptographic, 64-bit MIC) | | Authentication | Open / Shared Key (one-way) | PSK or 802.1X (mutual) | | Key management | Static / manual | Per-session PTK via 4-Way Handshake | | Replay protection | None | Monotonic PN enforced by receiver | | Current status | **Deprecated / broken** | **Standard, with KRACK caveats** | | KRACK-resistant? | N/A | No (vulnerable pre-2018 firmware) | > [!NOTE] > The **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack)** disclosed by Vanhoef and Piessens in 2017 exploits a flaw in the 4-Way Handshake: by replaying Message 3, an attacker can force the client to reinstall an *already-used* TK, which resets the PN counter and allows CTR-mode keystream reuse. Modern patches (802.11w / PMF — Protected Management Frames) mitigate this. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 WEP Encryption Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Plaintext Frame P] --> ICV1[CRC-32 Generator] ICV1 --> ICVout[ICV 32 bits] P --> Concat[Concatenation Block] ICVout --> Concat Concat --> Padded[P plus ICV 64 plus 32 bits] IVgen[24 bit IV generator] --> IVout[IV 24 bits] K[Shared Secret Key K 40 or 104 bits] --> Concat2[Concatenation IV plus K] IVout --> Concat2 Concat2 --> Seed[RC4 Seed 64 or 128 bits] Seed --> RC4[RC4 Stream Cipher] RC4 --> KS[Keystream KS] Padded --> XORnode[XOR Operation] KS --> XORnode XORnode --> Cipher[Ciphertext C] IVout --> TxFrame[802.11 Frame Header IV field in clear] Cipher --> TxFrame TxFrame --> Air[Over the air broadcast] ``` ### 4.2 WPA2 4-Way Handshake Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AP as Authenticator (AP) participant STA as Supplicant (Client) Note over AP,STA: Both know PMK from PSK or 802.1X AP->>STA: EAPOL-Key Message 1
ANonce (no MIC) Note over STA: STA derives PTK = PRF-384(PMK, PMK_LABEL, AA, SA, ANonce, SNonce) STA->>AP: EAPOL-Key Message 2
SNonce + MIC over frame using MIC_RX Note over AP: AP derives same PTK, verifies MIC_2 AP->>STA: EAPOL-Key Message 3
ANonce + encrypted GTK + Install=1 + MIC using MIC_TX Note over STA: STA decrypts GTK with KEK, installs TK, verifies MIC_3 STA->>AP: EAPOL-Key Message 4
Confirmation MIC using MIC_RX Note over AP,STA: Both sides now use CCMP with fresh PTK ``` ### 4.3 CCMP Frame Construction Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB AAD[MAC Header AAD] --> CBC[AES CBC MAC engine] Nonce[CCM Nonce 48 PN + 16 A2 + 8 Priority] --> CTR[AES CTR engine] TK[Temporal Key 128 bits] --> CBC TK --> CTR Plain[Plaintext MSDU] --> CTR Plain --> CBC CBC --> MIC[8 byte MIC] CTR --> Enc[Encrypted Payload] MIC --> Frame[Final 802.11 Frame CCMP header plus Enc plus MIC] Enc --> Frame PN[48 bit Packet Number] --> Frame Frame --> Air[Over the air] ``` ### 4.4 Protocol Evolution Timeline (Block Architecture View) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Open 802.11 1997] --> B1[WEP RC4 40 or 104 bit] B1 --> C1[WPA TKIP 2003 transitional] C1 --> D1[WPA2 AES CCMP 2004 IEEE 802.11i] D1 --> E1[WPA3 SAE 2018 IEEE 802.11ax era] Note1[Each arrow represents a hardened replacement] -.-> B1 Note1 -.-> C1 Note1 -.-> D1 Note1 -.-> E1 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]** *Explain the role of the 24-bit Initialization Vector (IV) in WEP. Why does its small size constitute a cryptographic weakness?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The IV is a 24-bit per-frame value prepended to the shared secret key to form the RC4 seed. It ensures that two frames encrypted with the same key do not produce the same keystream. However, the IV space has only $2^{24} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{7}$ values. By the **birthday paradox**, IV collisions occur with high probability after about $2^{12} \approx 4096$ frames. Once two ciphertexts share an IV, XORing them cancels the keystream and leaks $P_1 \oplus P_2$, enabling statistical plaintext recovery. Additionally, certain IV values (the *weak IVs* identified by Fluhrer–Mantin–Shamir) directly leak the secret key bytes. **(3 marks — full allocation)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** *Differentiate between **CCMP** and **TKIP** in the context of WPA/WPA2. State which is mandatory in WPA2 and why.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | TKIP | CCMP | |---|---|---| | Cipher | RC4 (legacy) | AES (block) | | Integrity | Michael MIC (weak by design) | CBC-MAC (cryptographic) | | Key mixing | Per-packet | Per-session (PTK) | | Status in WPA2 | Optional (legacy) | **Mandatory** | CCMP is mandatory in WPA2 because TKIP was retained only for backward compatibility with WPA hardware. CCMP provides strong confidentiality (AES-CTR), strong integrity (AES-CBC-MAC, 64-bit MIC), and a 48-bit PN for replay protection, addressing every major WEP weakness. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **WEP encryption process**. Clearly show the role of IV, RC4, and CRC-32. **(b)** List and explain any **four major security vulnerabilities** of WEP, citing the relevant attack (e.g., FMS, chopchop, IV reuse). #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks:** WEP uses the **RC4 stream cipher** for confidentiality and **CRC-32** for integrity. The encryption process is: 1. A **24-bit IV** is generated for the frame. 2. The IV is concatenated with the **shared secret key** $K$ (40 or 104 bits) to form the RC4 seed. 3. **RC4** produces a pseudorandom keystream $KS$ of length $\vert P \vert + 32$ bits. 4. **CRC-32** is computed over the plaintext $P$ to produce a 32-bit **ICV**. 5. The concatenation $(P \,\Vert\, ICV)$ is XORed with $KS$ to produce the ciphertext $C$. 6. The frame is transmitted with the IV in the clear and $C$ as the encrypted body. A block diagram should show the parallel paths: $IV \,\Vert\, K \rightarrow \text{RC4} \rightarrow KS$, and $P \rightarrow \text{CRC-32} \rightarrow ICV$, both feeding into the XOR with $C$ as output. (See Section 4.1 for the Mermaid diagram.) **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — 7 marks:** 1. **IV Reuse Attack (Birthday Paradox):** $2^{24}$ IV space ⇒ collision in ~4096 frames ⇒ keystream reuse ⇒ $P_1 \oplus P_2$ leaked. **(1.75 marks)** 2. **FMS Attack (Fluhrer–Mantin–Shamir, 2001):** Specific *weak IVs* (resolved keys) leak key bytes; Aircrack-ng recovers 104-bit WEP key from ~40–85k IVs. **(1.75 marks)** 3. **Chopchop Attack (KoreK, 2004):** Exploits the linearity of CRC-32 to decrypt ciphertexts *byte-by-byte* without the key. **(1.75 marks)** 4. **No Mutual Authentication / Evil Twin:** WEP only authenticates the client weakly; an attacker can set up a rogue AP with the same SSID and capture credentials / inject traffic. **(1.75 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **AES-CCMP** encryption mechanism of WPA2. Show how confidentiality and integrity are simultaneously achieved. **(b)** Describe the **4-Way Handshake** of WPA2 in detail, including the derivation of the PTK and its components. #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks:** AES-CCMP uses the AES block cipher in two modes operating on the same 128-bit Temporal Key (TK): * **AES-CTR (Counter Mode) for confidentiality:** A counter block is constructed from the **CCM Nonce** (48-bit PN + 16-bit A2 + 8-bit priority) and a 128-bit TK-derived key. AES encrypts successive counter values; the outputs are XORed with plaintext blocks to produce ciphertext. * **AES-CBC-MAC for integrity and authenticity:** The MAC header (with masked fields), CCMP header, and plaintext are authenticated using CBC-MAC, producing a **64-bit MIC** appended to the frame. Both operations use the **same TK** but with different internal derived keys. The **48-bit PN** is monotonically incremented per frame; the receiver drops any frame whose PN is not strictly greater than the highest seen for that TA/RA pair — providing **replay protection**, which WEP lacked. The 8-byte CCMP Header carries the PN in clear. (See Section 4.3.) **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — 7 marks:** The 4-Way Handshake is the **authenticated key-exchange** of 802.11i, designed to: 1. Confirm that the *peer possesses* the PMK. 2. Derive a fresh PTK. 3. Install cryptographic keys on both sides. 4. Securely transfer the GTK to the client. **Message 1 (AP → Client):** AP sends $ANonce$ in an EAPOL-Key frame. *No MIC yet.* The client now has enough material to compute the PTK. **Message 2 (Client → AP):** Client sends $SNonce$ + $MIC_2$ computed with the freshly derived $MIC_{RX}$ portion of PTK. AP, which also computes the same PTK, verifies the MIC. **Message 3 (AP → Client):** AP sends its $ANonce$ again, the **GTK wrapped under the KEK** (Key Encryption Key, also part of PTK), and $MIC_3$ computed with $MIC_{TX}$. The Install bit tells the client to activate the TK. **Message 4 (Client → AP):** Client sends a confirmation $MIC_4$, completing the handshake. **PTK Derivation Formula (must be memorized for KTU):** $$ PTK = \text{PRF-384}\big(PMK, \text{``Pairwise key expansion''}, AA \,\Vert\, SA \,\Vert\, ANonce \,\Vert\, SNonce\big) $$ The 384-bit PTK is split as $TK \,\Vert\, MIC_{TX} \,\Vert\, MIC_{RX}$ (and, for CCMP-128, additional KEK + EAPOL IV components). After Message 4, both peers switch to CCMP for unicast traffic. **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse TKIP and CCMP.** Many students incorrectly state that WPA2 *uses TKIP*. In strict KTU marking, WPA2 *deprecates* TKIP — the **mandatory** suite is **AES-CCMP**. Saying "WPA2 uses TKIP" will cost at least 1 mark. > 2. **Do NOT say CRC-32 is "secure" or "encrypted"** — it is a *linear* cyclic code, explicitly chosen for *speed*, not security. Calling it a "hash" or "MAC" is incorrect. > 3. **The 4-Way Handshake is NOT the authentication step itself.** Authentication (PSK or 802.1X) happens *before* the 4-Way Handshake and produces the PMK. The 4-Way Handshake is *key confirmation and key derivation*. Conflating the two is a common 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Remember the PN/IV sizes:** WEP IV = **24 bits**; CCMP PN = **48 bits**. This is a frequent 1-mark short-answer question. > 5. **The KRACK attack (2017)** is fair game in modern KTU questions. Be ready to state that it exploits **reinstallation of an already-used TK** by replaying Message 3, forcing **CTR-mode nonce reuse**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **WEP** = RC4 + 24-bit IV + CRC-32; cryptographically **broken** (FMS, chopchop, IV reuse). - **WPA2** = AES-CCMP + 4-Way Handshake + PSK/802.1X; strong, but vulnerable to **KRACK** (patched via 802.11w/PMF). - **IV = 24 bits** in WEP; **PN = 48 bits** in CCMP. - **Key size:** WEP 40/104 bits; WPA2 TK = **128 bits**. - **Integrity:** CRC-32 (linear, weak) vs. AES-CBC-MAC (64-bit MIC, cryptographic). - **PTK formula** $PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{``Pairwise key expansion''}, AA \,\Vert\, SA \,\Vert\, ANonce \,\Vert\, SNonce)$ — must be memorized verbatim. - **PTK sub-keys:** $TK, MIC_{TX}, MIC_{RX}, KEK, EAPOL-Key\_IV$. - **Authentication modes:** WPA2-Personal (PSK + PBKDF2 × 4096) vs. WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X + RADIUS). - **Replay protection:** WEP = none; WPA2 = monotonic 48-bit PN enforced by receiver. - **The 4-Way Handshake establishes keys; it does NOT authenticate the user** (that's PSK or 802.1X). - **CCM = CTR (confidentiality) + CBC-MAC (integrity)** — both AES, both same TK. - **WPA3 (beyond syllabus, FYI):** replaces PSK with **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** to resist offline dictionary attacks.

topics.title: Securing Wireless Access Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT412 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Securing Wireless Access Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Wireless Access Point (WAP)?

A **Wireless Access Point (WAP)** is a networking hardware device that allows Wi-Fi capable devices to connect to a wired network using Wi-Fi or related standards. From a security perspective, a WAP is the **perimeter gateway** of the wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) — the single chokepoint where every wireless frame must pass before entering the trusted internal network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECDT412 – Module 3):**
> *Securing Wireless Access Points* refers to the set of administrative, cryptographic, physical, and protocol-level controls applied to a WAP to prevent unauthorized association, eavesdropping, frame injection, and denial-of-service attacks against the wireless medium (IEEE 802.11 family).

### 1.2 The CIA Triad Applied to Wi-Fi

| CIA Property | Wi-Fi Threat Example | Security Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Packet sniffing with tools like Wireshark / Aircrack-ng | Encrypt every over-the-air frame (WPA2-CCMP / WPA3-GCMP) |
| **Integrity** | Frame injection / bit-flipping attacks | Use cryptographic MIC (Message Integrity Code) |
| **Availability** | De-authentication floods, RF jamming | 802.11w Management Frame Protection (MFP) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club"

Imagine a Wireless Access Point as the **bouncer of an exclusive nightclub**:

- **The bouncer's guest list (MAC Filter)** decides which faces (devices) may enter.
- **The secret password (WPA2/WPA3 Pre-Shared Key)** is whispered at the door — only those who know it get in.
- **A formal ID check (802.1X / EAP)** is required on busy nights — the bouncer radios the central security desk to verify identity.
- **Encrypted conversations inside the club (CCMP / AES-256)** ensure that even if someone presses their ear to the wall, they cannot understand the chatter.
- **The dance floor (the RF air medium)** is open-air — anyone can stand outside and listen. That is *why* encryption and authentication are non-negotiable.

> [!NOTE]
> Wireless signals propagate through walls, windows, and parking lots. An access point with default settings and no encryption is the digital equivalent of leaving a printed ledger of your bank account on a park bench with a sign saying "Free to Read."

## 1.4 Why "Securing" is Harder than Wired Networks

Wired Ethernet is a *closed physical medium* — an attacker needs a cable. Wireless networks broadcast through an inherently **unbounded, shared, and easily intercepted medium** (the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz radio bands). The 802.11 standard was originally designed for **convenience and mobility**, not for adversarial environments. Therefore, security must be **retro-engineered** on top of the physical layer through layered cryptographic protocols.

### 1.5 Standard Security Metrics Used in KTU Problems

- **WEP IV (Initialization Vector) length:** 24 bits
- **WEP Key length:** 40 or 104 bits (effective), 64 or 128 bits (with IV)
- **WPA2-PSK Passphrase:** 8 – 63 ASCII characters
- **WPA3 SAE Iteration Count:** minimum **5** (default 8) iterations of PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256
- **WPA3-Enterprise Cipher:** 128-bit GCMP-256 (optional), default GCMP-128
- **802.1X EAP Re-authentication Timeout:** typically **3600 s** (1 hour)
- **Default RADIUS Port:** UDP **1812** (auth), UDP **1813** (accounting)
- **RADIUS Shared Secret:** minimum **22 characters** (RFC 2865 recommendation)
- **Default WAP Administrative HTTP Port:** **80**, HTTPS: **443**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Point:** The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to differentiate **WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3** evolution, justify why WEP is mathematically broken, and recommend the correct security mode for a given scenario (Home SOHO vs Enterprise Campus).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Wireless Security Threat Landscape

A wireless LAN faces a **fundamentally different threat model** than a wired LAN. Below is the canonical attacker capability matrix used in 2024-scheme questions:

### 2.1.1 Threat Taxonomy

1. **Passive Eavesdropping** — Sniffing 802.11 frames using monitor-mode adapters (e.g., `airodump-ng`). No active transmission needed; almost impossible to detect.
2. **Unauthorized Association** — A rogue client connects to a legitimate AP (bypassing weak security) to gain LAN access.
3. **Rogue / Evil Twin AP** — Attacker broadcasts an AP with the **same SSID** as the legitimate network, luring clients to associate. The attacker then becomes the Man-in-the-Middle (MITM).
4. **MAC Spoofing** — Attacker sniffs a valid MAC address and clones it to bypass MAC filtering.
5. **WEP/WPA-PSK Cracking** — Offline dictionary or statistical attack against the captured 4-way handshake.
6. **Deauthentication / Disassociation Attack** — Forging 802.11 management frames to forcibly disconnect clients, enabling handshake capture.
7. **Jamming (DoS)** — Transmission of high-power RF noise on the channel to disrupt communication.
8. **Replay Attack** — Capturing and re-injecting a valid frame to fool the receiver.
9. **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack)** — Exploiting the 802.11r 4-way handshake by forcing nonce reuse (CVE-2017-13082, 2017).
10. **Birthday / Beck-Tews Attack on WPA-TKIP** — Recovering short keystreams by exploiting Michael MIC weakness (limited, 2010).

### 2.2 Layered Security Model for a WAP

Security is implemented in **four concentric rings** — the KTU examiner expects this hierarchy:

| Layer | Control | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 – Physical / RF** | Antenna direction, transmit power, placement, RF shielding | Reduce signal leakage beyond the building |
| **L2 – Link-Layer (802.11)** | WEP / WPA / WPA2 / WPA3, 802.11w (MFP) | Encrypt frames, authenticate stations |
| **L3 – Network / Identity** | 802.1X, RADIUS, EAP-TLS / PEAP, NAC | Strong per-user authentication |
| **L4 – Administrative / Policy** | SSID strategy, VLAN segmentation, firmware updates, logging, IPS | Defense-in-depth, policy enforcement |

## 2.3 Evolution of 802.11 Security Protocols

### 2.3.1 WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) — IEEE 802.11 (1999)

- **Algorithm:** RC4 stream cipher
- **Key Size:** 40-bit (WEP-64) or 104-bit (WEP-128) + 24-bit IV
- **Integrity:** CRC-32 (linear, **not cryptographic**)
- **Authentication:** Open System or Shared Key (broken)

**Why WEP is dead:**

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / 2 \cdot 2^{24}}$$

For $n = 5000$ frames (≈ 5 minutes of VoIP traffic), the probability that two IVs collide exceeds **99%**, enabling statistical key recovery via the **FMS attack (Fluhrer–Mantin–Shamir, 2001)** and the **KoreK** optimization. Tools like `aircrack-ng` recover a 128-bit WEP key in under **60 seconds** on commodity hardware.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never recommend WEP in any answer. If a question asks "Which protocol is broken?", the answer is *always* WEP.

### 2.3.2 WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) — IEEE 802.11i draft (2003)

- Transitional fix while 802.11i was finalized
- **Encryption:** TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol), still RC4-based but **per-packet key** mixed with IV
- **Integrity:** Michael MIC (still weak — 1 forgery per minute by design to limit DoS)
- **Authentication:** PSK (Pre-Shared Key) or 802.1X

### 2.3.3 WPA2 (IEEE 802.11i, ratified 2004)

- **Mandatory cipher:** CCMP (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol) using **AES-128** in CTR + CBC-MAC mode
- **Authentication:** PSK (Personal) or 802.1X (Enterprise)
- **Key Management:** 4-way handshake (supplicant ↔ authenticator) to derive **PTK** and **GTK**

$$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK}, \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce} \Vert \text{MAC}_A \Vert \text{MAC}_B)$$

Where PRF-384 is the **Pseudo-Random Function** outputting 384 bits split into **KCK (128) + KEK (128) + TK (128)**.

> [!NOTE]
> KRACK (2017) is a vulnerability in the **implementation of the 4-way handshake**, not in AES-CCMP itself. Properly patched WPA2 remains secure.

### 2.3.4 WPA3 (Wi-Fi Alliance, 2018)

- **Personal mode:** SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) — a **Dragonfly Key Exchange** (a Password-Authenticated Key Exchange, PAKE) replacing the 4-way PSK handshake. Provides **forward secrecy** — compromising the password does not decrypt past sessions.
- **Enterprise mode:** 192-bit minimum security suite, **GCMP-256**, mandatory **Protected Management Frames (PMF/802.11w)**
- **Easy Connect:** QR-code or NFC-based device onboarding for IoT

### 2.4 Side-by-Side Comparison (High-Yield Table)

| Feature | WEP | WPA | WPA2-Personal | WPA2-Enterprise | WPA3-Personal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Encryption | RC4-40/104 | RC4 + TKIP | AES-128-CCMP | AES-128-CCMP | AES-128-GCMP |
| Key Mgmt | Static | PSK / 802.1X | PSK | 802.1X + RADIUS | SAE (PAKE) |
| Integrity | CRC-32 | Michael MIC | CBC-MAC | CBC-MAC | GMAC |
| Forward Secrecy | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Offline Dictionary | Trivial | Moderate | Moderate | N/A | **Impractical** (SAE) |
| Status | **Broken** | Deprecated | Acceptable | Strong | **Recommended** |

## 2.5 Authentication Mechanisms

### 2.5.1 Open vs Shared Key vs PSK vs 802.1X

- **Open System:** No authentication, only encryption. Used in captive portals.
- **Shared Key (WEP):** Broken challenge-response. Avoid.
- **PSK (WPA2-Personal):** All clients share a passphrase; the AP derives a **256-bit PMK** using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-1.
- **802.1X (WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise):** Per-user, per-session credentials. Components: **Supplicant (client) ↔ Authenticator (AP) ↔ Authentication Server (RADIUS)**.

$$PMK = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{passphrase}, \text{SSID}, 4096, 256)$$

> The **4096 iterations** of HMAC-SHA-1 make brute-force slower. For **WPA3-SAE**, the iteration count is **5 – 8** of HMAC-SHA-256, but each iteration is computationally heavier per the Dragonfly algorithm.

### 2.5.2 EAP Variants (Common in KTU Questions)

| EAP Type | Credential | Tunnel | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EAP-TLS** | X.509 certificates (mutual) | TLS | Strongest; enterprise gold standard |
| **PEAP** | Username/password (inside TLS) | TLS | Common, server-cert only |
| **EAP-TTLS** | Username/password (inside TLS) | TLS | Versatile |
| **EAP-FAST** | PAC (Protected Access Credential) | TLS | Cisco environments |
| **EAP-SIM / EAP-AKA** | SIM / USIM | None | Cellular interworking |

## 2.6 Cryptographic Math Behind WPA2/WPA3

### 2.6.1 4-Way Handshake (WPA2-Personal)

1. **AP → Client:** ANonce
2. **Client → AP:** SNonce + MIC (proves possession of PMK)
3. **AP → Client:** GTK (encrypted with KEK) + MIC
4. **Client → AP:** Acknowledgement

Result: Both sides hold matching **PTK** and **GTK** without ever sending the PMK over the air.

### 2.6.2 CCMP Encryption (AES-CCM)

CCM = **CTR (confidentiality) + CBC-MAC (integrity)**. The 128-bit AES block cipher is invoked twice per packet: once for encryption, once for the MAC.

### 2.6.3 SAE (WPA3) — Dragonfly Handshake

Two parties $A$ and $B$ with password $p$ compute:

$$\text{PE} = H(\max(M_A, M_B) \Vert \min(M_A, M_B) \Vert \text{password})$$

Where $M = \text{commit scalar} \cdot P$ and $P$ is a generator of an elliptic curve (P-256 by default). This resists offline dictionary attacks because the attacker must complete the handshake per guess.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Term | Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| WEP IV length | **24 bits** |
| WEP key length | **40 / 104 bits** |
| RC4 stream cipher | Used in WEP & WPA-TKIP |
| AES block size | **128 bits** |
| TKIP MIC algorithm | Michael |
| CCMP cipher | **AES-128-CCM** |
| WPA3 cipher (Personal) | **AES-128-GCMP** |
| WPA3 cipher (Enterprise 192-bit) | **AES-256-GCMP + SHA-384** |
| PMK length | **256 bits** |
| PTK length | **384 bits** (KCK 128 + KEK 128 + TK 128) |
| GTK length | **128 / 256 bits** |
| ANonce / SNonce length | **256 bits** each |
| PBKDF2 iterations (WPA2) | **4096** |
| PBKDF2 iterations (WPA3-SAE) | **5 – 8** (counter-mode resistant) |
| 4-way handshake | Generates **PTK + GTK** |
| 802.1X port-control | Controlled by **RADIUS** Accept/Reject |
| RADIUS port | **UDP 1812 / 1813** |
| Default WAP admin HTTP | **80 / 443** |
| 802.11w | **Management Frame Protection (MFP)** |
| 802.11i | **WPA2** standard (ratified 2004) |
| FMS attack | Breaks **WEP** |
| KRACK (2017) | Breaks **802.11r / 4-way handshake** |
| Dragonfly | **PAKE used in WPA3-SAE** |
| Evil Twin | Rogue AP with same **SSID** |
| War Driving | Scanning WAPs from a **moving vehicle** |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Enterprise Campuses:** WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit + EAP-TLS for sensitive R&D.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** WPA2/WPA3 with PMF, MAC filtering as defense-in-depth.
- **Industrial IoT:** WPA3-Enterprise + 802.1X with device certificates.
- **SOHO:** WPA3-Personal with strong ≥ 20-character passphrase; disable WPS.
- **Hospitality / Guest:** Open SSID with **captive portal**, segmented VLAN, client isolation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: WEP IV Collision Probability (Birthday Bound)

The WEP IV is **24 bits** long. Two different frames sharing the same IV + RC4 keystream allow XOR keystream recovery. We derive the expected number of frames for a collision using the **Birthday Paradox**.

Let $N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ possible IVs. For $n$ captured frames, the probability that **at least two** share an IV is:

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-n(n-1) / 2N}$$

### Step-by-Step Evaluation for $n = 5000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n(n-1)}{2N} &= \frac{5000 \times 4999}{2 \times 16{,}777{,}216} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{24{,}995{,}000}{33{,}554{,}432} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.7449
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{collision}} &\approx 1 - e^{-0.7449} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1 - 0.4748 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.5252
\end{aligned}
$$

So with just **5000 frames** (~5 minutes of busy traffic), there is a **52.5%** probability of an IV reuse. For $n = 9000$ the probability crosses **95%**. This is why WEP is broken in seconds.

## 3.2 Derivation: PMK Derivation (WPA2-Personal)

Given a passphrase $P$ and an SSID $S$, the PMK is derived via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-1:

$$PMK = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{HMAC-SHA-1}, P, S, \text{SSID length}, 4096, 256)$$

The function returns a 256-bit key. In expanded form (using HMAC as $H$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= H(P, S \Vert \text{0x00 0x00 0x00 0x01}) \\
U_2 &= H(P, U_1) \\
U_3 &= H(P, U_2) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
U_{4096} &= H(P, U_{4095}) \\
PMK &= U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \dots \oplus U_{4096}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering intuition:** The 4096 iterations and HMAC structure make each password guess take ~1 ms on modern hardware, but **offline cracking** can still achieve millions of guesses per second on GPUs if the passphrase is weak (e.g., dictionary word). This is why **passphrase length × entropy** matters more than protocol choice.

## 3.3 Derivation: PTK Generation (4-Way Handshake)

After PMK is established, the PTK is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PTK} &= \text{PRF-384}\big(\text{PMK}, \\
&\quad \text{"Pairwise key expansion"} \Vert \\
&\quad \min(\text{MAC}_A, \text{MAC}_B) \Vert \max(\text{MAC}_A, \text{MAC}_B) \Vert \\
&\quad \min(\text{ANonce}, \text{SNonce}) \Vert \max(\text{ANonce}, \text{SNonce})\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

The 384-bit output is split:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PTK} = \underbrace{KCK}_{128 \text{ bits}} \Vert \underbrace{KEK}_{128 \text{ bits}} \Vert \underbrace{TK}_{128 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Sub-Key | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **KCK** (Key Confirmation Key) | Computes MIC on EAPOL frames |
| **KEK** (Key Encryption Key) | Encrypts the GTK in message 3 |
| **TK** (Temporal Key) | Used by CCMP to encrypt data frames |

## 3.4 Derivation: SAE Password Element (WPA3)

For a password $p$ and curve $E$ with generator $P$ of prime order $q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= H(\text{"SAE Password"} \Vert p \Vert \text{ID}_A \Vert \text{ID}_B \Vert \text{MAC}_A \Vert \text{MAC}_B) \pmod q \\
\text{seed} &= \text{PMK} \pmod q \\
k &= \text{BN\_KDF}(\text{PMK}, \text{"SAE KCK and PMK"}) \\
\text{commit} &= (r \cdot P, \, -m \cdot P) \quad \text{where} \quad r, m \text{ are random scalars masked with seed}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why this is strong:** Each guess of the password requires a full **scalar multiplication on P-256**, and a peer must complete the exchange — there is no offline shortcut. The number of guesses per second drops from **millions (WPA2)** to **tens (WPA3-SAE)** even with GPU acceleration.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attacker Capability vs Defense

| Attack | Required Proximity | Cryptographic Defense | Administrative Defense |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passive Sniffing | Within RF range (~100 m) | **WPA2-CCMP / WPA3-GCMP** encryption | Reduce TX power, Faraday shielding |
| IV Reuse (WEP) | Same | **WPA2+** (no IV reuse) | Disable WEP entirely |
| 4-Way Handshake Capture | Within RF range | **WPA3-SAE** (forward secrecy) | 802.11w PMF, strong passphrase |
| Evil Twin | Same | **802.11w MFP**, certificate pinning | WIDS (Wireless IDS) — Kismet, Aruba RFProtect |
| MAC Spoofing | Same | **802.1X EAP-TLS** (MAC not trusted) | Disable MAC filtering as sole control |
| Deauth Flood | Same | **802.11w (PMF)** | Rate-limit management frames |
| Jamming | Within RF range | **None** (RF layer) | 802.11n channel hopping, RRM, spectrum sensors |
| KRACK | Within RF range | Patch AP firmware | Disable 802.11r unless required |
| Rogue AP | Same | **WPA2-Enterprise 802.1X** | WIPS, NAC, AP-aware switches |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Q: A WPA2-PSK network uses SSID `KTU-Lab` and passphrase `Cyber@2024`. Compute the length of the PMK and PTK in bits, and identify the sub-keys.**

### Solution:

**Step 1 – PMK derivation:**

$$
PMK = \text{PBKDF2}(HMAC\text{-}SHA\text{-}1, \text{"Cyber@2024"}, \text{"KTU-Lab"}, 4096, 256)
$$

Length of PMK = **256 bits**.

**Step 2 – PTK derivation:**

$$
PTK = PRF\text{-}384(PMK, \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce} \Vert MAC_A \Vert MAC_B)
$$

Length of PTK = **384 bits** (3 × 128).

**Step 3 – Sub-key allocation:**

| Sub-Key | Length | Use |
|---|---|---|
| KCK | 128 bits | MIC over EAPOL |
| KEK | 128 bits | Encrypt GTK |
| TK | 128 bits | CCMP data encryption |

**Step 4 – Total secret material delivered to the client:**

$$
\text{Total} = PMK (256) + PTK (384) + GTK (128) = \mathbf{768 \text{ bits}}
$$

> **Engineering take-away:** Even the strongest cipher is only as strong as its key. A 20-character random passphrase has ~120 bits of entropy — beyond brute-force reach of all current computing.

## 3.7 Decision Logic for Choosing Security Mode (Exam Heuristic)

```
Is the deployment enterprise (100+ users, regulated data)?
├── YES → WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit + EAP-TLS + 802.1X
└── NO  → Is it consumer / SOHO / home?
         ├── YES → WPA3-Personal (SAE) with passphrase ≥ 20 chars
         └── NO  → Legacy device on network?
                  ├── YES → WPA2-Personal as fallback (VLAN-isolated)
                  └── NO  → Refuse the legacy device
```

    end

    subgraph apsec["WIRELESS ACCESS POINT (Authenticator)"]
        rfLayer["L1 RF Layer Antenna, TX Power, Channel Selection"]
        linkLayer["L2 Link Layer 802.11 MAC CCMP-AES / GCMP-AES"]
        mfp["802.11w Management Frame Protection"]
        apCtrl["Authenticator Port-based Access Control 802.1X"]
    end

    end

    devUser -->|1 Probe Request| rfLayer
    rfLayer -->|2 Beacon SSID| devUser
    supplicant -->|3 EAPOL Start| apCtrl

    mfp -.protects.-> rfLayer

```

## 4.2 WPA3-SAE Handshake Sequence (Dragonfly)

This Mermaid sequence diagram shows the **Simultaneous Authentication of Equals** flow that replaced the vulnerable 4-way PSK handshake in WPA3.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client (Supplicant)
    participant AP as Access Point
    participant R as RADIUS / AS

    C->>AP: Auth Commit (scalar, element)
    AP->>C: Auth Commit (scalar, element)
    Note over C,AP: Both compute shared secret via scalar multiplication
    C->>AP: Auth Confirm (scalar, send_confirm)
    AP->>C: Auth Confirm (scalar, send_confirm)
    Note over C,AP: Both derive PMK from shared secret
    C->>AP: 4-Way Handshake (using new PMK)
    AP->>C: GTK + MIC
    C-->>AP: Ack
    Note over C,AP: Session secured with forward secrecy
    AP->>R: Accounting Start (optional)
```
